title: Programmable control of bacterial operons with a single Cas13 RNA effector
summary: A Nature Biotechnology perspective piece by Tong et al. introduces the SONAR system, which uses a single engineered Cas13d effector to enable RNA-level control of bacterial operons through crRNA design alone. The atnCas13d-IF3 fusion can switch between transcript degradation, translation inhibition, and translation activation by adjusting only the guide RNA sequence—an advance over approaches requiring separate effectors for each modality. This consolidation is significant because polar effects in polycistronic operons have long limited combinatorial metabolic-engineering efforts; achieving multiple regulatory outputs from one effector simplifies design substantially. The work builds on a growing literature of high-fidelity Cas13 variants, dCas13-based repression schemes, and CRISPR-Cas13d eukaryotic studies now brought together in a unified bacterial platform.

title: Attenuated Cas13d variants enable tunable, multiplexed RNA regulation in Escherichia coli
summary: Researchers at Shanghai Jiao Tong University developed attenuated Cas13d (atnCas13d) variants through rational protein engineering targeting truncation of flexible regions, thereby reducing the inherent cytotoxicity and collateral cleavage that have hindered widespread use of Cas13-based RNA effectors in bacteria. The engineered system demonstrated a 2.2-fold improvement in growth optical density compared with wild-type Cas13d while maintaining effective transcript knockdown. By introducing proximal mismatches at the 5’ end of CRISPR RNA spacers, the team achieved functional switching between three modes: translation inhibition, polycistronic mRNA degradation, and IF3-fusion-based translation-level CRISPR activation (RNAa). The system was validated with programmable, orthogonal, multiplexed regulation of individual genes within polycistronic mRNAs and synthetic circuits. Application to lycopene biosynthesis optimization in E. coli demonstrated robust pathway rewiring and improved yields alongside fine-tuned modulation of essential and competing metabolic pathways.
analysis_notes: No named biotech company, drug, or deal present. The authors declare no competing interests; the work is primarily academic (State Key Laboratory of Microbial Metabolism). Priority set to high because Cas13-based RNA regulation with reduced toxicity represents a significant methodological advance for microbial synthetic biology and metabolic engineering pipelines used by many bioprocessing companies.

the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in

and JavaScript.

### Subjects

- [Genetic engineering](https://www.nature.com/subjects/genetic-engineering)
- [Synthetic biology](https://www.nature.com/subjects/synthetic-biology)

## Abstract

Cas13-based RNA effectors may enable dynamic, multiplexed and reversible gene regulation in bacteria. Yet, their widespread adoption is hindered by inherent cytotoxicity and collateral cleavage. Here we present a rational protein engineering strategy to generate attenuated Cas13d variants with tunable RNase activity through targeted truncation of flexible regions. This permits effective transcript knockdown while greatly reducing toxicity as reflected by a 2.2-fold higher growth optical density. By introducing proximal mismatches at the 5′ end of CRISPR RNA spacers, our system allows functional switching between translation inhibition, polycistronic mRNA degradation and IF3-fusion-based translation-level CRISPR activation. We demonstrate programmable, orthogonal and multiplexed regulation of individual genes within polycistronic mRNAs and synthetic circuits. Application to lycopene biosynthesis optimization shows robust pathway rewiring and improved yields alongside fine-tuned modulation of essential and competing pathways in _Escherichia_ _coli_. Our work provides a versatile RNA-regulatory toolkit for next-generation microbial synthetic biology and RNA-based biotechnology.

title: Self-organization from multicellularity to morphogenesis: principles and applications in regenerative strategies
summary: This Perspective piece, authored by researchers at Zernicka-Goetz Lab including Maithem Al-Hariri and Kraser from Cambridge, traces cellular self-organization from evolutionary origins through biomechanical inevitability to regenerative medicine applications. The paper argues that multicellular coordination emerged as an evolutionary necessity driven by physical constraints like oxygen and nutrient transport, which generate collective behaviors such as cavitation, folding, and branching. These behaviors couple mechanics, signaling pathways, and gene regulation to build tissues with spatiotemporal precision through iterative layering of simple rules. Stem cell-derived models of embryogenesis are making these principles experimentally tractable, revealing both canonical developmental routes and alternative trajectories that expose bottlenecks and failure modes. The authors argue that decoding these morphogenic rules will enable rational engineering of living tissue architecture, though the piece remains theoretical rather than reporting specific experimental results.
analysis_notes: Content is an abstract for a perspective/theoretical review paper, not a full article. No commercially actionable entities such as companies, drugs, trials, or deals are mentioned. The paper surveys academic research areas (stem cell models, embryo models in vitro, self-organization principles) rather than reporting specific experimental results or commercial developments. Priority set at medium because the subject area is foundational for stem-cell biotech and regenerative medicine sectors but carries no immediate investment signal.

### Subjects

- [Stem-cell biotechnology](https://www.nature.com/subjects/stem-cell-biotechnology)
- [Stem cells](https://www.nature.com/subjects/stem-cells)

## Abstract

Cellular self-organization reflects an evolutionary leap in which multicellular coordination became essential. Driven by fundamental constraints like oxygen and nutrient transport, physical laws generate inevitable collective behaviors such as cavitation, folding and branching. These behaviors couple mechanics, signaling and gene regulation to build tissues and organs with spatiotemporal precision through the iterative layering of simple rules. Stem cell-based models of embryogenesis and organogenesis make these principles experimentally tractable, revealing canonical developmental routes and alternative trajectories and failure modes that expose bottlenecks and constraints. In this Perspective, we trace self-organization from evolutionary origins to biophysical inevitability and discuss how emerging tools in stem cell biology and bioengineering are beginning to translate these insights into regenerative strategies. Decoding the rules of morphogenesis will open possibilities to reimagine, simulate and rationally engineer the architecture of living tissues.

title: Structural motif search across the protein universe with Folddisco
summary: Researchers at Seoul National University (Hyunbin Kim, Rachel Seongeun Kim, Milot Mirdita, Jaewon Yoon, Martin Steinegger) have developed Folddisco, a computational tool that dramatically accelerates the detection of similar protein structural motifs within large protein structure databases. The tool uses an index of position-independent geometric features—including side-chain orientation—combined with a rarity-based (IDF-style) scoring system. Benchmarks show Folddisco is 20-fold faster in query speed and fourfold more storage-efficient than existing methods, while improving accuracy across the board. Published in June 2026 in Nature Biotechnology, this work follows the group's prior Foldseek publication (van Kempen et al., Nat. Biotechnol. 2024) and extends the MMseqs/Foldseek ecosystem for structural bioinformatics. Folddisco is freely available as GPLv3 open-source software via https://folddisco.foldseek.com/ along with a webserver, precomputed databases, and benchmark data on Zenodo.
analysis_notes: Full article text behind paywall; summary derived from abstract, extended data figure captions, and reference section. The authors are affiliated with Seoul National University and Sungkyunkwan University, Republic of Korea. No commercial biotech deal or drug development implications detected—this is a methodological tool publication for the structural bioinformatics community.

Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles
and JavaScript.

## Abstract

Detecting similar protein structural motifs in large structure collections is computationally expensive. We developed Folddisco, a fast structural motif search tool that uses an index of position-independent geometric features, including side-chain orientation, combined with a rarity-based scoring system. Folddisco is 20-fold faster in querying and fourfold more storage-efficient than existing methods while improving accuracy. Folddisco is freely available online ( [https://folddisco.foldseek.com](https://folddisco.foldseek.com/)), along with a webserver ( [https://search.foldseek.com/folddisco](https://search.foldseek.com/folddisco)).

## Data availability

The benchmark data are available from Zenodo ( [https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18443780](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18443780))[45](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9#ref-CR45 "Kim, H., Kim, R. S., Mirdita, M., & Steinegger, M. Data for structural motif search across the protein-universe with Folddisco. Zenodo                  https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18443780                                 (2026)."). [Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9#Sec46) are provided with this paper.

## Code availability

Folddisco is GPLv3-licensed free open-source software. The source code and ready-to-use binaries, as well as precomputed databases, can be obtained online ( [https://folddisco.foldseek.com](https://folddisco.foldseek.com/)). The scripts used for the benchmarks and plotting are available from GitHub ( [https://github.com/steineggerlab/folddisco-analysis](https://github.com/steineggerlab/folddisco-analysis)). The webserver code is available from GitHub ( [https://github.com/soedinglab/mmseqs2-app](https://github.com/soedinglab/mmseqs2-app)).

## Extended data

### [Extended Data Fig. 1 Performance comparison of Folddisco to pyScoMotif and RCSB.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9/figures/3)

Precision, recall, F1-score and search time (in seconds) with 12 threads were evaluated for **top** serine peptidase motif query and **bottom** zinc-finger motif with 3 residues. Folddisco achieved the best performance for the serine peptidase query. While pyScoMotif and RCSB exhibited low sensitivity on the 4-residue zinc-finger query, they performed well with 3 residues, where Folddisco showed comparable performance (ranking second). Consistently, Folddisco was over 10 times faster than pyScoMotif across these settings.

[Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9#MOESM6)

### [Extended Data Fig. 2 Sensitivity of Folddisco using two scoring modes and of pyScoMotif on SCOPe-constructed benchmarks.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9/figures/4)

This figure extends Fig. [1f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9#Fig1), where the goal is to match SCOPe sequences of the same family as the query before matching a different fold, using all conserved columns (“full”) or a random subsample of them (60%, 20%). Folddisco’s matches were ranked either by its IDF-based pre-filter coverage score (pink) or its full pipeline’s RMSD (orange), which is also used for ranking by pyScoMotif (blue). Folddisco achieves higher area under the curve values than pyScoMotif in all cases (bottom panel). Comparing Folddisco’s two scoring modes, RMSD is more sensitive for ranking matches of short queries (20% benchmark), while the IDF-based coverage score yields higher area the under curve values for the longer 60% and 100% benchmarks (bottom panel).

[Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9#MOESM6)

### [Extended Data Fig. 3 Sensitivity of Folddisco using two modes and of pyScoMotif on M-CSA-constructed benchmarks.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9/figures/5)

This figure extends Fig. [1g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9#Fig1). Here, we used a set of 713 queries from M-CSA reference catalytic sites, excluding motifs with only two residues to ensure comparability with pyScoMotif. The target database consisted of 62,122 PDB structures annotated as containing homologous catalytic sites. We measured sensitivity up to the first false positive (FP), defining a true positive as the retrieval of a PDB structure annotated as a homolog in M-CSA, with all remaining structures in the dataset serving as negatives. Folddisco (default), Folddisco (Sensitive) and pyScoMotif achieved AUCs of 0.432, 0.463 and 0.344, respectively.

[Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9#MOESM6)

### [Extended Data Fig. 4 Partial zinc finger motifs identified in ubiquitin-specific peptidases.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9/figures/6)

From H. sapiens proteome, Folddisco detected partial matches of the zinc finger motif ( **top**) in ubiquitin-specific peptidases USP42 and USP17L20 ( **middle**). Notably, the residue order is reversed in these matches. AlphaFold3 predictions confirmed zinc coordination within these motifs ( **bottom**).

### [Extended Data Fig. 5 Evaluation of double-motif search capabilities.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9/figures/7)

Proteins are often regulated by multiple distinct motifs, necessitating the simultaneous detection of functional sites. We assessed the capability to detect double motifs by querying 14 residues (10 allosteric, 4 active) in hemoglobin ( **top**). Folddisco successfully identified matches sharing the same Allosteric Database (ASD) ID. Query residues are colored in gray, while matched active and allosteric residues are shown in purple and pink, respectively. In contrast, a test on an ATP-binding cassette transporter revealed a limitation in detecting widely distributed motifs ( **bottom**). The algorithm failed to retrieve the distal allosteric sites (black) alongside the detected region (blue) because the spatial separation (>20 Å) exceeded the connectivity threshold.

### [Extended Data Fig. 6 Detection of single disulfide bonds and knottin motifs.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9/figures/8)

Folddisco identified single disulfide bonds (red) in insulin ( **top**) and complex knottin motifs formed by multiple disulfide bonds ( **bottom**). From search of a knottin motif in a spider toxin, a conotoxin was retrieved with a partial match.

### [Extended Data Fig. 7 Identification of short linear motifs by Folddisco.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03162-9/figures/9)

Folddisco searched and detected the known “GGRGG” motif ( **top**) and a arbitrarily generated “APPLE” motif ( **bottom**). The query “APPLE” motif structure (gray) was predicted using ColabFold.

title: Artificial intelligence in drug discovery
summary: Nature Biotechnology published a perspective on June 16, 2026 covering recent patents related to methods and systems for applying artificial intelligence in drug discovery. The article surveys intellectual property developments at the intersection of AI and pharmaceutical research, mapping the patent landscape that defines how computational approaches are being protected and commercialized. For biotech operators, this provides a window into which AI methodologies firms are treating as proprietary competitive assets. The piece signals continued maturation of AI-driven drug discovery from an experimental capability toward a heavily patented, commercially defended domain.
analysis_notes: Content is a thin preview/paywall-locked article. The only visible body text reads 'Recent patents relating to methods and systems for using artificial intelligence in drug discovery.' Full article text is unavailable without subscription. Summary and priority inferred from title, publication date, and descriptive snippet alone; actual patent details, specific companies, or quantitative findings cannot be confirmed.

title: Reply to 'Methodological concerns and a lack of evidence for reforming regulatory exclusivities for pharmaceuticals'
summary: Robin Feldman, Gideon Schor, and Ramy Alsaffar (University of California College of the Law, San Francisco) respond to a critical letter by K.M.L. Acri published in Nature Biotechnology that raised methodological concerns about their 2025 paper on the New Clinical Investigation (NCI) exclusivity provision under Hatch-Waxman. The authors argue Acri's rebuttal misunderstood both the study's goals and its conclusions, overstated their claims, and ignored the majority of the paper's results by falsely framing the work as resting on a single ancillary finding near the end. Their original 2025 research examines the outsized role and real-world impact of the NCI exclusivity compared to expectations from the Hatch-Waxman enactment history, including historical concerns raised by then-Representative Albert Gore Jr. that the exclusivity was a last-minute giveaway to a minority of industry members. The piece is significant for biotech operators navigating orphan drug exclusivity and pharmaceutical policy/regulatory strategy given its focus on one of the key regulatory exclusivity mechanisms in U.S. drug development.
analysis_notes: Paywalled preview-only article. Full text of the 2025 original paper (Feldman, Schor & Alsaffar, Nat Biotechnol 43, 857-862) and Acri's full rebuttal letter are not accessible from this page. This is a brief reply/letter response (~1-2 pages typical for Nature correspondence). The core subject matter (NCI exclusivity policy analysis) is relevant and specific but the debater-response format means no new scientific or commercial data is presented.

We appreciate that the letter writer[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03164-7#ref-CR1 "Acri, K. M. L. Nat. Biotechnol.                    https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03165-6                                     (2026).") took the time to read and examine our work[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03164-7#ref-CR2 "Feldman, R., Schor, G. & Alsaffar, R. Nat. Biotechnol. 43, 857–862 (2025).") in depth. However, her letter, which purports to raise “methodological concerns” regarding our work, appears to have misunderstood both the goals of the study and the conclusions reached. The letter also appears to have overstated our claims and placed them in different contexts, in some circumstances. Of greatest importance, the letter ignores most of the study’s results, suggesting instead that the paper rests on a single, ancillary finding near the end.

The goal of our work was to examine the outsized role of the New Clinical Investigation (NCI) exclusivity in comparison to the expectations suggested in the enactment history of the Hatch–Waxman provision authorizing the exclusivity. Thus, we spend considerable time at the outset examining the history of the provision, including then-Representative Albert Gore Jr.’s concern that the NCI exclusivity was a last-minute giveaway to a minority of industry members.

title: Methodological concerns and a lack of evidence for reforming regulatory exclusivities for pharmaceuticals
summary: Kristina Acri of Colorado College publishes a methodological critique in Nature Biotechnology of a prior paper by Feldman, Schor and Alsaffar (Nat. Biotechnol. 43, 857–862, 2025) on New Clinical Investigation (NCI) exclusivity in the pharmaceutical sector. The Feldman study analyzed drugs from the 2019 Medicare Part D and Medicaid formularies, identifying 236 qualifying drugs of which 176 carried post-patent exclusities—with NCI comprising 70.5% of exclusivities expiring after core patents. Acri contends the data do not substantiate the claim that NCI exclusivity is a major driver of rising drug prices or stalled generic competition, nor does it justify proposed reform mechanisms such as capping the number of regulatory exclusivities per company or drug.

In a recent issue, Feldman et al.[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03165-6#ref-CR1 "Feldman, R., Schor, G. & Alsaffar, R. Nat. Biotechnol. 43, 857–862 (2025).") examined the New Clinical Investigation (NCI) exclusivity by analyzing the non-patent exclusivities protecting a subset of drugs included in the 2019 Medicare Part D and 2019 Medicaid formularies. The study focuses on drugs that account for substantial federal drug spending: specifically, drugs with ten years of market time for which 2019 spending totaled at least $10 million. These conditions provide a baseline subset of 236 drugs, of which 176 drugs had exclusivities that expired after the drug’s core patent and any patent-term extension. A further subset of these 176 drugs account for 210 instances of four types of exclusivities: NCI (148), Pediatric (42), Orphan Drug (19) and New Chemical Entity (1). The authors calculate that the NCI exclusivity comprises 70.5% of exclusivities expiring after expiration of a drug’s core patent. This result is claimed to establish that NCI exclusivities are a substantial contributor to rising drug prices and stalled generic entry. Finally, the study notes that the potential for rampant use of NCI exclusivity provides a guide for reform, proposing a cap on the number of regulatory exclusivities that may be granted to a company or drug.

High drug prices are a serious issue and efforts to address them are laudable. However, the methods employed to advocate for systemic reform are concerning. The authors’ data fall short of establishing a significant problem or substantiating the claim that the pharmaceutical industry has shifted its energy away from innovation and toward preclusion of competition.

title: Experiment-guided AlphaFold3 resolves measurement-consistent protein ensembles
summary: Researchers have modified AlphaFold3 to generate experimentally grounded structural ensembles rather than single static structures, using guidance terms derived from NMR spectroscopy data (NOE distance restraints and order parameters), X-ray crystallographic electron density maps, and cryo-EM electrostatic potential maps injected during the model reverse-diffusion sampling. Tested on ubiquitin as an NMR benchmark, NOE-guided AlphaFold3 reduced pairwise distance constraint violations in 70 of 91 cases (approximately 77%) compared to unguided AlphaFold3 and outperformed deposited PDB ensembles. The method also recovers alternate conformations, fills missing electron density regions, and resolves amyloid fibril structures that AlphaFold3 entirely mispredicts at the dimerization interface. Joint guidance combining cryo-EM, NMR and dihedral angle constraints on a RIPK3 human amyloid fibril produces further improvements. The authors demonstrate computational efficiency (minutes per target on H100 GPUs versus days of traditional molecular dynamics) and compare against BioEmu, AlphaFlow, AFCluster and ProteinEBM energy-based models. Published in Nature Biotechnology using Protenix, ByteDance AML's open-source PyTorch reimplementation of AlphaFold3, the work reframes diffuse predictors as sequence-conditioned priors steerable by experimental evidence to yield compact conformational ensembles consistent with measurements across multiple modalities.

Experiment-guided AlphaFold3 resolves measurement-consistent protein ensembles


[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5.pdf)

[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5.pdf)

## Abstract

AlphaFold3 predicts highly accurate protein structures from sequence but tends to collapse to a single dominant conformation, even when the underlying structure is inherently heterogeneous. Moreover, its predictions are oblivious to experimental conditions that can alter local sequence conformation. In this work, we show that AlphaFold3 can be guided to match data obtained by nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, X-ray crystallography and cryogenic electron microscopy (cryo-EM) experiments and combinations thereof. Our approach can also incorporate data that explicitly report on dynamics, such as site-resolved order parameters. We demonstrate that this methodology generates compact structural ensembles whose ensemble-averaged observables agree with experiment, with fewer distance restraint violations than traditionally resolved NMR structures and with unmodeled alternate conformations uncovered in electron density. This methodology paves the way for experimentally aware predictive models that generate structural ensembles consistent with the measurements, potentially over multiple modalities, and that can be further refined toward thermodynamically grounded ensembles by incorporating energetics.

### Similar content being viewed by others

![](https://media.springernature.com/w215h120/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41589-024-01638-w/MediaObjects/41589_2024_1638_Figa_HTML.png)

### [The power and pitfalls of AlphaFold2 for structure prediction beyond rigid globular proteins](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41589-024-01638-w?fromPaywallRec=false)

Article21 June 2024

![](https://media.springernature.com/w215h120/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-022-20390-6/MediaObjects/41598_2022_20390_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Confrontation of AlphaFold models with experimental structures enlightens conformational dynamics supporting CYP102A1 functions](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-20390-6?fromPaywallRec=false)

ArticleOpen access25 September 2022

![](https://media.springernature.com/w215h120/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42003-025-08783-5/MediaObjects/42003_2025_8783_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Distance-AF improves predicted protein structure models by AlphaFold2 with user-specified distance constraints](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42003-025-08783-5?fromPaywallRec=false)

ArticleOpen access30 September 2025

## Main

Proteins are inherently dynamic entities, sampling variable conformational states in response to their surroundings and to fulfill their biological roles. Although experimental techniques including X-ray crystallography, NMR spectroscopy and cryo-EM observe ensemble averages of these macromolecular structures, protein structural models often report only the dominant conformation, overlooking the underlying conformational heterogeneity.

Building upon the fundamental discovery that amino acids in spatial proximity within a protein structure co-evolve[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR1 "Göbel, U., Sander, C., Schneider, R. & Valencia, A. Correlated mutations and residue contacts in proteins. Proteins 18, 309–317 (1994)."), [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR2 "Hopf, T. A. et al. Sequence co-evolution gives 3D contacts and structures of protein complexes. eLife 3, e03430 (2014)."), and training on the hundreds of thousands of experimentally determined X-ray crystal structures available today, deep-learning-based models such as AlphaFold[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR3 "Jumper, J. et al. Highly accurate protein structure prediction with AlphaFold. Nature 596, 583–589 (2021)."), [4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR4 "Abramson, J. et al. Accurate structure prediction of biomolecular interactions with AlphaFold 3. Nature 630, 493–500 (2024).") have enabled protein structure prediction approaching experimental accuracy. However, AlphaFold’s training objective—to predict a single ‘most probable’ structure—biases its output toward static snapshots, effectively marginalizing conformational heterogeneity encoded in its training data. This emphasizes the need for new models that can explicitly generate protein structural ensembles that are consistent with experimental measurements.

To bridge this gap, we present a framework for building experiment-grounded protein structure generative models that infer conformational ensembles consistent with measured experimental data. The key idea is to treat state-of-the-art protein structure predictors (for example, AlphaFold3) as sequence-conditioned structural priors and cast ensemble modeling as posterior inference of protein structures given experimental measurements. Recent studies have explored ways to incorporate experimental restraints into structure prediction. AlphaLink[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR5 "Stahl, K., Graziadei, A., Dau, T., Brock, O. & Rappsilber, J. Protein structure prediction with in-cell photo-crosslinking mass spectrometry and deep learning. Nat. Biotechnol. 41, 1810–1819 (2023).") integrates cross-linking mass spectrometry-derived distances as bias terms in AlphaFold’s pair representation to guide single-structure inference. In the present study, we instead modify the generative process of AlphaFold3 itself: experimental likelihoods enter as guidance terms during sampling, enabling direct conditioning on any experimental modality to generate ensembles consistent with both sequence and experiment.

## Results

We adapt AlphaFold3 to generate ensembles by modifying the reverse diffusion steps such that it includes a gradient-based guidance term derived from the experimental likelihood. This term introduces a data-dependent force that steers sampling toward structures compatible with measurements. A scaling hyperparameter controls the influence of this guidance, allowing interpolation between purely prior-driven and strongly data-conditioned outputs. Our framework supports multiple experimental modalities by defining appropriate differentiable likelihood functions. After sampling, we perform energy minimization to correct geometric distortions and ensure chemical plausibility—a procedure that is used in many generative models, including AlphaFold itself. We then apply ensemble selection to identify the minimal subset of structures that best explains the experimental observations. See Fig. [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig1) for a schematic description of the proposed method and Sections 8.4 (‘Protein structure inverse problems’), 8.5 (‘X-ray crystallography models’), 8.6 (‘NMR models’) and 8.7 (‘Cryo-EM models’) for algorithmic and implementation details.

**Fig. 1: Schematic depiction of the proposed method.**

![Fig. 1: Schematic depiction of the proposed method.](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41587-026-03166-5/MediaObjects/41587_2026_3166_Fig1_HTML.png)

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5/figures/1)

AlphaFold3 allows the sampling of protein structures given an amino acid sequence. To further condition the model by experimental observations, at each timestep of the AlphaFold3 diffusion model, an ensemble of structures is generated. Hydrogens are differentiably added to the heavy atoms (addh), and likelihoods of experimental observations are calculated given each individual ensemble member (for example, to enforce locations of certain atoms) and on ensemble averages (for example, calculated electron density, _F_ c, average interatomic distances or order parameters). The gradient of the combined log-likelihood terms is used as the guidance term. At the final diffusion step, the generated ensemble is refined by force field relaxation and pruned to select a minimal subset maximizing the likelihood terms. ED, electron density.

### Guiding AlphaFold3 with NMR distance restraints and order parameters efficiently generates experiment-consistent ensembles

NMR structure determination is largely based on the detection of interatomic proximities, which is a manifestation of the dipolar coupling between spins and detected in nuclear Overhauser effect (NOE) experiments in solution-state NMR[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR6 "Shcherbakov, A. A., Medeiros-Silva, J., Tran, N., Gelenter, M. D. & Hong, M. From angstroms to nanometers: measuring interatomic distances by solid-state NMR. Chem. Rev. 122, 9848–9879 (2021)."). Typically, the structure is then determined by applying these distance restraints in molecular dynamics simulations to obtain the set of lowest energy conformations[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR7 "Schwieters, C. D., Kuszewski, J. J. & Clore, G. M. Using XPLOR–NIH for NMR molecular structure determination. Prog. Nucl. Magn. Reson. Spectrosc. 48, 47–62 (2006)."), [8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR8 "Güntert, P. Automated NMR structure calculation with CYANA. Methods Mol. Biol. 338, 353–378 (2004)."). However, simulating conformers individually often leads to mode collapse, producing rigid ensembles that poorly capture true conformational dynamics. Also, oftentimes, no single mode’s pairwise Euclidean distances can fully explain the observed distance averages over multiple incongruent conformations. The explicit determination of multiple state-structural ensembles is becoming more accessible[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR9 "Vögeli, B., Olsson, S., Güntert, P. & Riek, R. The exact NOE as an alternative in ensemble structure determination. Biophys. J. 110, 113–126 (2016)."), [10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR10 "Chi, C. N. et al. A structural ensemble for the enzyme cyclophilin reveals an orchestrated mode of action at atomic resolution. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. Engl. 54, 11657–11661 (2015)."), but it often requires highly accurate (‘exact’) NOE distances and is not yet common practice in NMR. Ensemble-based molecular dynamics approaches simulating multiple conformers simultaneously to satisfy experimental restraints remain computationally expensive, requiring days even for small systems like the 76-residue ubiquitin[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR11 "Lindorff-Larsen, K., Best, R. B., DePristo, M. A., Dobson, C. M. & Vendruscolo, M. Simultaneous determination of protein structure and dynamics. Nature 433, 128–132 (2005)."), [12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR12 "Lange, O. F. et al. Recognition dynamics up to microseconds revealed from an RDC-derived ubiquitin ensemble in solution. Science 320, 1471–1475 (2008).").

We use ubiquitin (Protein Bata Bank (PDB) [1D3Z](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/1D3Z); Biological Magnetic Resonance Bank (BMRB) [6457](http://bmrb.io/data_library/summary/?bmrbId=6457) and [5387](http://bmrb.io/data_library/summary/?bmrbId=5387)) as an NMR structure determination benchmark. Guiding AlphaFold3 with NOE distance restraints generates ensembles with substantially fewer pairwise distance constraint violations. Figure [2a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig2) shows the cumulative distribution of violation magnitudes; a spatial visualization of the violated constraints is depicted in Fig. [2c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig2). From Fig. [2b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig2), it is evident that the ensemble produced by NOE-guided AlphaFold3 is more heterogeneous. Supplementary Figs. [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) and [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) evaluate the improvement of constraint satisfaction with the increase of the relative weight of the NOE guidance term. The runtime of the proposed method is several GPU minutes, overcoming the computational bottleneck of traditional ensemble methods.

As an independent verification of ensemble fit to actual protein dynamics, we cross-validated our results using experimental spin relaxation measurements of the backbone amide bond order parameter ( _S_ 2) on timescales shorter than a few nanoseconds[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR13 "Palmer III, A. G. NMR characterization of the dynamics of biomacromolecules. Chem. Rev. 104, 3623–3640 (2004)."). We computed ensemble order parameters and compared them to experimental measurements reported in ref. [14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR14 "Lienin, S., Bremi, T., Brutscher, B., Brüschweiler, R. & Ernst, R. Anisotropic intramolecular backbone dynamics of ubiquitin characterized by NMR relaxation and MD computer simulation. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 120, 9870–9879 (1998)."). As illustrated in Fig. [2d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig2), the deposited ensemble derived from standard NMR protocols (PDB [1D3Z](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/1D3Z)) as well as the unguided AlphaFold3 prediction are dominated by rigid conformations. Additional quantitative analysis in Supplementary Fig. [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) shows that these ensembles exhibit only moderate agreement with the experimental _S_ 2. By contrast, NOE-guided AlphaFold3 substantially improves agreement, better replicating the dynamic behavior observed in both flexible and structured regions.

To better match the N-H order parameter experimental data, we added an _S_ 2 guidance term to the NOE-guided AlphaFold3. This resulted in a substantially higher agreement with the experimental _S_ 2 measurements and similar to the result of computationally expensive NMR-guided ensemble molecular dynamics simulations (PDB [1XQQ](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/1XQQ) from ref. [11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR11 "Lindorff-Larsen, K., Best, R. B., DePristo, M. A., Dobson, C. M. & Vendruscolo, M. Simultaneous determination of protein structure and dynamics. Nature 433, 128–132 (2005).")) at a small fraction of the computational cost (Fig. [2d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig2) and Supplementary Fig. [3a−c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1)). For the case of ubiquitin, numerous additional experimental data have been collected, including methyl axis order parameters from spin relaxation (reporting on ps−ns motions[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR15 "Liao, X., Long, D., Li, D.-W., Brüschweiler, R. & Tugarinov, V. Probing side-chain dynamics in proteins by the measurement of nine deuterium relaxation rates per methyl group. J. Phys. Chem. B 116, 606–620 (2012).")) and order parameters from residual dipolar couplings (RDCs) for amide and methyl sites, which report on ps−ms motions[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR12 "Lange, O. F. et al. Recognition dynamics up to microseconds revealed from an RDC-derived ubiquitin ensemble in solution. Science 320, 1471–1475 (2008)."), [16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR16 "Markwick, P. R. et al. Toward a unified representation of protein structural dynamics in solution. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 131, 16968–16975 (2009)."), [17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR17 "Sabo, T. M. et al. ORIUM: optimized RDC-based iterative and unified model-free analysis. J. Biomol. NMR 58, 287–301 (2014)."). Supplementary Figs. [3d−f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) and [4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) show the result of guidance with these data. In all cases, guidance with NOE and _S_ 2 terms shows high agreement with the experiment at no or little expense of pairwise distance constraint violation.

We further evaluated our method on two benchmarks: eight peptides shown to be mispredicted by AlphaFold3 (refs. [18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR18 "Vedula, S., Bronstein, A. M. & Marx, A. Improving prediction accuracy in chimeric proteins with windowed multiple sequence alignment. Comput. Struct. Biotechnol. J. 27, 3292−3298 (2025)."), [19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR19 "McDonald, E. F., Jones, T., Plate, L., Meiler, J. & Gulsevin, A. Benchmarking AlphaFold2 on peptide structure prediction. Structure 31, 111–119 (2023).")) and a subset of 83 proteins from the recently compiled 100-protein NMR spectra database[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR20 "Klukowski, P. et al. The 100-protein NMR spectra dataset: a resource for biomolecular NMR data analysis. Sci. Data 11, 30 (2024)."). Supplementary Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) details quantitative performance indicators for individual structures; Fig. [3a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig3) presents summary statistics. We observe that, compared to the ensembles deposited into the PDB, NOE-guided AlphaFold3 improves distance constraint satisfaction in 70 out of 91 cases (about 77%), compared to unguided AlphaFold3, which outperforms the PDB ensembles in only 15 cases (17%). NOE-guided AlphaFold3 outperforms its unguided counterpart in all cases. Additional comparison to inductive ensemble generative models, including AlphaFlow and ESMFlow[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR21 "Jing, B., Berger, B. & Jaakkola, T. AlphaFold meets flow matching for generating protein ensembles. ICMLʼ24: Proceedings of the 41st International Conference on Machine Learning (eds Salakhutdinov, R. et al.) 22277−22303 (JMLR.org, 2024)."), BioEmu[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR22 "Lewis, S. et al. Scalable emulation of protein equilibrium ensembles with generative deep learning. Science 389, eadv9817 (2025).") and AFCluster[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR23 "Wayment-Steele, H. K. et al. Predicting multiple conformations via sequence clustering and AlphaFold2. Nature 625, 832−839 (2024)."), is presented in Supplementary Table [6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1).

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5/figures/3)

**a**, Distribution of percent of violated distance constraints (top left) and median violation (bottom left) for PDB, unguided and NOE-guided AlphaFold3 and the relative improvement of these metrics compared to the deposited PDB structures. **b**, Distribution of _F_ o and _F_ c cosine similarity (top left) and R-factors (top right, split between _R_ free and _R_ work), with the relative improvement shown on the right. Additional summary statistics are shown in Supplementary Figs. [10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) and [11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1).

In all the reported cases, we used uniformly weighed ensembles. Supplementary Fig. [11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) presents an additional evaluation when a force field predicted energy is used to weigh the different conformations either as a postprocessing step or by fully integrating the force field into the guidance term and producing ensembles that combine experimental guidance with force field energetic information, thereby moving the sampled distribution closer to a thermodynamically meaningful one. The latter weighing technique improves the distance constraint satisfaction in 78 cases (about 87%) with the median improvement of about 20%. For methodological details, see Section 8.13 (‘Energy-reweighted guidance’).

Figure [4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig4) depicts examples of ensembles generated by NOE-guided AlphaFold3. We observe that AlphaFold3 tends to overpredict order (for example, longer helical structures in [2B3W](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/2B3W), [2JVD](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/2JVD) and [6SOW](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/6SOW)). In these cases, NOE guidance increases the ensemble heterogeneity, considerably improving constraint satisfaction and outperforming the corresponding PDB ensembles. In [2K52](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/2K52), no single conformer of the ensemble satisfies the distance constraints, whereas the ensemble average guidance term improves constraint satisfaction, also compared to the PDB ensemble. In three other cases, AlphaFold3 completely mispredicts parts of the structure ( [2LI3](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/2LI3), [2B3W](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/2B3W) and [1DEC](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/1DEC)), resulting in many distance constraint violations. NOE guidance reduces the number of violations.

Finally, as an independent source of validation, we used the Accuracy of NMR Structures Using RCI and Rigidity (ANSURR) metrics introduced in ref. [24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR24 "Fowler, N. J., Sljoka, A. & Williamson, M. P. A method for validating the accuracy of NMR protein structures. Nat. Commun. 11, 6321 (2020).") that compare the rigidity profile of the model to that inferred from NMR chemical shifts. We observe that ensembles generated by NOE-guided AlphaFold3 improve the root mean square deviation (RMSD) score in 92% of the cases and the correlation score in 54% of the cases when compared to the corresponding PDB ensembles. See Supplementary Fig. [5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) for more information.

### Guiding AlphaFold3 with electron density captures previously unmodeled residues in X-ray crystallography data

AlphaFold3 is oblivious to environmental influences that may be present in experimental settings, such as interactions with ions, ligands and other macromolecular partners or different crystal contacts, resulting in conformational variations. For example, HSP90α crystallizes as a dimer in the asymmetric unit cell with only chain A, and not chain B, captured in the ligand-bound state (PDB [6CYH](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/6CYH)). The loop adjacent to this ligand adopts a different conformation between the two chains, neither of which is accurately predicted by AlphaFold3. Figure [5a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig5) shows that the poor AlphaFold3 prediction is restored to experimental accuracy by electron density guidance. The method is similarly successful when applied across experiments. Supplementary Table [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) shows that a pair of sequence-identical constructs for the Orf9b protein from SARS-CoV-2 exhibit varying conformations at several protein chain locations (PDB [9N55](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/9N55) and [9MZB](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/9MZB)). Electron density guidance successfully restores the AlphaFold3 model generation to match the PDB-deposited structures. The success of fitting on electron density maps having very different resolutions (PDB [9N55](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/9N55) at 1.65 Å and PDB [9MZB](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/9MZB) at 2.8 Å) indicates the generalizability of our method. In another example, we consider a pair of myoglobin structures that differ in surface-exposed loop conformations due to altered crystal packing (PDB [1U7R](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/1U7R) and PDB [1U7S](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/1U7S)). Electron density-guided AlphaFold3 reproduces both conformations with density fits similar to deposited models. It has been noted that these alternate conformations closely resemble those observed in solution by NMR (PDB [1MYF](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/1MYF)), suggesting that the lattice selects from intrinsically adopted conformations[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR25 "Kondrashov, D. A., Zhang, W., Aranda IV, R., Stec, B. & Phillips Jr, G. N. Sampling of the native conformational ensemble of myoglobin via structures in different crystalline environments. Proteins 70, 353–362 (2008).") and underscoring the biological relevance of training AlphaFold3 on ensembles detectable in crystal density, now an achievable task with our methodology.

**Fig. 5: X-ray electron density-guided AlphaFold produces structures and ensembles of equivalent or better accuracy to those deposited in the PDB and detects plausible conformations in unmodeled electron density.**

![Fig. 5: X-ray electron density-guided AlphaFold produces structures and ensembles of equivalent or better accuracy to those deposited in the PDB and detects plausible conformations in unmodeled electron density.](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41587-026-03166-5/MediaObjects/41587_2026_3166_Fig5_HTML.png)

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5/figures/5)

**a**, [6CYH](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/6CYH) chain B is poorly predicted by AlphaFold3 in the region of amino acids 101−116. Guidance using the electron density maps restores the fit of the model. **b**, An unmodeled region in [5SUJ](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/5SUJ) has different conformations at two locations due to the presence of ligands in one chain. **c**, The structure of an 18-amino-acid peptide from PAK2 bound to the SH3 domain of _β_ −PIX is poorly predicted by AlphaFold3, but this prediction can be guided by the electron density to recapture the experimental structure. **d**, AlphaFold3 poorly predicts multiple regions of [6S7N](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/6S7N). Electron density guidance essentially reproduces the PDB structure. **e**, **f**, Electron density-guided AlphaFold3 produces conformational ensembles explaining the multimodal electron density on par with or better than the PDB model. **g**, Electron density-guided AlphaFold3 predicts unmodeled altlocs in [5NVJ](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/5NVJ), better explaining the electron density. Electron densities are visualized as 0.5 e− Å−3 isosurfaces. Numbers below indicate cosine similarities between calculated and observed densities. ED, electron density; med., median; Rel., relative.

Another region-specific phenomenon in X-ray crystal structures is unmodeled regions. Local flexibility results in a smeared electron density map (measured by the B-factor), to the point where the density is too blurred to readily infer structure. We show in Fig. [5b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig5) that density-guided AlphaFold3 has the potential to fill in missing regions to best explain the scarce density. The crystal structure of the protein LPG2148 from _Legionella pneumophila_ (PDB [5SUJ](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/5SUJ)) crystallizes as a dimer in the asymmetric unit cell, and both chains have a missing segment in the model at the same surface-exposed loop region. Figure [5b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig5) and Supplementary Fig. [7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) show that the density-guided version fills the missing space in both chains with better local real-space correlation to the sparse density than the AlpaFold3 prediction or the commonly used PDBFixer[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR26 "Eastman, P. et al. OpenMM 7: rapid development of high performance algorithms for molecular dynamics. PLoS Comput. Biol. 13, e1005659 (2017).").

Another challenge in crystallography is modeling peptides. These short protein chains are often very poorly predicted by AlphaFold3 because they are highly adaptable to their binding partners. Figure [5c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig5) shows that our density-guided method restores the fit of the peptide to the electron density from a very poor AlphaFold3 prediction. This example leverages AlphaFold3 as a simple structural prior, because these ensembles were generated without multiple sequence alignment (MSA) conditioning. Additional examples in Supplementary Table [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) and the summary statistics in Fig. [3b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig3) highlight the method’s ability to generalize across sequences of varying lengths. Comparison to ensemble generative models is presented in Supplementary Table [7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1). Going beyond segment-based guidance, we automatically guide an entire protein chain through an iterative process of detection and improvement of regions poorly fit to the electron density and phase refinement. The final fitted structure shown in Fig. [5d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig5) results from iterative improvements in real-space correlation (Supplementary Fig. [8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1)). We hypothesize that the results appear slightly inferior to those deposited in the PDB mainly due to the manual placement of ions and structural waters that AlphaFold does not generate.

Although early crystallographic refinements prioritized single-conformer models, often protein crystal structures can also capture ensembles as two or more distinct conformations within the same electron density map[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR27 "Smith, J. L., Hendrickson, W. A., Honzatko, R. B. & Sheriff, S. Structural heterogeneity in protein crystals. Biochemistry 25, 5018–5027 (1986)."), [28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR28 "Furnham, N., Blundell, T. L., DePristo, M. A. & Terwilliger, T. C. Is one solution good enough? Nat. Struct. Mol. Biol. 13, 184–185 (2006)."), [29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR29 "Van Den Bedem, H. & Fraser, J. S. Integrative, dynamic structural biology at atomic resolution—itʼs about time. Nat. Methods 12, 307–318 (2015)."), [30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR30 "Wankowicz, S. A. et al. Automated multiconformer model building for X-ray crystallography and cryo-EM. eLife 12, RP90606 (2024)."). A recent study[31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR31 "Rosenberg, A. A., Marx, A. & Bronstein, A. M. A dataset of alternately located segments in protein crystal structures. Sci. Data 11, 783 (2024).") compiled a comprehensive catalog of alternate locations (altlocs) from PDB structures and also showed that, even for regions with well-separated and stable altlocs, structural ensemble predictors such as AlphaFold3 fail to reproduce the experimentally determined distributions or capture the bimodal nature of backbone conformations[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR32 "Rosenberg, A. A., Vedula, S., Bronstein, A. M. & Marx, A. Seeing double: molecular dynamics simulations reveal the stability of certain alternate protein conformations in crystal structures. Preprint at bioRxiv                  https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.08.31.610605                                 (2024)."). Here, we demonstrate that electron density guidance on this same set of altlocs generates an ensemble that captures the backbone’s bimodal nature, providing a better explanation of the density. This method is effective for different degrees of backbone separation spanning from 1 Å (Fig. [5e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig5)) to tens of angstroms (Fig. [5f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig5)). Supplementary Table [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) further demonstrates that alternate conformations are successfully detected across different sequence types and lengths and within electron density maps having different resolutions and qualities. Moreover, Supplementary Table [4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) shows that these conformations are consistently reproduced across independent runs using different types of density maps, with the consistency further improving as batch size increases (Supplementary Fig. [9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1)). Finally, we demonstrate the potential of this method for detecting previously unmodeled altloc conformations (Fig. [5g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig5)).

### Guiding AlphaFold3 with cryo-EM maps

Cryo-EM imaging allows to capture the conformations of intricate protein complexes in the form of electrostatic potential (ESP) maps. Because the standard reconstruction pipelines rely on rigid-body consensus sets, individual ESP maps do not capture the true thermodynamic heterogeneity of the particle. At the same time, the achievable resolution, especially in flexible regions, is often insufficient for precise placement of individual atoms. We demonstrate on several examples that AlphaFold3 guidance with ESP maps achieves better ab initio modeling accuracy.

Figure [6a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig6) shows the reconstruction of insulin receptor IR-B in its symmetric apo state (PDB [8U4B](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/8U4B); Electron Microscopy Data Bank (EMDB) [41877](http://www.ebi.ac.uk/emdb/EMD-41877)) and asymmetric conformation (PDB [8U4E](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/8U4E); EMDB [41880](http://www.ebi.ac.uk/emdb/EMD-41880)) with three bound IGF2 factors. AlphaFold3 tends to oversymmetrize the predicted homodimers, mispredicting large portions of the asymmetric conformation but also inaccurately predicting the symmetric parts. ESP map guidance produces conformations that agree with the observed densities.

**Fig. 6: Multimeric structure reconstruction using cryo-EM ESP map guidance.**

![Fig. 6: Multimeric structure reconstruction using cryo-EM ESP map guidance.](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41587-026-03166-5/MediaObjects/41587_2026_3166_Fig6_HTML.png)

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5/figures/6)

**a**, Reconstruction of insulin receptor IR-B in its symmetric apo state (left) and asymmetric conformation (right) with three bound IGF2 factors (yellow). **b**, Reconstruction of an amyloid-β fibril. AlphaFold3 (red) and ESP-guided (green) structures are shown alongside the deposited PDB models. Density cross-correlation values are indicated below each structure. **c**, Reconstruction of a human RIPK3 amyloid fibril using unguided AlphaFold3 (red), ESP-guided AlphaFold3 (green) and ESP-guided AlphaFold3 with the addition of NMR chemical shift-inferred backbone dihedral angles _ϕ_ and _ψ_ (cyan). The addition of the dihedrals further improves agreement with both experimental observations. Cross-correlations, NOE constraint violations and dihedral errors are reported below each structure. cross-corr., cross-correlation.

Figure [6b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig6) shows the reconstruction of an amyloid-β fibril (PDB [9FH1](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/9FH1); EMDB [50436](http://www.ebi.ac.uk/emdb/EMD-50436)) formed by a nearly planar homodimer that further oligomerizes in the perpendicular direction forming a very long fiber structure. AlphaFold3 completely mispredicts the dimerization interface and the structure of the dimeric unit. ESP guidance produces accurate agreement with the density.

### Guiding AlphaFold3 jointly with cryo-EM and NMR data

In many cases, structural biology approaches comprise data from several techniques. Our approach can integrate all the above sources of data and more. We exemplify such a case here for the combination of cryo-EM and NMR data, applied to amyloid fibrils. Figure [6c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#Fig6) shows the reconstruction of a RIPK3 human amyloid fibril (PDB [7DA4](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/7DA4); EMDB [30622](http://www.ebi.ac.uk/emdb/EMD-30622)) for which solid-state NMR chemical shift-derived dihedral angles are also available (PDB [7DAC](http://www.rcsb.org/structure/7DAC); BMRB [36392](http://bmrb.io/data_library/summary/index.php?bmrbId=36392)). In this case, AlphaFold3 predicts the global structure of the multimer relatively well within the resolution of the ESP map, and ESP-only guidance improves the density fit quality while producing a relatively poor fit of the backbone dihedrals and NOE constraints. Guiding jointly by ESP, NOE and dihedral angles improve the local structure accuracy at a reasonable tradeoff of density fit (see Supplementary Table [5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1) for quantitative analysis).

## Discussion

This work reframes AlphaFold3 as a powerful sequence-conditioned prior that can be steered by experimental evidence to yield small, testable conformational ensembles rather than a single consensus model. These ensembles should be interpreted as posterior structural hypotheses constrained by the supplied measurements and the model prior, not as fully calibrated Boltzmann equilibrium ensembles. In this sense, the method addresses a practically important inference problem in structural biology: generating compact sets of conformations whose ensemble-averaged observables explain heterogeneous experimental data. In practice, composable likelihoods from heterogeneous modalities—interatomic distances, dihedral angle order parameters from NMR, electron density from X-ray crystallography and ESP maps from cryo-EM—are injected as gradients during AlphaFold3’s reverse-diffusion trajectory and followed by relaxation and sparsifying ensemble selection. The method’s ability to sample from the joint distribution of all atoms unlocks a richer picture of the protein’s conformational landscape that is partially lost in the traditional PDB representation of its structure as a mixture of Gaussian marginals of individual atoms (for X-ray data) or ensembles of conformers from NMR, which, as we show here, do not reflect dynamic amplitudes faithfully.

Currently, the field lacks human-intelligible visualization and machine-interpretable representations of the joint probability distributions that govern proteins and complexes. An intriguing question is whether nature reuses a restricted vocabulary of local distributional motifs, analogous to secondary structure, but defined over distributions of backbone/side chain states and whether there exist non-local distributional motifs of correlated motion and allostery, analogous to structural domains. The degree to which such motifs are imprinted in evolutionary signals (for example, MSA features) will determine how far inductive models can progress from predicting static structure to predicting relative populations of states. The comparison to existing inductive ensemble generators such as BioEmu and AlphaFlow suggests that, in the absence of experimental evidence, these models largely fail to produce ensembles in agreement with the experiment.

Methodologically, three features are central in this study. First, ensembles are jointly guided during diffusion, which preserves diversity where the data admit multiple explanations while avoiding trivial replication of a single conformation; subsequent relaxation ensures chemical plausibility; and a matching pursuit-like pruning step yields a compact set that still explains the observations. Second, the framework naturally supports ‘local-to-global’ strategies, targeting problematic segments (for example, loops) and then iterating to the whole chain, mirroring how crystallographers and NMR spectroscopists often work. Third, the computational footprint is small (minutes per target in typical cases, which is likely to be further optimizable), making the method practical as an interactive assistant within existing pipelines rather than a replacement for refinement or simulation. For the same reason, the tool enables large-scale reanalysis of legacy datasets (‘PDB-Redo’) across single modalities and their combinations.

Several caveats warrant emphasis. The reported ensembles are explanatory with respect to the provided data and the AlphaFold3 prior; they are not calibrated thermodynamic populations. Agreement with a finite set of observables does not uniquely determine the underlying conformational distribution, and distinct ensembles may explain the same data comparably well. Accordingly, mixture weights should not be overinterpreted as equilibrium populations. Notably, the improvement observed when force field energy is incorporated indicates that energetic information contributes complementary constraints and can move the inferred ensembles closer to thermodynamically meaningful ones. A key direction for future work is, therefore, to integrate energetic priors and thermodynamic observables more tightly, for example, through maximum-entropy or evidence-based population estimation.

Guidance strength and gradient handling are important guardrails to avert models being pulled into noise, especially in low-resolution electron microscopy or poorly phased crystallographic maps. Although, in practice, ensemble pruning and cross-validation techniques mitigate overfitting, a more principled statistically robust procedure is needed to disentangle true thermodynamic entropy from epistemic uncertainty. Finally, the present implementation focuses primarily on proteins; treatment of ligands, metals, post-translational modifications, structural waters and complex symmetries remains limited when the only evidence is a density-like signal. A more faithful modeling of ligands will also allow disentangling between native thermodynamic and compositional conformational heterogeneity.

These limitations notwithstanding, the posterior sampling view opens practical routes to improve mainstream structural workflows. In crystallography, experiment-guided AlphaFold3 can propose altlocs and occupancies during model building, repair loops with weak electron density and accelerate convergence by offering a small set of candidate conformers that already satisfy the map. In NMR, it enables rapid, ensemble-aware interpretation of nuclear Overhauser effect spectroscopy (NOESY) spectra and relaxation data without days of molecular dynamics and can suggest mutations or ligands that stabilize minor predicted states for validation. In cryo-EM, it provides a principled way to inject atomic detail into flexible regions and to couple map-based guidance with dihedral or secondary structure information where local resolution is limited. We anticipate that experiment-guided generative models will make ensemble-centric modeling routine in crystallography, NMR and cryo-EM, with near-term impact on ligand discovery, variant interpretation and the design of experiments that stabilize or reveal otherwise cryptic states.

## Methods

### Models, resources and packages

For all experiments, we used Protenix[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR33 "ByteDance AML AI4Science Team et al. Protenix - advancing structure prediction through a comprehensive AlphaFold3 reproduction. Preprint at bioRxiv                  https://doi.org/10.1101/2025.01.08.631967                                 (2025)."), an open-source PyTorch reimplementation of AlphaFold3. For the AlphaFold3 baseline mentioned across experiments, we used the official AlphaFold3 weights and source code[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR4 "Abramson, J. et al. Accurate structure prediction of biomolecular interactions with AlphaFold 3. Nature 630, 493–500 (2024).").

To implement the corresponding forward models, we used PyTorch (2.3.1 + cu121)[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR34 "Ansel, J. PyTorch 2: faster machine learning through dynamic Python bytecode transformation and graph compilation. 29th ACM International Conference on Architectural Support for Programming Languages and Operating Systems. Vol. 2 (2024)."). However, to efficiently calculate the density for larger structures, we used PyKeOps[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR35 "Charlier, B., Feydy, J., Glaunès, J. A., Collin, F.-D. & Durif, G. Kernel operations on the GPU, with Autodiff, without memory overflows. J. Mach. Learn. Res. 22, 1–6 (2021).") and PyTorch. All computations were performed on NVIDIA H100 and L40 GPUs running on Debian GNU/Linux 12 distribution.

Across all experiments, we retrieved the MSAs using a wrapper around the ColabFold MMseqs2 API[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR36 "Mirdita, M. et al. ColabFold: making protein folding accessible to all. Nat. Methods 19, 679–682 (2022)."). The wrapper submits the query sequences to the remote MMseqs2 server via HTTP POST, polls for job completion and downloads and extracts the results. The output is provided in A3M format, which is compatible with AlphaFold3.

### Input data

#### X-ray crystallography

### Atomic models

In the X-ray crystallography workflow, the input consists of a PDB ID, a chain identifier and an amino acid subsequence in the chain. For this modality, we restrict our modeling to single protein chains. The corresponding PDB structure and MTZ file are retrieved from PDB-Redo[37](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR37 "Joosten, R. P., Long, F., Murshudov, G. N. & Perrakis, A. The PDB_REDO server for macromolecular structure model optimization. IUCrJ 1, 213–220 (2014)."), and the specific chain is identified using Gemmi (version 0.6.5)[38](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR38 "Wojdyr, M. et al. Gemmi - library for structural biology. GitHub.                  https://github.com/project-gemmi/gemmi                                 (2021)."). From this chain, we extract only the standard amino acid residues explicitly modeled in the PDB file, while excluding structural waters, hydrogen atoms and all other non-standard amino acids, and save the resulting coordinates as a separate PDB file. In addition, we mutate selenium methionine to methionine by replacing selenium atoms with sulfur. We also mutate S-hydroxycysteine to cysteine by removing the oxygen from the hydroxyl group. If altlocs are present[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR32 "Rosenberg, A. A., Vedula, S., Bronstein, A. M. & Marx, A. Seeing double: molecular dynamics simulations reveal the stability of certain alternate protein conformations in crystal structures. Preprint at bioRxiv                  https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.08.31.610605                                 (2024)."), the PDB is split into the corresponding altloc files. Because AlphaFold3 uses 1-based residue indexing, we renumber the residues in each split PDB to start from 1. In many cases, the retrieved PDB files can contain incomplete or unmodeled residues due to poor quality density. To address this, we use PDBFixer[39](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR39 "Eastman, P. et al. PDBFixer. GitHub.                  https://github.com/openmm/pdbfixer                                 (2013).") (version 1.9.0), which employs standard residue templates from OpenMM[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR26 "Eastman, P. et al. OpenMM 7: rapid development of high performance algorithms for molecular dynamics. PLoS Comput. Biol. 13, e1005659 (2017).") force fields, to model the missing atoms in all split PDBs. To avoid steric clashes after imputing the atoms with PDBFixer, we relax the structure using AMBER14 force field[40](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR40 "Wang, J., Wolf, R. M., Caldwell, J. W., Kollman, P. A. & Case, D. A. Development and testing of a general amber force field. J. Comput. Chem. 25, 1157–1174 (2004).") relaxation. The isotropic B-factor for these imputed atoms is set to 100.00. For AlphaFold3, the input sequence is limited to the residues explicitly modeled in the reference chain.

To construct the atom mask for applying the substructure conditioner, we use the provided amino acid subsequence to identify residues that will primarily be optimized with the density-based loss versus those optimized using the substructure conditioner.

### Electron density maps

Our proposed method generalizes across different types of real-space electron density maps. Here, we considered three map types.

- 2 _F_ o − _F_ cmaps: These maps are rendered using the PHENIX[41](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR41 "Adams, P. D. et al. PHENIX: a comprehensive Python-based system for macromolecular structure solution. Acta Crystallogr. D Biol. Crystallogr. 66, 213–221 (2010).")fft command:



$$\\begin{array}{rcl} & & {\\mathtt{fft}}\\,{\\mathtt{hklin}}\\,{\\mathtt{mtz}}\\\_{\\mathtt{path}}\\,{\\mathtt{mapout}}\\,{\\mathtt{map}}\\\_{\\mathtt{output}}\\\_{\\mathtt{path}}\\\ & & {\\mathtt{LABIN}}\\,{\\mathtt{F1}}{\\mathtt{=\\; FWT}}\\,{\\mathtt{PHI}}{\\mathtt{=\\; PHWT}}\\,{\\mathtt{SIG1}}{\\mathtt{=\\; SIGFP}}\\,{\\mathtt{VF000}}\\,{\\mathtt{volume}}\\,{\\mathtt{f000}}\\end{array}$$



The unit cell volume (volume) and the total scattering factor at zero scattering angle _F_(0, 0, 0) (f000) are derived from the input atomic model. For a triclinic cell with edge lengths _a_, _b_, _c_ (in Å) and angles _α_, _β_, _γ_ (in radians), the unit cell volume is:



$${\\mathtt{volume}}=a\\cdot b\\cdot c\\cdot \\sqrt{(1-{\\cos }^{2}\\alpha -{\\cos }^{2}\\beta -{\\cos }^{2}\\gamma +2\\cos \\alpha \\cos \\beta \\cos \\gamma )}$$



Unit cell parameters ( _a_, _b_, _c_, _α_, _β_, _γ_) are parsed from the input atomic model. _F_(0, 0, 0) is obtained using phenix.f000, which estimates the zero-angle scattering factor by summing electron counts from both the atomic model and the bulk solvent. The bulk solvent contribution is calculated using the default average solvent density of 0.35 e− Å−3. The atomic and solvent electron contributions are then combined to yield _F_(0, 0, 0).

- Absolute-scale END maps: These density maps are generated using the END map workflow of ref. [42](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR42 "Lang, P. T., Holton, J. M., Fraser, J. S. & Alber, T. Protein structural ensembles are revealed by redefining X-ray electron density noise. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 111, 237–242 (2014)."), which combines PHENIX and CCP4 programs to produce absolute-scale electron density maps (e− Å−3 units). We run



$$\\begin{array}{rcl} & & {\\mathtt{phenix.refine}}\\,{\\mathtt{atomic}}\\\_{\\mathtt{model}}\\\_{\\mathtt{file}}\\,{\\mathtt{mtz}}\\\_{\\mathtt{file}}\\,{\\mathtt{strategy}}{\\mathtt{=\\; rigid}}\\\_{\\mathtt{body}}\\\ & & {\\mathtt{./END}}\\\_{\\mathtt{RAPID.com}}\\,{\\mathtt{pdb}}\\\_{\\mathtt{refine}}\\\_{\\mathtt{001.eff}}\\,{\\mathtt{-norapid}}\\end{array}$$



The atomic model first undergoes rigid-body refinement to prevent artifacts from misaligned models. The END script then rescales the observed structure factors against the calculated ones and offsets the density by adding _F_(0, 0, 0) to every voxel. This places the map on an absolute scale, where zero corresponds to vacuum.

- Composite omit maps: These density maps are computed in PHENIX using



$${\\mathtt{phenix.composite}}\\\_{\\mathtt{omit}}\\\_{\\mathtt{map}}\\,{\\mathtt{atomic}}\\\_{\\mathtt{model.pdb}}\\,{\\mathtt{reflection}}\\\_{\\mathtt{data.mtz}}$$



These maps reduce model bias while minimizing phase degradation.


To align the real-space density maps with the corresponding atomic models from Section 8.2.1 (‘X-ray crystallography’), we used phenix.map\_box with selection\_radius = 10 to carve the map around the corresponding atomic model with sufficient padding.

Because our X-ray density guidance operates locally, we carved the input density map to include voxels within a radius of 5 Å of all atoms in the residue range (and chain) of interest from the reference PDBs. This preprocessing reduces VRAM memory consumption. During _F_ c computation, the ensemble density is calculated at voxels within this 5-Å local neighborhood.

#### NMR

We used three sources of NMR observables for guidance: NOE-implied distance restraints, chemical-shift-implied backbone dihedral angles and per-residue order parameters.

### NOE-implied distance restraints

NOE restraint lists were parsed from NMR-STAR files (via pyNMR-STAR), selecting only NOE-derived distance restraints; other restraint types such as dihedral angles, hydrogen bonds and RDC were excluded. ‘Ambiguous NOEs’—cross-peaks whose atom pair assignment is uncertain—were retained and represented as disjunctive (OR) constraints: each ambiguity group defines multiple candidate atom pairs sharing the same bounds, and the restraint is satisfied if any one candidate meets those bounds (not necessarily all). Lower/upper bounds were read directly from the file; missing lower bounds were set to 0.0 Å. We note that, although NOE physics is intensity based, we adopt the common semiquantitative distance-bound representation here, as is standard practice in molecular dynamics-resolved NMR ensemble workflows; a future extension of our pipeline may incorporate intensity averaging within ambiguity groups prior to distance conversion. For each system, the corresponding NMR-STAR restraint file was retrieved from the PDB entry on the RCSB website.

### Chemical-shift-implied dihedral angles

Backbone _Φ_ tgt/ _Ψ_ tgt angles used in combined cryo-EM and NMR guidance were inferred from raw chemical shifts using TALOS-N[43](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR43 "Shen, Y. & Bax, A. Protein backbone and sidechain torsion angles predicted from NMR chemical shifts using artificial neural networks. J. Biomol. NMR 56, 227–241 (2013)."), a tool that infers potential ranges of backbone dihedral angles from chemical shifts. Per target, the chemical shift lists were retrieved from the BMRB entry cross-referenced to the corresponding PDB ID.

### Order parameters

Order parameter ( _S_ 2) values were taken on a per-residue basis from the respective publications (or associated [supplementary datasets](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#MOESM1)) for each target. Across all observables, when required values or bounds were unavailable for a residue or atom pair, we masked that item and did not guide on it.

#### Cryo-EM

In the cryo-EM workflow, the inputs include a reference atomic model, amino acid sequences and the corresponding ESP map from the EMDB[44](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR44 "Turner, J. et al. EMDB—the Electron Microscopy Data Bank. Nucleic Acids Res. 52, D456–D465 (2024).").

### Atomic models

In cryo-EM, the objective is not to refine high-resolution reference structures, as in X-ray crystallography, but to fit amino acid sequences into experimentally resolved volumes. This distinction is because of the lower resolution of cryo-EM ESP maps, which makes them useful for studying larger protein structures. Here, reference PDB structures from RCSB[45](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR45 "Rose, P. W. et al. The RCSB Protein Data Bank: redesigned web site and web services. Nucleic Acids Res. 39, D392–D401 (2010).") are primarily used to align chain labels, construct atom masks and serialize outputs. Unlike crystallographic preprocessing pipelines, missing residues or atoms are not imputed with modeling software (such as PDBFixer), and alternate conformations are not split into separate models. To ensure consistency with AlphaFold3, all residues are renumbered to be 1-indexed. Finally, because cryo-EM often targets large complexes, multimeric inputs along with multiple sequences can be provided. Relevant residue ranges and chain identifiers are specified alongside the sequences before guidance.

### ESP maps

The input ESP volumes are obtained from the EMDB[44](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03166-5#ref-CR44 "Turner, J. et al. EMDB—the Electron Microscopy Data Bank.
