title: Hybrid solid-liquid optics enable scalable, high-resolution light-sheet microscopy across diverse immersion media
summary: Researchers introduced HySIL (hybrid solid-liquid optics), a refractive design framework that pairs a solid optical element with refractive-index-matched liquid to form a continuous optical system for wavefront correction and numerical aperture enhancement. Implemented as the modular SCOPE device and its higher-performance Super-SCOPE variant, the technology enables submicron-resolution (<0.75 mu m lateral) imaging using inexpensive long-working-distance air objectives (34 mm working distance). Demonstrated across cleared and expanded mouse and salamander brains, iDISCO-cleared cavefish brains, iPSC-derived brain organoids co-cultured with microglia, and intact human breast tissue for 3D histopathology. HySIL achieves effective NA scaling as n for SCOPE and n^2 for Super-SCOPE, works across a broad RI range (1.38-1.56) without hardware modification, and integrates into both open-source pLSM and commercial SLICE platforms at significantly reduced cost compared to high-NA immersion objectives.

Hybrid solid−liquid optics enable scalable, high-resolution light-sheet microscopy across diverse immersion media


[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7.pdf)

[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7.pdf)

## Abstract

Many data-driven approaches rely on scalable and affordable three-dimensional (3D) imaging across subcellular to organ scales. Although advances in tissue clearing, expansion microscopy and light-sheet microscopy (LSM) have enabled high-resolution imaging of intact specimens, scalability in sample size, throughput and accessibility remains fundamentally limited by detection optics. Here we introduce hybrid solid−liquid optics (HySIL), a flexible refractive design framework in which a solid optical element and a refractive index (RI)-matched liquid function as a continuous optical system for wavefront correction and numerical aperture enhancement. We implement this framework as SCOPE and Super-SCOPE, enabling submicron-resolution, aberration-corrected LSM using long-working-distance air objectives. We demonstrate high-resolution volumetric imaging across diverse biological contexts, including cleared and expanded mouse, salamander and cavefish brains, human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived brain organoids and large intact human tissues for 3D histopathology. By combining enhanced optical performance with low-cost, long-working-distance and multi-immersion compatibility, HySIL provides an accessible and scalable foundation for next-generation volumetric imaging and data-driven biological discovery.

![](https://media.springernature.com/w215h120/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41587-025-02882-8/MediaObjects/41587_2025_2882_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Isotropic, aberration-corrected light sheet microscopy for rapid high-resolution imaging of cleared tissue](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-025-02882-8?fromPaywallRec=false)

ArticleOpen access13 November 2025

![](https://media.springernature.com/w215h120/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41551-024-01249-9/MediaObjects/41551_2024_1249_Fig1_HTML.png)

### [Low-cost and scalable projected light-sheet microscopy for the high-resolution imaging of cleared tissue and living samples](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41551-024-01249-9?fromPaywallRec=false)

Article29 August 2024

## Main

Breakthroughs such as AlphaFold and ESM3 for protein structure prediction[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR1 "Jumper, J. et al. Highly accurate protein structure prediction with AlphaFold. Nature 596, 583–589 (2021)."), [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR2 "Hayes, T. et al. Simulating 500 million years of evolution with a language model. Science 387, 850–858 (2025)."), advances in computational histopathology[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR3 "van der Laak, J., Litjens, G. & Ciompi, F. Deep learning in histopathology: the path to the clinic. Nat. Med. 27, 775–784 (2021).") and generative modeling of virtual cells[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR4 "Bunne, C. et al. How to build the virtual cell with artificial intelligence: priorities and opportunities. Cell 187, 7045–7063 (2024).") exemplify the transformative power of data-driven approaches in life sciences. Extending this paradigm from molecular to multicellular systems requires imaging technologies capable of routinely generating subcellular-resolution volumetric datasets from intact tissues and organs. Recent developments in tissue clearing and expansion microscopy (ExM)[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR5 "Chen, F., Tillberg, P. W. & Boyden, E. S. Expansion microscopy. Science 347, 543–548 (2015)."), [6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR6 "Wassie, A. T., Zhao, Y. & Boyden, E. S. Expansion microscopy: principles and uses in biological research. Nat. Methods 16, 33–41 (2019).") have provided unprecedented optical access to intact specimens. In parallel, light-sheet microscopy (LSM) has emerged as the leading modality for high-speed volumetric imaging, offering minimal phototoxicity and exceptional optical efficiency[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR7 "Stelzer, E. H. K. et al. Light sheet fluorescence microscopy. Nat. Rev. Methods Primers 1, 73 (2021)."). Open-source initiatives such as OpenSPIM[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR8 "Pitrone, P. G. et al. OpenSPIM: an open-access light-sheet microscopy platform. Nat. Methods 10, 598–599 (2013).") and mesoSPIM[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR9 "Voigt, F. F. et al. The mesoSPIM initiative: open-source light-sheet microscopes for imaging cleared tissue. Nat. Methods 16, 1105–1108 (2019)."), [10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR10 "Vladimirov, N. et al. Benchtop mesoSPIM: a next-generation open-source light-sheet microscope for cleared samples. Nat. Commun. 15, 2679 (2024).") have broadened access to LSM, and our open-source projected light-sheet microscopy (pLSM) platform[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR11 "Chen, Y. et al. Low-cost and scalable projected light-sheet microscopy for the high-resolution imaging of cleared tissue and living samples. Nat. Biomed. Eng. 8, 1109–1123 (2024).") has further reduced optical and mechanical footprints and material costs by orders of magnitude through repurposing consumer-grade components.

However, achieving scalability in imaging volume, sample throughput and widespread adoption while maintaining subcellular resolution remains constrained by fundamental limitations in detection optics. Immersion objectives enable aberration-free, high numerical aperture (NA) detection necessary for achieving submicron resolution but also impose severe constraints: short working distances (WDs) limiting imaging depth, high costs, narrow refractive index (RI) correction ranges limiting multi-immersion compatibility and operational complexity requiring sealing within imaging chambers. Air objectives offer long WDs, cost reductions of several orders of magnitude and mechanical simplicity for integration into low-cost, portable platforms. However, they suffer from severe spherical aberrations, reduced photon collection and degraded resolution when imaging immersed samples, limiting performance to cellular resolution. Existing correction approaches, including adaptive optics[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR12 "Turcotte, R., Liang, Y. & Ji, N. Adaptive optical versus spherical aberration corrections for in vivo brain imaging. Biomed. Opt. Express 8, 3891–3902 (2017)."), custom-fabricated meniscus lenses such as SIMlens[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR13 "Barner, L. A., Glaser, A. K., True, L. D., Reder, N. P. & Liu, J. T. C. Solid immersion meniscus lens (SIMlens) for open-top light-sheet microscopy. Opt. Lett. 44, 4451–4454 (2019)."), [14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR14 "Barner, L. A., Glaser, A. K., Huang, H., True, L. D. & Liu, J. T. C. Multi-resolution open-top light-sheet microscopy to enable efficient 3D pathology workflows. Biomed. Opt. Express 11, 6605 (2020).") and specialized corrected air objectives[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR15 "Glaser, A. et al. Expansion-assisted selective plane illumination microscopy for nanoscale imaging of centimeter-scale tissues. eLife 12, RP91979 (2025)."), mitigate some of these effects but remain system specific, require custom engineering or are expensive. Consequently, current LSM systems often sacrifice imaging performance for portability, performing adequately for cellular-resolution applications but leaving efficient subcellular-resolution imaging of large volumes inaccessible. This limitation has become particularly pressing with the advent of ExM, which can increase specimen dimensions by an order of magnitude, creating urgent demand for aberration-free imaging over extended WDs.

To address these challenges, we developed hybrid solid−liquid optics (HySIL), a refractive design framework in which a solid optical element is paired with an RI-matched liquid to form a continuous hybrid optical system with engineered wavefront correction properties. Building upon the principles of solid immersion lenses (SILs)[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR16 "Mansfield, S. M. & Kino, G. S. Solid immersion microscope. Appl. Phys. Lett. 57, 2615–2616 (1990)."), [17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR17 "Wu, Q. ian, Ghislain, L. P. & Elings, V. B. Imaging with solid immersion lenses, spatial resolution, and applications. Proc. IEEE 88, 1491–1498 (2000)."), [18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR18 "Barnes, W. L. et al. Solid-state single photon sources: light collection strategies. Eur. Phys. J. D 18, 197–210 (2002)."), which enhance resolution through RI tailoring at the specimen−objective interface, HySIL extends these concepts to a hybrid solid−liquid configuration that decouples the optical element from both the sample and the objective. Although classical SIL implementations typically require direct optical contact and are, therefore, limited to surface-proximal imaging[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR19 "Wang, L. et al. Solid immersion microscopy images cells under cryogenic conditions with 12 nm resolution. Commun. Biol. 2, 74 (2019)."), related curved interface approaches, including custom-designed meniscus systems such as SIMlens[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR13 "Barner, L. A., Glaser, A. K., True, L. D., Reder, N. P. & Liu, J. T. C. Solid immersion meniscus lens (SIMlens) for open-top light-sheet microscopy. Opt. Lett. 44, 4451–4454 (2019)."), [14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR14 "Barner, L. A., Glaser, A. K., Huang, H., True, L. D. & Liu, J. T. C. Multi-resolution open-top light-sheet microscopy to enable efficient 3D pathology workflows. Biomed. Opt. Express 11, 6605 (2020)."), [20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR20 "Glaser, A. K. et al. A hybrid open-top light-sheet microscope for versatile multi-scale imaging of cleared tissues. Nat. Methods 19, 613–619 (2022).") and recent implementations using off-the-shelf meniscus lenses[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR21 "Aakhte, M. et al. Isotropic, aberration-corrected light sheet microscopy for rapid high-resolution imaging of cleared tissue. Nat. Biotechnol.                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-025-02882-8                                 (2025)."), have demonstrated that air objectives can be effectively coupled to immersed samples with reduced spherical aberration.

HySIL builds upon these approaches by integrating the solid optical element and immersion medium into an RI-matched configuration that functions as a continuous optical interface. A key distinction from meniscus-based approaches lies in the treatment of internal refractive interfaces. In these approaches, the meniscus element acts as a wavefront-matching window with two refractive boundaries, requiring precise RI matching across the sample, holder and immersion medium. By contrast, HySIL eliminates internal refractive discontinuities by matching the solid element to the immersion medium, forming a continuous optical system with a single functional interface. This architecture reduces sensitivity to RI mismatch, relaxes optical constraints and enables flexible implementation across multiple lens geometries without reoptimization. Consequently, HySIL enables improved aberration correction and effective NA enhancement across configurations and establishes a generalizable refractive framework for high-resolution volumetric imaging.

Building upon this framework, we developed SCOPE (Solid-liquid hybrid immersion-Corrected Optics for Phase Errors), an objective-agnostic modular imaging device, and its variant, Super-SCOPE, which incorporates Weierstrass super-hemispherical geometry[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR17 "Wu, Q. ian, Ghislain, L. P. & Elings, V. B. Imaging with solid immersion lenses, spatial resolution, and applications. Proc. IEEE 88, 1491–1498 (2000)."), [18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR18 "Barnes, W. L. et al. Solid-state single photon sources: light collection strategies. Eur. Phys. J. D 18, 197–210 (2002)."). These devices (1) increase the effective detection NA, scaling as _n_ for SCOPE and _n_ 2 for Super-SCOPE. This scaling arises from RI-mediated angular compression at the hybrid interface, which increases the range of collection angles accessible to the air objective, with additional amplification in the super-hemispherical (Weierstrass) geometry; (2) correct spherical aberrations; (3) maintain compatibility across diverse immersion media; and (4) preserve the key advantages of air objective detection, including long WD, chromatic alignment, low cost and ease of integration into existing imaging platforms.

We validate SCOPE through optical simulations and point spread function (PSF) measurements. These analyses demonstrated aberration-free, submicron-resolution (<0.75 µm) imaging using inexpensive air objectives with long WDs (approximately 34 mm) that maintain high performance across diverse tissue-clearing media. We then integrated SCOPE with our low-cost pLSM-based LSM platform to create a compact, cost-effective and scalable three-dimensional (3D) imaging system for submicron-resolution imaging of centimeter-scale samples. SCOPE enabled imaging of diverse specimens, including expanded mouse and salamander brains, CUBIC-R-cleared intact mouse brains, iDISCO-cleared cavefish brains, brain organoids with microglia and 3D histopathology of human breast tissue. In all cases, SCOPE produced high-contrast, color-corrected imaging with robust spherical aberration correction across tiled volumes spanning entire specimens. Altogether, SCOPE provides a simple, low-cost and scalable solution that enables air objectives to achieve high-resolution imaging of centimeter-scale specimens through tiled acquisition, bridging affordability and performance for next-generation, data-driven biological discovery.

## Results

### Design and theoretical validation of SCOPE and Super-SCOPE

Achieving high-resolution, large-volume imaging with inexpensive, long-working-distance air objectives requires overcoming spherical aberrations, resolution loss and reduced signal collection that arise when imaging through high-RI media typical of cleared tissues (Fig. [1a,b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1)). To address these challenges, we developed the HySIL framework (Fig. [1c,d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1)), in which a solid optical element is paired with a precisely RI-matched immersion liquid to form a continuous hybrid optical system with engineered wavefront correction properties. Similar to a classical hemispherical SIL (Fig. [1c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1)), HySIL precompensates phase errors introduced by high-RI specimens and increases effective detection NA (Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1)). However, unlike conventional solid contact SIL implementations, which are typically limited to surface-proximal imaging[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR19 "Wang, L. et al. Solid immersion microscopy images cells under cryogenic conditions with 12 nm resolution. Commun. Biol. 2, 74 (2019).") (Fig. [1c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1)), HySIL decouples the solid element from both the sample and the objective, enabling robust aberration-corrected imaging across tiled volumes spanning large, cleared specimens.

For the simulation, samples with thicknesses of 2 mm, 4 mm and 6 mm and RIs ranging from 1.38 to 1.54 are represented as planar slabs adjacent to the sample plane. RMS wavefront error is plotted as a function of sample RI and thickness. **h**, Computer-aided design (CAD) model (SolidWorks) demonstrates modular integration of the SCOPE imaging device with the compact, low-cost pLSM platform. Imm., immersion; norm., normalized; obj., objective; Cm, camera.

To translate the HySIL framework into practical platforms for LSM, we developed SCOPE and Super-SCOPE modular imaging devices (Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1)), which can be integrated into a wide range of LSM implementations. SCOPE employs an off-the-shelf plano-convex quartz lens (RI ≅ 1.46) paired with an RI-matched immersion oil (RI ≅ 1.46) to form a hybrid solid−liquid system with hemispherical geometry, increasing effective NA by a factor of _n_. Super-SCOPE extends this concept using a Weierstrass super-hemispherical geometry, achieving NA scaling of _n_ 2 and enabling higher resolution, albeit with reduced tolerance to optical variations. The chosen RI (≅1.46) enables compatibility across a broad range of clearing methods, from aqueous (RI ≅ 1.33) to solvent-based (RI ≅ 1.56) protocols, without hardware modification or change of chamber medium. The chamber geometry is designed such that the distance between the illumination plane and the curved lens surface equals the lens radius ( _r_) for SCOPE or _r_(1 + 1 / _n_) for Super-SCOPE (Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1)), ensuring proper alignment of illumination and detection and effective wavefront precompensation. Although implemented here using a plano-convex lens for simplicity and availability, the HySIL framework supports a wide range of optical geometries (discussed below) through RI-matched solid−liquid pairing. In its current implementation, Super-SCOPE serves primarily as a proof-of-principle extension of the HySIL framework, demonstrating the potential for further resolution enhancement, and is not yet a fully optimized, robust configuration for routine imaging.

We performed Zemax optical simulations to evaluate the performance of SCOPE and Super-SCOPE for imaging large samples across diverse clearing conditions (Fig. [1e−g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1)). The detection system was modeled using a 40-mm focal length lens (NA\_air = 0.28) and a 200-mm tube lens. For direct comparison across configurations (air, immersion, SCOPE and Super-SCOPE), a hemispherical curvature of 16 mm was used, with an additional simulation at 27.5-mm radius to match experimental conditions (Supplementary Fig. [1a−c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)), yielding consistent results.

As shown in Fig. [1e−g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1) and Supplementary Fig. [1a−d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1), HySIL-based SCOPE and Super-SCOPE designs substantially reduce spherical aberrations, achieving diffraction-limited or near-diffraction-limited performance (Fig. [1e,f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1)) and resolution enhancement by factors of approximately _n_ and _n_ 2, respectively. Wavefront error analysis further confirms effective compensation of optical path differences across both sagittal and tangential planes (Supplementary Fig. [1b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)), demonstrating efficient precompensation of phase errors introduced by high-RI media.

We next assessed tolerance to variations in sample RI (1.38−1.54) and thickness (2−6 mm). In the simulations, a generic sample with thicknesses of 2 mm, 4 mm and 6 mm and RIs ranging from 1.38 to 1.54 was modeled as a planar slab adjacent to the sample plane (Fig. [1g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1)) to evaluate the tolerance of SCOPE to variations in sample optical properties. As shown in Fig. [1g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1) and Supplementary Fig. [1c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1), the root mean square (RMS) wavefront error for SCOPE remained below or near the Maréchal criterion ( _λ_/14) across all tested conditions, indicating robust aberration correction without hardware adjustments. As expected, Super-SCOPE exhibited reduced tolerance to RI variation but still outperformed conventional air objective imaging in immersion media.

Finally, we evaluated the generality of the HySIL framework across different lens geometries. Optical simulations show that off-the-shelf bi-convex and meniscus lenses achieve image quality similar to the plano-convex configuration when implemented within the HySIL framework ( [Methods](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Sec9), Supplementary Fig. [2a−f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1) and Supplementary Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)), demonstrating that HySIL provides a generalizable refractive framework in which optical performance is largely independent of the specific lens geometry and instead governed by the RI-matched hybrid configuration. In addition, off-axis performance was quantified using Strehl ratio analysis across the field of view (FOV), defining the diffraction-limited region (Strehl ≥ 0.8) and corresponding usable field limits for each objective (Supplementary Fig. [2c,f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)). These results indicate that HySIL implementations maintain high image quality across multiple geometries while effectively suppressing spherical aberrations relative to conventional implementations of the same lenses (for example, an off-the-shelf meniscus lens used in a standard configuration; Supplementary Fig. [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)). Furthermore, system performance remains robust to temperature-dependent RI variations typical of standard laboratory conditions ( [Methods](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Sec9) and Supplementary Fig. [2e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)).

Collectively, these simulations establish HySIL as a generalizable refractive framework for aberration-corrected, high-resolution imaging of large, cleared samples using inexpensive, long-working-distance air objectives. The passive, modular design of SCOPE and Super-SCOPE eliminates the need for sealed immersion assemblies and enables straightforward integration into diverse imaging systems, including our lab-built pLSM (Fig. [1h](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1)) and its commercial implementation (SLICE; MBF Bioscience). In addition, the framework introduces additional degrees of freedom for optical optimization, as demonstrated by its compatibility with multiple lens geometries.

### Experimental validation of SCOPE through PSF characterization

To experimentally validate SCOPE performance, we measured and characterized the PSF of the detection optics using two inexpensive long-working-distance air objectives: Mitutoyo ×10/0.28 NA/34-mm WD and ×5/0.14 NA/34-mm WD. The SCOPE imaging device incorporated a plano-convex fused quartz lens (RI ≅ 1.46; radius of curvature of 27.5 mm) paired with an RI-matched immersion oil to form the hybrid solid−liquid optical system.

We first imaged subresolution (150-nm) gold nanoparticles embedded in 0.5% agarose (H2O) under wide-field illumination to assess the detection PSF of SCOPE (Fig. [2a,b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig2)) and Super-SCOPE (Supplementary Fig. [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)). Identical illumination power was applied for both SCOPE and conventional air immersion configurations to enable direct comparison of resolution and signal collection efficiency. As shown in Fig. [2a,b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig2), SCOPE effectively corrected spherical aberrations across multi-millimeter-scale FOVs (1.2 mm for the ×10 objective and 2.7 mm for the ×5 objective) and produced higher signal intensity relative to the air immersion configuration (compare lookup tables in Fig. [2a,b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig2)).

Box plot elements are defined as in **c**. The diffraction-limited FOV, defined using a Strehl ratio ≥ 0.8, provides a conservative criterion for optical performance. Based on Strehl ratio analysis (Supplementary Fig. [2c,f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)), this corresponds to approximately 0.74 mm for the ×10/0.28 NA objective and approximately 1.7 mm for the ×5/0.14 NA objective. However, experimental PSF measurements show that lateral resolution remains better than the air objective diffraction limit over larger regions (approximately 1.2 mm for ×10 and approximately 2.5 mm for ×5; Fig. [2c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig2)), which we define as the practical usable FOV. This corrected optical field corresponds to the per-tile imaging region, with larger specimens acquired through tiled imaging and stitching of subvolumes. Experimental characterization of the Super-SCOPE configuration (Supplementary Fig. [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)) further demonstrates reduced lateral and axial PSF widths, consistent with the expected _n_ 2 scaling of the effective NA for the Weierstrass geometry.

We next quantified the detection PSF by measuring lateral and axial full width at half maximum (FWHM; Fig. [2c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig2) and Supplementary Fig. [3a−c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)), yielding lateral resolutions of approximately 0.75 µm (×10; SCOPE), approximately 0.56 µm (×10; Super-SCOPE) and approximately 1.2 µm (×5; SCOPE) and axial FWHM values of approximately 11 µm (×10; SCOPE), approximately 5 µm (×10; Super-SCOPE) and approximately 18 µm (×5; SCOPE). As in classical SIL configurations (hemispherical and Weierstrass geometries), the resolution achieved with SCOPE and Super-SCOPE exceeds the diffraction limits predicted by Abbeʼs criterion for the nominal design NA in air (red dashed lines in Fig. [2c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig2) and Supplementary Fig. [3c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)), reflecting the effective NA enhancement enabled by the hybrid refractive configuration. Notably, these values approach those obtained with high-NA immersion objectives, which are substantially more expensive and limited by shorter WDs (Supplementary Fig. [4a,b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)). Together, these results demonstrate that the hybrid solid–liquid optical approach enables inexpensive air objectives to achieve high-resolution imaging over large volumes. Consistent with the comparative analysis in Supplementary Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1), HySIL achieves improved performance relative to meniscus-based approaches under similar conditions.

Finally, we integrated the SCOPE imaging device into our low-cost pLSM/SLICE platform and imaged 200-nm fluorescent beads (Fig. [2d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig2)) to evaluate performance under light-sheet illumination. Both lateral and axial PSF widths were quantified across the FOV, providing a complete characterization of light-sheet detection performance consistent with wide-field measurements. The resulting data confirmed high-resolution, aberration-corrected imaging (see lateral and axial FWHM quantification in Fig. [2d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig2)) with preserved chromatic alignment across multiple excitation channels (Fig. [2e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig2)). Moreover, SCOPE maintained high performance for deep imaging, shown up to 1 cm in depth and across a 2.6-mm FOV (Supplementary Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)), demonstrating that integration into LSM enables standard air objectives to achieve enhanced resolution and achromatic imaging across large volumes while retaining long WDs.

Collectively, these experiments demonstrate that SCOPE enables aberration-corrected, submicron-resolution imaging across multi-millimeter FOVs and centimeter-scale imaging depths using standard, inexpensive air objectives. Together with low-cost LSM platforms such as pLSM/SLICE, SCOPE establishes a scalable and cost-effective solution for high-throughput, high-resolution volumetric imaging—a key advance toward democratizing advanced optical microscopy and accelerating data-driven biological discovery.

### Integration of ExM with SCOPE for large-volume, nanoscale imaging

Recent advances in ExM[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR5 "Chen, F., Tillberg, P. W. & Boyden, E. S. Expansion microscopy. Science 347, 543–548 (2015)."), [6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR6 "Wassie, A. T., Zhao, Y. & Boyden, E. S. Expansion microscopy: principles and uses in biological research. Nat. Methods 16, 33–41 (2019).") enable nanoscale interrogation of biological structures by physically enlarging specimens, allowing effectively subdiffraction imaging using conventional light microscopy. However, ExM introduces additional challenges: multifold expansion dilutes fluorophores and reduces signal per voxel; expanded tissues are mechanically fragile; and imaging intact specimens requires long-working-distance detection with well-controlled aberrations and mounting strategies that preserve image quality across large volumes.

Leveraging the HySIL framework implemented in SCOPE, which combines long-working-distance air objectives with effective NA enhancement and aberration correction, we integrated SCOPE with ExM for high-resolution imaging of large, expanded tissues. Using expanded _Thy1_ − _GFP_ mouse brain sections ( [Methods](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Sec9)), we systematically optimized the immersion formulation and mounting strategy to balance optical performance, fluorophore stability and structural integrity while maintaining isotropic expansion. Optimal conditions employed 65−85% glycerol supplemented with >2.5 mg ml−1 1,4-diazabicyclo\[2.2.2\]octane (DABCO), which preserved fluorophore brightness, minimized photobleaching and stabilized the expanded tissue. Samples were mounted in quartz cuvettes (RI ≅ 1.46; 10 × 10-mm, 10 × 20-mm or 20 ×20-mm base dimensions, as appropriate) to maintain RI continuity with the SCOPE system.

Under these conditions, expanded (approximately 2.45-fold; [Methods](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Sec9)) _Thy1_ − _GFP_ mouse brain sections were imaged using a ×10/0.28 NA/34-mm WD air objective for detection and a ×5/0.14 NA/34-mm WD air objective for light-sheet illumination with the SCOPE-enabled pLSM/SLICE system. As shown in Fig. [3a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig3) (Supplementary Video [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM4)), SCOPE enabled high-resolution, aberration-corrected imaging of neuronal architecture, clearly resolving dendritic spines and fine neural processes across large tissue volumes (Fig. [3b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig3)). The resulting datasets were readily compatible with automated reconstruction tools, enabling comprehensive neuronal and dendritic spine tracing (Fig. [3b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig3)). Given the measured lateral detection PSF (approximately 0.75 µm; Fig. [2c,d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig2)) and tissue expansion, the effective resolution was sufficient for automated neurite and spine quantification.

We next developed an ExM-SCOPE workflow for whole intact salamander ( _Pleurodeles waltl_) brains, an emerging model in regenerative and comparative neurobiology[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR22 "Matheson, A. M. M., Chua, N. J. & Tosches, M. A. Iberian ribbed newts. Curr. Biol. 35, R49–R51 (2025)."). By optimizing gelation, digestion and immersion conditions, we achieved robust expansion while preserving cellular morphology. Quantitative analysis of neuronal soma dimensions across multiple brain regions confirmed an approximately 2.5-fold expansion (Supplementary Fig. [5a,b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)) with preserved cellular ellipticity (Supplementary Fig. [5c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)). Brains immunostained for tyrosine hydroxylase were mounted in quartz cuvettes (RI ≅ 1.46) and imaged using the SCOPE-enabled pLSM/SLICE system, yielding high-resolution volumetric datasets of the entire expanded brain. These datasets capture both the brain-wide organization of the dopaminergic system (Fig. [4a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig4)) and its fine anatomical detail at the regional level (Fig. [4b,c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig4) and Supplementary Videos [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM5) and [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM6)).

Together, these results demonstrate that SCOPE, through its hybrid solid−liquid optical architecture, enables scalable, high-resolution imaging of large expanded tissues using long-working-distance air objectives. The combined ExM-SCOPE framework provides a practical route to organ-scale nanoscale mapping with cost-effective, high-throughput and portable instrumentation.

### SCOPE enables multi-immersion imaging across diverse clearing methods

Advanced tissue-clearing methods exhibit wide variability in their optical properties: aqueous-based protocols such as CLARITY[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR23 "Chung, K. et al. Structural and molecular interrogation of intact biological systems. Nature 497, 332–337 (2013)."), [24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR24 "Tomer, R., Ye, L., Hsueh, B. & Deisseroth, K. Advanced CLARITY for rapid and high-resolution imaging of intact tissues. Nat. Protoc. 9, 1682–1697 (2014).") yield RIs of approximately 1.44−1.48, whereas solvent-based methods such as iDISCO[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR25 "Renier, N. et al. iDISCO: a simple, rapid method to immunolabel large tissue samples for volume imaging. Cell 159, 896–910 (2014).") and ethyl cinnamate (ECi)[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR26 "Klingberg, A. et al. Fully automated evaluation of total glomerular number and capillary tuft size in nephritic kidneys using lightsheet microscopy. J. Am. Soc. Nephrol. 28, 452–459 (2017).") reach RI values approaching 1.56. High-quality, aberration-corrected imaging across this RI range typically requires different specialized objectives or hardware modifications.

Optical simulations of SCOPE (system RI ≅ 1.46; Fig. [1g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1)) indicated broad tolerance to variations in sample RI while maintaining effective aberration correction across an RI range of 1.38−1.54. This performance eliminates the need for hardware adjustments and supports a single, generalizable workflow for diverse biological and clinical specimens. To experimentally assess this versatility, we evaluated SCOPE (implemented at RI ≅ 1.46) across samples immersed in media spanning from aqueous to organic solvent conditions.

All imaging experiments were performed with samples mounted in quartz cuvettes (RI ≅ 1.46). As shown in Supplementary Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1), SCOPE enabled color-corrected deep imaging (up to 1 cm shown) of polychromatic fluorescent beads embedded in 0.5% agarose/H2O (RI ≅ 1.33), despite the large RI mismatch between the aqueous sample and the hybrid solid−liquid immersion lens implemented with RI ≅1.46. We next applied SCOPE to image CUBIC-R-cleared mouse brains (RI ≅ 1.51) from the fosTRAP2−tdTomato transgenic line. As shown in Fig. [5a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig5) and Supplementary Video [4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM7), the SCOPE approach provided high-contrast, aberration-corrected imaging throughout the entire centimeter-scale brain volume (21.13 mm × 16.98 mm × 9.55 mm; _x_, _y_, _z_). The resulting datasets were directly compatible with standard neuroinformatics pipelines, including registration to the Allen Brain Atlas[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR27 "Wang, Q. et al. The Allen Mouse Brain Common Coordinate Framework: a 3D reference atlas. Cell 181, 936–953 (2020).") and automated segmentation and quantification of tdTomato+ neurons, revealing the expected brain-wide activation patterns. Next, we imaged iDISCO-cleared (RI ≅ 1.56) cavefish ( _Astyanax mexicanus_) brains stained with nuclear dye TO-PRO-3 (Fig. [5b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig5) and Supplementary Video [5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM8)). SCOPE-enabled imaging yielded high-contrast, single-nucleus-resolved images throughout the entire brain, including densely labeled regions (Fig. [5b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig5), insets), confirming its effective performance in high-RI, solvent-cleared tissues.

SCOPE imaging of day 94 iPSC-derived cortical organoids incorporated with microglia[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR28 "Guttikonda, S. R. et al. Fully defined human pluripotent stem cell-derived microglia and tri-culture system model C3 production in Alzheimer’s disease. Nat. Neurosci. 24, 343–354 (2021).") (labeled with anti-IBA1) showed complete colonization of the organoid after 2 weeks of co-culture (Fig. [5c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig5) and Supplementary Video [6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM9)). They show wide distribution and tiling throughout the organoid as well as varying morphology, including amoeboid (Fig. [5c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig5), image 1), elongated (Fig. [5c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig5), image 2) and ramified (Fig. [5c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig5), images 3 and 4), indicating heterogeneity in inflammatory states.

Together, these results demonstrate that the SCOPE imaging device provides robust, multi-immersion compatibility across tissue-clearing methods ranging from aqueous to solvent-based immersion media without requiring hardware changes, optical realignment or system reconfiguration.

### Scalable high-resolution 3D histopathology with SCOPE

Histopathological analysis remains the cornerstone of preclinical disease research, clinical diagnosis, therapeutic monitoring and drug discovery. Traditional workflows rely on microscopic examination of thin tissue sections stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) or immunohistochemical markers. Although these methods have been indispensable for over a century, they inherently disrupt the native 3D tissue architecture, limiting insight into spatial cell−cell and cell−matrix relationships that underpin disease progression—a critical limitation in heterogeneous pathologies such as cancer[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR29 "Liu, J. T. C., Glaser, A. K., Poudel, C. & Vaughan, J. C. Nondestructive 3D pathology with light-sheet fluorescence microscopy for translational research and clinical assays. Ann. Rev. Anal. Chem. 16, 231–252 (2023).").

Recent studies have demonstrated that deep learning models trained on volumetric histopathological datasets markedly outperform two-dimensional-based approaches for key clinical tasks, including cancer detection, tumor grading, surgical margin assessment and recurrence prediction[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR29 "Liu, J. T. C., Glaser, A. K., Poudel, C. & Vaughan, J. C. Nondestructive 3D pathology with light-sheet fluorescence microscopy for translational research and clinical assays. Ann. Rev. Anal. Chem. 16, 231–252 (2023)."), [30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR30 "Ertürk, A. Deep 3D histology powered by tissue clearing, omics and AI. Nat. Methods 21, 1153–1165 (2024)."), [31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR31 "Song, A. H. et al. Analysis of 3D pathology samples using weakly supervised AI. Cell 187, 2502–2520 (2024)."). These findings underscore the transformative potential of 3D histopathology for precision medicine. Nevertheless, widespread adoption of 3D imaging techniques faces persistent barriers, including prohibitive microscope costs, operational complexity and limited accessibility. Here, we demonstrate how the SCOPE platform addresses these barriers through its combination of affordability and high-resolution 3D imaging capabilities.

As an initial application, we focused on normal and cancerous human breast tissue. We implemented fluorescent H&E analog labeling of fresh-frozen breast specimens, using TO-PRO-3 for nuclear staining and eosin for cytoplasmic labeling[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR32 "Bishop, K. W. et al. An end-to-end workflow for nondestructive 3D pathology. Nat. Protoc. 19, 1122–1148 (2024)."). Samples were cleared and imaged in ECi (RI ≅ 1.56), and data were acquired in two channels. Through a false-coloring procedure[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR33 "Serafin, R., Xie, W., Glaser, A. K. & Liu, J. T. C. FalseColor-Python: a rapid intensity-leveling and digital-staining package for fluorescence-based slide-free digital pathology. PLoS ONE 15, e0233198 (2020)."), the two-channel fluorescence images were converted into conventional H&E-like representations for compatibility with standard pathology workflows.

As shown in Fig. [6a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig6) and Supplementary Videos [7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM10) and [8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM11), the SCOPE system enables rapid, high-quality 3D imaging of large, intact human breast tissue samples at nuclear resolution. The volumetric datasets reveal the mesoscopic organization of terminal duct-lobular units (TDLUs) into tree-like networks while resolving subcellular structures within individual acini. Imaging of cancerous tissue (Fig. [6b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig6) and Supplementary Video [9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM12); Fig. [6c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig6) shows conventional H&E-stained tissue section from the same specimen) further demonstrates the platform’s capability to capture diagnostic histopathological features of malignancy, including abnormal nuclear morphology, fibrotic foci and normal and disrupted tissue architecture, highlighting the potential of SCOPE for scalable, high-resolution 3D histopathology and disease characterization.
