## Discussion

We present a hybrid solid–liquid optical framework that enables precise wavefront control through RI-matched integration of a solid optical element with a liquid medium. In this configuration, the two components function as a continuous optical system, enabling aberration-corrected, high-resolution 3D imaging of large specimens when combined with long-working-distance air objectives. The SCOPE and Super-SCOPE implementations translate this framework into modular devices that integrate readily into both open-source and commercial LSM platforms, including low-cost pLSM-based systems[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR11 "Chen, Y. et al. Low-cost and scalable projected light-sheet microscopy for the high-resolution imaging of cleared tissue and living samples. Nat. Biomed. Eng. 8, 1109–1123 (2024)."), enabling scalable subcellular-resolution imaging across large volumes. Although demonstrated here using a plano-convex lens, HySIL is inherently generalizable and supports a wide range of optical geometries through RI-matched solid−liquid pairing (Supplementary Fig. [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)).

Curved interface approaches such as SIMlens[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR13 "Barner, L. A., Glaser, A. K., True, L. D., Reder, N. P. & Liu, J. T. C. Solid immersion meniscus lens (SIMlens) for open-top light-sheet microscopy. Opt. Lett. 44, 4451–4454 (2019)."), [14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR14 "Barner, L. A., Glaser, A. K., Huang, H., True, L. D. & Liu, J. T. C. Multi-resolution open-top light-sheet microscopy to enable efficient 3D pathology workflows. Biomed. Opt. Express 11, 6605 (2020)."), [20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR20 "Glaser, A. K. et al. A hybrid open-top light-sheet microscope for versatile multi-scale imaging of cleared tissues. Nat. Methods 19, 613–619 (2022).") reduce spherical aberrations using carefully designed optical windows but rely on multiple refractive interfaces and precise RI matching across optical components. By contrast, HySIL adopts a distinct refractive architecture in which the solid optical element and immersion medium are index matched to form a continuous optical interface (Supplementary Fig. [2a,b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)). This eliminates internal refractive discontinuities, reduces sensitivity to RI mismatch, improves performance and enables flexible implementation across multiple lens geometries. As a result, optical performance is governed primarily by the hybrid refractive configuration rather than a specific lens design (see Supplementary Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1) for a feature-by-feature comparison with SIMlens-based and off-the-shelf meniscus approaches).

This framework also enables extension to higher-performance geometries. For example, the Super-SCOPE configuration (Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1) and Supplementary Fig. [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)), based on Weierstrass super-hemispherical geometry, achieves _n_ 2 scaling of the effective NA. Such configurations are naturally supported within HySIL, whereas similar implementations in multi-interface systems would require more stringent optical constraints and reoptimization. Together, these architectural features translate into improved robustness and consistent imaging performance across diverse sample conditions.

A key practical advantage of SCOPE is its robustness to variations in sample RI, enabling consistent high performance across a broad range (1.33−1.56) without hardware modification while preserving chromatic correction and providing a wide practical per-tile FOV (up to 2.6 mm; Fig. [2b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig2) and Supplementary Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)). This capability is validated across diverse preparations, including cleared and expanded brain tissues, organoids and large-volume specimens (Figs. [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig3)– [5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig5) and Supplementary Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)). As in other high-resolution systems, the usable FOV is constrained by the effective NA; in practice, large volumes are acquired through tiled imaging with reliable stitching using standard software pipelines. Acquisition times remain manageable (on the order of hours depending on resolution), and overall throughput is determined primarily by the underlying light-sheet platform.

Although demonstrated here using fixed and cleared samples, the HySIL framework is, in principle, compatible with live imaging. Its tolerance to RI variation and use of long-working-distance air objectives provide a favorable basis for live-sample applications, particularly when combined with lower-index materials and optimized system configurations.

Beyond the proof-of-concept demonstrations presented here, SCOPE is well suited for applications requiring simultaneous access to fine cellular features and large-scale spatial organization, such as mapping neuronal projections across whole brains or quantifying cell type distributions in intact tissues and organoids. More broadly, its multi-immersion compatibility enables systematic imaging of heterogeneous samples prepared using different clearing or expansion protocols within a unified workflow. We further demonstrate SCOPE as a scalable benchtop solution for 3D histopathology (Fig. [6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig6)), enabling nuclear-resolution imaging of large intact human tissues and preserving the spatial context lost in conventional sectioning.

Despite its robust performance, several opportunities for further development remain. First, although HySIL enhances effective NA, ultimate resolution and light collection remain constrained by the NA of the air objective. Super-SCOPE provides additional NA scaling but with increased sensitivity to alignment and optical variations and reduced spectral bandwidth. Accordingly, SCOPE offers a robust, broadly applicable solution, whereas Super-SCOPE is best suited for applications requiring maximum resolution under controlled conditions. Future work may further optimize these geometries or incorporate computational approaches for chromatic correction. Second, although compatibility is demonstrated across common clearing methods, samples with extreme RIs may require alternative materials or immersion formulations. The modular SCOPE design facilitates straightforward adaptation to such conditions. Third, although demonstrated in horizontal benchtop systems, the HySIL framework is configuration independent and can be extended to upright or inverted geometries.

## Methods

### Design, simulations and PSF characterization of SCOPE

#### Optical simulations

All optical simulations were performed in Ansys Zemax OpticStudio (2024). As illustrated by the ray traces in Fig. [1e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1) and Supplementary Fig. [1a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1), the optical model consisted of a paraxial detection lens (serving as the objective), a Thorlabs TTL200 tube lens (black box model provided by Thorlabs) and a plano-convex fused silica lens (radius of curvature = 16 mm in Fig. [1e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1) or 27.5 mm in Supplementary Fig. [1a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1); Corning, 7980 substrate) serving as the solid element of the hybrid immersion lens. The RI of the immersion liquid was set to match that of the fused silica ( _n_ ≅ 1.46), forming a continuous solid−liquid optical system. The sample was modeled as a planar slab of defined thickness and RI.

To enable direct comparison across configurations—air−air, air immersion, SCOPE and Super-SCOPE—a fixed aperture stop was used to represent the same detection objective in all models. The aperture diameter was set according to an air objective with NA = 0.28, matching the Mitutoyo ×10/0.28 NA objective used experimentally. For the air immersion configuration, the distance between the flat glass window and the object plane being imaged was adjusted based on the optical path difference in oil to ensure equivalent optical and geometrical distances. In the SCOPE configuration, the distance between the convex surface of the correction lens and the object plane being imaged was set equal to the lens radius ( _r_). For Super-SCOPE, this distance followed _r_ (1 + 1 / _n_), where _r_ is the lens radius and _n_ is the RI of the lens material. The fast Semi-Diameters option in Zemax was used to ensure that the aperture was calculated using marginal rays for high accuracy. The field type was defined as an object height corresponding to a 700-µm FOV at the sample plane. Simulations were performed for three representative wavelengths, 520 nm, 590 nm and 640 nm, with relative weights of 5.0, 1.0 and 1.0, respectively. The objective position was optimized as a variable within ±2 mm of its nominal position in all configurations.

The Huygens PSF, its cross-section and the corresponding diffraction-based encircled energy plots (shown in Fig. [1e,f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1)) were calculated at 520 nm for each configuration, with pupil and image sampling set to 512 × 512. The relative irradiance, normalized to the Strehl ratio, was calculated, and detection plane distances were scaled by the system magnification: ×5 (air), ×4.9 (air immersion), ×7.3 (SCOPE) and ×10.7 (Super-SCOPE). The optical path difference (OPD) wavefront error, used to quantify residual aberrations (Supplementary Fig. [1b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)), was evaluated at 520 nm with NA = 0.28 (corresponding to the Mitutoyo ×10 objective) over a 700-µm FOV. Tangential and sagittal planes were analyzed along orthogonal field axes to capture off-axis aberrations, and results were compared between the center and edge of the FOV for SCOPE and conventional flat-window (air immersion) configurations. The RMS wavefront error (Fig. [1g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#Fig1) and Supplementary Fig. [1c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#MOESM1)) was computed at 520 nm while varying both sample RI and sample thickness. Specifically, samples with thicknesses of 2 mm, 4 mm and 6 mm and RIs ranging from 1.38 to 1.54 were modeled as planar slabs adjacent to the sample plane. The system was reoptimized for each condition to maintain optimal performance. RI values were varied in increments of ±0.02 around the system baseline ( _n_ = 1.46).

#### Extended optical simulations of different lens geometries

Additional optical simulations were performed to evaluate the impact of RI matching on spherical aberration and usable FOV across different lens geometries. Three commercially available off-the-shelf lenses were modeled, including plano-convex (Edmund Optics, 48670), meniscus (Thorlabs, LE1234) and bi-convex (Thorlabs, LB1761) configurations. For each geometry, corresponding index-modified versions were generated to enable direct comparison between index-matched and index-mismatched conditions relative to the immersion medium ( _n_ ≅ 1.46).

All configurations incorporated a modeled ×5 detection objective combined with a Thorlabs TTL200 tube lens (black box model). NA values were scaled according to the RI of the immersion medium. Specifically, a 0.28 NA objective in air corresponds to an effective NA of approximately 0.41 and a 0.14 NA objective to approximately 0.20 under oil immersion (NA\_oil = NA\_air × 1.46). The higher NA configuration (0.41) was used to assess sensitivity to RI mismatch, whereas the moderate NA configuration (0.20) was used to evaluate FOV expansion. Image quality was quantified at field positions of 0 mm, 0.15 mm, 0.35 mm and 0.55 mm, and the usable field was defined using a Strehl ratio threshold of 0.8.

For the plano-convex lens configuration, the distance between the focal plane and the curved surface vertex was set equal to the lens radius of curvature, and the spacing between the lens and objective was numerically optimized to maximize image quality. The commercial lens was modeled in fused silica and compared to a material-substituted configuration using N-BK7. After material substitution, each system was reoptimized to achieve best performance.

For the meniscus lens configuration, both index-matched and index-mismatched conditions were evaluated. In the index-matched case, the focal-plane-to-vertex distance was set equal to the outer radius of curvature, and the lens–objective spacing was optimized. The lens material was set to fused silica to approximate index matching to the immersion medium. In the index-mismatched case, the commercial Thorlabs LE1234 lens (N-BK7) was used, and the focal-plane-to-vertex distance was adjusted and optimized to partially compensate for spherical aberration arising from RI mismatch.

For the bi-convex lens configuration, the focal-plane-to-vertex distance was set equal to the outer surface radius of curvature, and the spacing between the lens and objective was optimized. The commercial Thorlabs LB1761 lens (N-BK7) was compared to a fused silica version under otherwise identical conditions. As in the other geometries, all configurations were independently reoptimized after material substitution.

Across all three geometries, RI matching consistently reduced primary spherical aberration and improved Strehl ratios across the field. The improvement was particularly pronounced at higher effective NA (0.41), where aberration sensitivity to RI mismatch is amplified. Under both NA conditions, RI matching expanded the FOV, as defined by Strehl ≥ 0.8.

#### Temperature sensitivity of RI matching

To evaluate the influence of temperature-dependent RI variations of the immersion oil, optical simulations were performed using the SCOPE configuration. According to the manufacturer’s specifications (Fused Silica Matching Liquid Code 50350; Cargille Laboratories), the RI of the immersion oil changes by approximately 0.0018 per 5 °C. Based on this relationship, RIs corresponding to temperatures of 20 °C, 25 °C, 30 °C and 35 °C were modeled. Note that the RI of the lens was not altered in these simulations, as its change with temperature is negligible relative to oil.

For each temperature condition, the RI of the immersion oil was adjusted accordingly, and the optical system was reoptimized to achieve best performance. The RMS wavefront error at the field center was extracted as a quantitative measure of optical performance. The 25 °C condition was used as the baseline reference, corresponding to the nominal operating temperature of the system.

#### SCOPE device implementation

The SCOPE imaging device consisted of an off-the-shelf plano-convex lens (Edmund Optics, 48-670; _R_ = 27.5 mm, _Ø_ = 25 mm) as the solid component and a Cargille Laboratories RI-matching immersion oil ( _n_ ≅ 1.46, code: 50350) as the liquid component, forming a hybrid solid−liquid seamless system. The chamber body was 3D printed using black resin and sealed with optical-grade epoxy to ensure mechanical stability and prevent leakage. The lens was fixed to the chamber window using ultraviolet-curable adhesive. For detection, Mitutoyo ×10/0.28 NA and ×5/0.14 NA long-working-distance air objectives were used; illumination was provided by Mitutoyo ×5/0.14 NA for most experiments. For optimal aberration correction, the imaged region of the sample was positioned approximately at the designed distance from the curved optical interface (equal to the lens radius in the SCOPE configuration), although small deviations of several hundred micrometers did not noticeably degrade image quality in practice.

The solid−liquid interface was maintained by cleaning the lens surface and chamber with lens tissue and ethanol after imaging sessions, once the chamber was emptied. In practice, the immersion oil was replaced only when debris accumulation was observed that could degrade image quality. No specialized cleaning procedures were required beyond standard optical handling practices.

#### PSF imaging and characterization

To measure the system PSF, gold nanoparticles (Sigma-Aldrich, 742058; 150-nm diameter) were suspended in 0.5% agarose to a final concentration of 5% (v/v) in 1.5 ml of total volume. For wide-field PSF imaging, the pLSM illumination light sheet thickness was expanded to full extent to result in illumination of the entire volume. The scattered light was collected in the detection arm without emission filter. _z_-stacks were acquired at 2-µm step size using identical laser power for all conditions to enable quantitative signal comparison. For fluorescence-based PSF measurements and analysis, 15-µm polychromatic (FocalCheck Microspheres; Thermo Fisher Scientific, F7239) and 200-nm fluorescent beads (Fluoresbrite YG Carboxylate Microspheres, 0.20 µm; Polysciences, 09834-10) were embedded in 0.5% agarose under identical conditions. Appropriate excitation and emission filters were selected for each spectral channel.

#### PSF FWHM analysis

Image stacks were analyzed to determine the lateral ( _x_ − _y_) and axial ( _z_) FWHM of individual PSFs. All beads or nanoparticles in an image volume were manually marked by using a custom Python-based graphical user interface (GUI) from maximum-intensity projection image. Center coordinates were further refined by locating the pixel of maximum intensity in the immediate neighborhood. Intensity profiles along the _x_, _y_ and _z_ axes were extracted and fit to a Gaussian model, from which FWHM values were calculated. The resulting lateral and axial resolutions were compared across objectives and configurations (SCOPE versus air immersion).

### Sample preparations

#### ExM of salamander brain

Building upon multiple published ExM protocols, we systematically optimized experimental parameters to establish a reliable method for whole-brain expansion in the salamander _P. waltl_, as described below.

An adult salamander was obtained from a breeding colony established at Columbia University, with all experiments conducted in accordance with National Institutes of Health (NIH) guidelines and Columbia University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) policies governing animal use and welfare (IACUC protocol AC-AABF2564). Animals were maintained in an aquatics facility at 20 °C under a 12-hour light, 12-hour dark cycle. The animal was anesthetized by immersion in 0.1% MS-222, followed by transcardial perfusion with 10 ml of cold 1× PBS to flush out the blood and then with 10 ml of cold 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) in PBS for fixation. The brain was carefully extracted and post-fixed overnight in 4% PFA at 4 °C. Whole-brain immunolabeling was performed according to the salamander immunohistochemistry protocol described previously[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR34 "Woych, J. et al. Cell-type profiling in salamanders identifies innovations in vertebrate forebrain evolution. Science 377, eabp9186 (2022)."). To label dopaminergic neurons and their processes, we used a rabbit anti-tyrosine hydroxylase primary antibody (1:1,000; Sigma-Aldrich, AB152), followed by a goat anti-rabbit Alexa Fluor 546-conjugated secondary antibody (1:1,000; Thermo Fisher Scientific, A-11035).

After immunostaining, the sample was transferred from PBS to MES-buffered saline (2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid) to enhance penetration of the anchoring reagent. This step was adapted from a whole-brain ExM protocol for mouse brain[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR35 "Ouellette, N. et al. Whole mouse brain delipidation, immunolabeling, and expansion microscopy. protocols.io                  https://doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.n92ldpwjxl5b/v1                                 (2023)."). The brain was incubated in Acryloyl-X (AcX) at 4 °C for 48 hours, adjusted for the smaller size of the salamander brain. The sample was then washed twice in PBS at 4 °C for 1 hour each, followed by an overnight wash. For hydrogel embedding, the Stock X monomer solution was adapted from the TissUExM protocol[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR36 "Steib, E., Vagena-Pantoula, C. & Vermot, J. TissUExM protocol for ultrastructure expansion microscopy of zebrafish larvae and mouse embryos. STAR Protoc. 4, 102257 (2023)."), with increased acrylamide concentration based on our optimization experiments. The final monomer composition was as follows: 23% sodium acrylate, 10% acrylamide, 0.1% N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide and 1× PBS. This solution was activated with 0.12% (w/v) VA-044, and the brain was incubated in the activated monomer mix at 4 °C for 2 days.

For gelation, the sample was degassed in a vacuum chamber for 20 minutes, purged with nitrogen gas and then incubated in an airtight container at 37 °C for approximately 3 hours or until complete polymerization was observed[37](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR37 "Asano, S. M. et al. Expansion microscopy: protocols for imaging proteins and RNA in cells and tissues. Curr. Protoc. Cell Biol. 80, e56 (2018)."). The gelled brain was then removed from the container, trimmed and briefly washed twice in PBS (5 minutes each). The embedded sample was incubated in digestion buffer containing 8 U ml−1 Proteinase K at room temperature for 24 hours or until the tissue appeared fully cleared. After digestion, the gel was washed twice in PBS (1 hour each), followed by an overnight wash at room temperature.

Expansion was initiated by immersing the sample in deionized water, with water changes every 30−60 minutes. Expansion progress was monitored visually and halted once the desired expansion factor was achieved. The sample was then reimmersed in PBS to stabilize the gel and reduce overexpansion. To better match the RI of the expanded sample for optimal imaging, the tissue was equilibrated in a graded series of glycerol/PBS solutions, culminating in 65% glycerol in PBS (RI ≅ 1.42). To prevent photobleaching during imaging and long-term storage, 0.5% DABCO was added to all glycerol solutions. The fully expanded, RI-matched sample was imaged using the pLSM/SLICE microscope equipped with the SCOPE imaging device.

#### Expansion factor quantification

To quantify the expansion factor, the embedding gel was measured before and after expansion using photographs analyzed in ImageJ. In addition, cell size and morphology were compared between pre-expansion and post-expansion samples across multiple brain regions.

A salamander brain stained with tyrosine hydroxylase was sectioned and imaged using a confocal microscope (LSM 800, ×20) without optical clearing to preserve native morphology. Regions containing cells positive for tyrosine hydroxylase were selected along the rostro-caudal axis (diencephalon, hypothalamus) and medio-lateral axis (left and right olfactory bulbs). Corresponding regions were analyzed in both pre-expansion and post-expansion samples; expanded samples imaged sagittally were resliced in ImageJ to match coronal sections.

For each region, one section was selected, and 20 cells were measured at random. If fewer than 20 cells were present, an adjacent section five sections caudal was used to avoid repeated sampling. The major axis was defined as the longest distance across the cell and the minor axis as the perpendicular distance. These measurements were compared across regions and conditions to assess isotropy. Cell circularity was evaluated using the minor-to-major axis ratio to assess morphological preservation after expansion.

#### ExM of mouse brain sections

These mouse procedures were approved by the Columbia University IACUC (protocol AC-AABG0556). An adult (8−10 weeks old) transgenic _Thy1_ − _GFP_ (C57BL/6) mouse was transcardially perfused with 4% PFA, and the brain was extracted and post-fixed overnight in 4% PFA at 4 °C. The brain was then washed in PBS three times for 30 minutes each. A 400-μm-thick vibratome section was prepared for ExM. For processing, the same overall protocol described for the salamander brain was used, with adjustments to incubation times to account for the reduced tissue thickness. Specifically, AcX treatment was carried out overnight in PBS instead of MES-buffered saline, as the lower pH was not required for thin section penetration[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR35 "Ouellette, N. et al. Whole mouse brain delipidation, immunolabeling, and expansion microscopy. protocols.io                  https://doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.n92ldpwjxl5b/v1                                 (2023)."). The brain section was then incubated in activated Stock X monomer solution for 1 hour at 4 °C, followed by gelation and digestion as described above. Digestion was carried out for 24 hours at room temperature in the dark. The embedded sample was expanded by immersion in deionized water for a total of 3 hours, with water changes every 30−60 minutes, and was subsequently returned to PBS. For imaging, the sample was RI matched using the same 65% glycerol in PBS solution with 0.5% DABCO and imaged using the pLSM/SLICE microscope with SCOPE imaging device. The approximate expansion factor (approximately 2.45×) was estimated by comparing the outlines of multiple pre-expansion and post-expansion mouse brain sections.

#### CUBIC-R clearing of fosTRAP2 whole mouse brain

All animal procedures were conducted in accordance with an IACUC-approved protocol from the National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH; protocol LSN-02). FosTRAP2 transgenic mice[38](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR38 "Guenthner, C. J., Miyamichi, K., Yang, H. H., Heller, H. C. & Luo, L. Permanent genetic access to transiently active neurons via TRAP: targeted recombination in active populations. Neuron 78, 773–784 (2013)."), aged 3−5 months, were exposed to a novel complex environment for 1 hour to induce neuronal activity. Immediately after this exposure, mice received an intraperitoneal injection of 4-hydroxytamoxifen (4-OHT; 50 mg kg−1). One week later, animals were transcardially perfused with saline followed by 4% formaldehyde. Brains were extracted, post-fixed in 4% formaldehyde for 24 hours at 4 °C and then transferred to PBS. Tissue clearing was performed using a modified version of the CUBIC-R protocol[39](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR39 "Susaki, E. A. et al. Advanced CUBIC protocols for whole-brain and whole-body clearing and imaging. Nat. Protoc. 10, 1709–1727 (2015)."). Neurons that expressed _Fos_ during the 1-hour stimulation period prior to tamoxifen administration were permanently labeled with tdTomato, enabling visualization of activity-tagged neuronal populations across the entire brain. Cleared brains were imaged using the SCOPE-enabled pLSM/SLICE microscope.

#### iDISCO-cleared cavefish brains

Adult _A. mexicanus_ (Pachón cavefish population) were euthanized and decapitated at the gill plate using a scalpel, and the heads were briefly immersed in ice-cold 1× PBS for 30 seconds to remove residual blood. Samples were then fixed in 4% PFA in 1× PBS at 4 °C overnight (18−20 hours) with gentle shaking. The next day, heads were washed three times in 1× PBS at room temperature for 30 minutes each, with shaking. Brains were dissected and dehydrated through a graded methanol/Milli-Q water series (20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, 100%, 100%) and stored in 100% methanol at 4 °C until further processing for iDISCO+ staining, as in refs. [25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR25 "Renier, N. et al. iDISCO: a simple, rapid method to immunolabel large tissue samples for volume imaging. Cell 159, 896–910 (2014)."), [40](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR40 "Kenney, J. W. et al. A 3D adult zebrafish brain atlas (AZBA) for the digital age. eLife 10, e69988 (2021)."), [41](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR41 "Rajput, N. et al. Whole-brain mapping in adult zebrafish and identification of the functional brain network underlying the novel tank test. eNeuro 12, ENEURO.0382-24 (2025).").

For staining, brains were bleached overnight at 4 °C in 5% hydrogen peroxide in methanol without shaking (16−18 hours). The next day, samples were rehydrated through a reverse methanol/Milli-Q water gradient (80%, 60%, 40%, 20%) and finally placed into 1× PBS. Samples were then washed twice in 0.2% Triton X-100 in PBS (PBSTx) for 30 minutes each at room temperature with rocking. Brains were permeabilized in a solution of 2.3% glycine and 20% dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) in PBSTx at 37 °C for 24 hours without shaking. Blocking was performed in 6% normal donkey serum and 10% DMSO in PBSTx at 37 °C for an additional 24 hours without shaking. Samples were then incubated in TO-PRO-3 iodide nuclear stain (Invitrogen, T3605) at a 1:10,000 dilution in PTwH buffer (0.1% heparin, 0.2% Tween 20 in 1× PBS) containing 3% normal donkey serum and 5% DMSO, for 24 hours at 37 °C without shaking. After staining, brains were washed 4−5 times in PTwH over the course of 1 day at room temperature on a rocker. This wash step was repeated the following day under the same conditions, with samples protected from light, as in refs. [25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR25 "Renier, N. et al. iDISCO: a simple, rapid method to immunolabel large tissue samples for volume imaging. Cell 159, 896–910 (2014)."), [40](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR40 "Kenney, J. W. et al. A 3D adult zebrafish brain atlas (AZBA) for the digital age. eLife 10, e69988 (2021)."), [41](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR41 "Rajput, N. et al. Whole-brain mapping in adult zebrafish and identification of the functional brain network underlying the novel tank test. eNeuro 12, ENEURO.0382-24 (2025).").

For clearing, the brains were dehydrated again through a methanol/Milli-Q water gradient (20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, 100%, 100%) for 30 minutes per step at room temperature on a rocker. Samples were then incubated in 66% dichloromethane (DCM) in methanol for 3 hours at room temperature with rocking, followed by two 15-minute washes in 100% DCM. Final clearing was performed in dibenzyl ether (DBE) for at least 24 hours at room temperature in the dark. All tubes were filled completely with DBE to prevent air exposure during storage and imaging. The cleared sample was mounted in a cuvette and imaged with pLSM/SLICE equipped with SCOPE imaging device.

#### Preparation of iPSC-derived cortical organoids with microglia

Cortical organoids with microglia were generated from a human iPSC line derived from a healthy male under Columbia University institutional review board (IRB) protocol AAAP0052 (Lopes Da Costa et al.)[42](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR42 "Lopes Da Costa, B. et al. Prime editing for the investigation of aberrant splicing defect associated with a pathogenic PRPH2 variant. Mol. Ther. Nucleic Acids 36, 102740 (2025)."). The human iPSC line (1535-2) was authenticated by G-banding karyotyping (Cell Line Genetics), validated for pluripotency (OCT4, NANOG) and tri-lineage potential (PAX6/NESTIN, BRACHYURY/NCAM, SOX17/FOXA2) by immunohistochemistry and screened periodically for mycoplasma by polymerase chain reaction.

On day 0, 9,000 iPSCs were seeded per well in a 96-well U-bottom plate in DMEM/F12, 15% KSR, 5% FBS, 1% NEAA, 1% GlutaMAX, 100 µM β-mercaptoethanol, 100 nM LDN-193189, 10 µM SB-431542, 2 µM XAV-939 and 50 µM Y27632. On day 2, the media were replenished without Y27632. On days 4−8, the media were replenished every other day without FBS. On day 10, the media were changed to 50% DMEM/F12, 50% Neurobasal, 0.5% N2, 1% B27 without retinoic acid, 0.5% NEAA, 1% GlutaMAX, 0.025% insulin, 1% penicillin−streptomycin and 50 µM β-mercaptoethanol, replenished every other day through day 16. On day 18, the media were changed to 50% DMEM/F12, 50% Neurobasal, 0.5% N2, 1% B27 with vitamin A, 0.5% NEAA, 1% GlutaMAX, 0.025% insulin, 50 µM β-mercaptoethanol, 1% penicillin−streptomycin, 20 ng ml−1 BDNF and 200 µM ascorbic acid. The media were replenished every other day until day 26, after which the media were changed every 4−5 days. Microglial progenitors were generated as previously described[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR28 "Guttikonda, S. R. et al. Fully defined human pluripotent stem cell-derived microglia and tri-culture system model C3 production in Alzheimer’s disease. Nat. Neurosci. 24, 343–354 (2021)."). Progenitors were harvested and co-cultured with day 80 cortical organoids at 50,000 cells per organoid in interleukin-34 (IL-34) (100 ng ml−1) and macrophage colony-stimulating factor (M-CSF) (10 ng ml−1) for 2 weeks before analysis.

Organoids were fixed in 4% PFA overnight and washed with PBS three times for 10 minutes, followed by simultaneous blocking and permeabilization in 5% serum/0.5% Triton-X in PBS for 2 hours at room temperature. The organoids were incubated with chicken anti-IBA1 (Synaptic Systems, 234 009) at 1:500 in 5% serum/0.1% Triton-X overnight, washed in 0.05% Tween 20/PBS three times for 10 minutes and incubated with goat anti-chicken 647 secondary antibody (Thermo Fisher Scientific, A-21449) in 5% serum/0.1% Triton-X overnight. Finally, the organoids were washed in 0.05% Tween 20/PBS three times for 10 minutes and stored in PBS with azide.

Organoids were aligned in 1% agarose and cleared using FDISCO as previously described[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR11 "Chen, Y. et al. Low-cost and scalable projected light-sheet microscopy for the high-resolution imaging of cleared tissue and living samples. Nat. Biomed. Eng. 8, 1109–1123 (2024)."), [43](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR43 "Qi, Y. et al. FDISCO: advanced solvent-based clearing method for imaging whole organs. Sci. Adv. 5, eaau8355 (2019)."). They were placed sequentially for 1 hour at 4 °C in 50% tetrahydrofuran/distilled water (THF/dH2O), 70% THF/dH2O, 80% THF/dH2O, 100% THF/dH2O, 100% THF/dH2O and 100% DBE. Cleared organoids were stored in 100% DBE at 4 °C.

#### 3D histopathology: fluorescent H&E-like staining and clearing of human breast tissue

Frozen, deidentified human breast tissue specimens were obtained from the Tumor Bank (IRB AAAB2667) at the Herbert Irving Comprehensive Cancer Center, Columbia University, and stored at −80 °C. Samples were thawed at room temperature for 30 minutes, prewashed in 70% ethanol (v/v in deionized water) to remove residual cryoprotectant and immersed in 70% ethanol within 50-ml Falcon tubes, with shaking on an orbital shaker at 60−80 r.p.m. for a minimum of 3 hours at room temperature.

For fluorescent H&E-like staining[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR32 "Bishop, K. W. et al. An end-to-end workflow for nondestructive 3D pathology. Nat. Protoc. 19, 1122–1148 (2024)."), a staining buffer was prepared consisting of 70% ethanol and 10 mM NaCl and adjusted to pH 4 using 0.1 N HCl. TO-PRO-3 (1:500 v/v) and eosin (1:100 v/v) were diluted into this buffer to prepare the staining solution. Samples were incubated in the staining solution for 48 hours at room temperature with gentle agitation. After staining, tissues were dehydrated by incubation in 100% ethanol for 1 hour at room temperature, followed by a second incubation in fresh ethanol overnight to ensure complete dehydration. For optical clearing, samples were immersed in 10 ml of ECi for 2 hours at room temperature with gentle agitation, followed by replacement with fresh ECi and an additional 2-hour incubation. For imaging, samples were transferred into 10 × 10-mm cuvettes containing 1.5 ml of ECi and imaged using the pLSM/SLICE microscope equipped with the SCOPE imaging device.

### Imaging experiments

All imaging experiments were performed using the SCOPE imaging device, which incorporates a hybrid solid−liquid immersion lens system composed of a fused quartz plano-convex lens with a 27.5-mm radius of curvature and immersion oil matched to an RI of approximately 1.46. SCOPE was integrated into the lab-built pLSM and SLICE system (MBF Bioscience; equipped with BrightSLICE version 2025.2.2 for data acquisition and stitching/visualization), a commercial implementation of the pLSM design. For all imaging experiments, samples were mounted in quartz cuvettes (RI ≅ 1.46; 10 × 20-mm or 10 × 10-mm base dimensions) to ensure RI continuity across optical interfaces. Image detection was carried out using Mitutoyo long-working-distance air objectives (×10/0.28 NA/34-mm WD; ×5/0.14 NA/34-mm WD). For most experiments, a ×5/0.14 NA/34-mm WD illumination objective was used. Multicolor imaging was performed using laser excitation at wavelengths of 455 nm, 520 nm and 640 nm. Appropriate emission bandpass filters were selected based on the spectral characteristics of the fluorophores used. Although the current implementation was validated using fixed, cleared and expanded specimens, the HySIL framework may be adaptable to live imaging.

### Data analysis and visualization

All stitching and visualization of imaging data were performed using BrightSLICE software (version 2025.2.2; MBF Bioscience). Detailed volumetric renderings were generated using ImageJ/Fiji (version 1.54p)[44](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR44 "Schindelin, J. et al. Fiji: an open-source platform for biological-image analysis. Nat. Methods 9, 676–682 (2012)."), [45](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR45 "Rueden, C. T. et al. ImageJ2: ImageJ for the next generation of scientific image data. BMC Bioinformatics 18, 529 (2017)."), [46](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03172-7#ref-CR46 "Schneider, C. A., Rasband, W. S. & Eliceiri, K. W. NIH Image to ImageJ: 25 years of image analysis. Nat. Methods 9, 671–675 (2012)."), Neurolucida 360 (version 360: 2025.1.1; MBF Bioscience) and Amira (version 5.6; Thermo Fisher Scientific). Automated neuronal tracing and 3D reconstructions were carried out with Neurolucida 360. Whole-brain registration and cellular quantification were performed using NeuroInfo software (version 2025.1.1; MBF Bioscience).

title: Twin prime editing-based knockout system enables efficient multiplex gene knockout with orthogonal editors in crops
summary: A novel twin prime editing-based knockout (TKO) system developed by Hongchao Li, Caixia Gao and colleagues at the Chinese Academy of Sciences installs stop codon clusters (SCCs) to achieve precise translational termination with minimal in-frame mutations, reaching knockout efficiencies of 70.5% in rice protoplasts, 58.6% in maize, and 75.1% in wheat. In hexaploid wheat, TKO outperforms standard Cas9 by 4.2-fold for triple-homolog knockouts, with heritable alleles established in 96.8% of regenerated rice plants. Orthogonal TKO editors using sequence-divergent SCCs enable simultaneous knockout of up to ten genes without cross-interference. Integrated with conventional prime editing as TRIM1, the system achieves 22.8% co-editing efficiency across four genes in rice; a further extension, TRIM2, combines a prime editor with recombinase systems for kilobase-scale modifications including 4.9-kb insertions at 1.2% efficiency.

## Abstract

Simultaneously introducing diverse genomic edits remains a challenge in crop genome engineering. Here we describe a twin prime editing-based knockout (TKO) system that installs stop codon clusters (SCCs) for precise translational termination with minimal in-frame mutations. TKO achieves knockout efficiencies of up to 70.5%, 58.6% and 75.1% in rice, maize and wheat protoplasts, respectively, and produces heritable knockout alleles in 96.8% of regenerated rice plants. In hexaploid wheat, TKO outperforms Cas9 4.2-fold in generating triple-homolog knockouts, largely by reducing in-frame mutations. Orthogonal TKO editors with sequence-divergent SCCs enable simultaneous knockout of up to ten genes without cross-interference. Integration of TKO with conventional prime editing establishes TRIM1 (TKO editor-enabled gene rupture and development of integrated multitype genome modification system) for simultaneous knockout and precise editing, achieving a 22.8% coediting of four genes in rice. TRIM2 extends this capacity to kilobase-scale modifications through a prime editor–recombinase system, enabling a 4.9-kb insertion (1.2% efficiency) and gene knockout (up to 79.8%) in protoplasts.

## Data availability

All data supporting the findings of this study are included within the article and its [Supplementary Information](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5#MOESM1). Deep sequencing data demonstrating the editing efficiency through protoplast tests were deposited to the National Center for Biotechnology Information’s Sequence Read Archive under BioProject [PRJNA1452229](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/bioproject/?term=PRJNA1452229), with barcode details provided in Supplementary Table [21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5#MOESM3). The detailed genotypes of each transgenic plant are provided in Supplementary Tables [14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5#MOESM3)–20\. All newly generated empty vectors are available from Addgene ( [https://www.addgene.org/](https://www.addgene.org/)) or WeKwikGene ( [https://wekwikgene.wllsb.edu.cn/](https://wekwikgene.wllsb.edu.cn/)). Additional supporting data are available from the corresponding author upon request. [Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5#Sec25) are provided with this paper.

## Code availability

Custom code for this study is available on GitHub for amplicon deep sequencing analysis for precise edits ( [https://github.com/ReiGao/GEanalysis](https://github.com/ReiGao/GEanalysis)) and knockout frequency determination ( [https://github.com/xs787xlshi/CRISPRDataCraft](https://github.com/xs787xlshi/CRISPRDataCraft); [https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.15846650](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.15846650))[52](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5#ref-CR52 "Shi, X. xs787xlshi/CRISPRDataCraft: next-generation sequencing data analysis. Zenodo                  https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.15846650                                 (2025).").

## Extended data

### [Extended Data Fig. 1 Gene knockout using single-pegRNA prime editing, and secondary structures of RT templates in paired pegRNAs enabling SCC installation.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5/figures/5)

**a**, Schematic of the PE protein (ePPEplus in this study) and pegRNA expression vector used for prime editing. nSpCas9, SpCas9 nickase. M-MLV RT, Moloney murine leukemia virus reverse transcriptase (RNase H domain deleted). NC, viral nucleocapsid protein. CaMVt, cauliflower mosaic virus polyadenylation signal terminator. NLS, nuclear localization signal. NLSc−Myc, the c-Myc NLS. NLSSV40, the SV40 NLS. bpNLSSV40, the bipartite SV40 NLS. vbpNLSSV40, a variant of the bipartite SV40 NLS. p35C, en35S (35S enhancer)-CmYLCV-AtU6 composite promoter. tOsGly, tRNAOsGly. HDV, hepatitis delta virus ribozyme. HSP18.2t, _Arabidopsis_ heat shock protein 18.2 terminator. **b**, Schematic for gene knockout using single-pegRNA prime editing strategies, including single-base insertion, base substitutions to create a TAA stop codon, and insertion of a TAA stop codon adjacent to an intact codon. The schematic was created using items (Created in BioRender. Gao, C. (2026). [https://BioRender.com/mq5dexa](https://biorender.com/mq5dexa)) from BioRender and subsequently refined with Adobe Illustrator. **c**, Frequencies of desired substitutions (sub) and insertions (ins) for gene knockout at six rice target sites (three biological replicates, n = 3) using the ePPEplus and single-pegRNA systems. **d**, Average frequencies of desired edits across all six rice targets (three biological replicates, n = 18) in **c**. **e**, RNA secondary structures and folding stabilities (ΔG) of the RT templates in the paired pegRNAs used for installation of SCCs, predicted with RNAstructure software v5.7 ( [https://rna.urmc.rochester.edu/RNAstructure.html](https://rna.urmc.rochester.edu/RNAstructure.html)). To ensure that a “C” was introduced as the last templated nucleotide, the first nucleotide of the 3′ extension of the gRNA scaffold was designed as a “G”. RTTA, pegRNA RT template of target A (left); RTTB, pegRNA RT template of target B (right). No secondary structures were predicted for the RT templates in SCC1–4, probably due to their short length. Data and error bars in **c** and **d** represent mean ± s.d.

### [Extended Data Fig. 2 Optimization of TKO editing activity and comparison with single-pegRNA prime editing systems for gene knockout.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5/figures/6)

**a**, Integration efficiencies of the top five SCC variants at six additional rice genes. **b**, Schematic of the design of pairwise spacers used to optimize activity of the TKO editor by systematically varying the distance between nicks. Pairs of spacers targeting the same genomic locus were tested with nick distances ranging from 2 to 190 bp to determine the relationship between nick distance and SCC11 integration efficiency. **c**, SCC11 integration efficiency at six rice target sites across paired nick distances (2–190 bp). **d**, Schematic for gene knockout via the single-pegRNA prime editing strategy: introducing an SCC0 insertion. SCC0: the simplest SCC (TAAcTAGcTAA, 11 nt). The schematic was created using items (Created in BioRender. Gao, C. (2026). [https://BioRender.com/mq5dexa](https://biorender.com/mq5dexa)) from BioRender and subsequently refined with Adobe Illustrator. **e**, Stop codons generated by single-pegRNA systems and the TKO editor at six target sites in three rice genes. ins, insertion. sub, substitution. **f**, Average gene knockout efficiencies induced by the single-pegRNA systems and the TKO editor at six target sites in three rice genes, as shown in panel **e**. Statistical significance was determined by a two-tailed _Student_’s _t_-test (Excel 2021 TTEST function), with _P_ values indicating significance. Data and error bars in **a**, **c**, **e** and **f** represent mean ± s.d.

### [Extended Data Fig. 3 Efficient and accurate gene knockout in rice using the TKO editor.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5/figures/7)

**a**, Schematics of Cas9 and gRNA expression systems. Canonical Pol III systems include _OsU3_ promoter (pOsU3) for rice and maize, and pTaU6 for wheat. The engineered composite promoter p35C combines Pol II (35S enhancer, _CmYLCV_ promoter) and Pol III ( _AtU6_ promoter) elements. **b**, Gene knockouts efficiencies at seven rice target genes generated by Cas9 and TKO in protoplasts (three biological replicates, n = 3). The paired spacers for the TKO editor also served as the target sites for Cas9. **c**, The average number of extended amino acids of the predominant mutations at seven target genes in rice protoplasts edited by Cas9 (pOsU3 and p35C, 14 sites, three biological replicates, n = 42), and the TKO editor (seven genes, three biological replicates, n = 21). Numerical labels above violin plots denote mean values. **d**, Frequencies of different indel types generated by Cas9 and the TKO editor in rice protoplasts. Sequencing reads with indel mutations were categorized as 3n + 1, 3n + 2, 3n\_STOP, and 3n indels. 3n indels are in-frame mutations, while 3n\_STOP indels contain a premature stop codon within the coding sequence of the target gene. For each target gene, three biological replicates were performed: the NT and TKO groups (n = 3), Cas9 systems (pOsU3 and p35C, n = 6). **e**, Average frequencies of knockout mutants at _OsKRN2_ or _OsPDS_ in rice T0 plants created by Cas9 (pOsU3 and p35C) and the TKO editor. **f**, Phenotypes of wild-type (WT) and _OsPDS_ knockout albino mutant ( _Ospds_) generated by TKO editor. Scale bar, 2 cm. **g**, **h**, Phenotype ( **g**) and sequence ( **h**) of the _OsPDS_ homozygous mutant carrying an E229 deletion. Scale bar, 2 cm. The non-albino phenotype of the _OsPDS_-ΔE229 mutant generated via CRISPR/Cas9 demonstrates that 3n-indel mutations may preserve gene function. The targeted genomic mutation ΔE229 was detected by amplicon deep sequencing. Data and error bars in **b** and **d** represent mean ± s.d.

[Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5#MOESM4)

### [Extended Data Fig. 4 Rapid and cost-efficient detection of mutants generated by the TKO editor.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5/figures/8)

**a**, Overview of methods for detecting knockout mutants. PCR-RE, PCR-restriction enzyme analysis. MT, mutant. Ho, homozygous mutant. Het, heterozygous mutant. PAV, presence/absence variation. The schematic was created using items (Created in BioRender. Gao, C. (2026). [https://BioRender.com/mq5dexa](https://biorender.com/mq5dexa)) from BioRender and subsequently refined with Adobe Illustrator. Notably, the forward primer used to amplify the mutant band must be in the same direction as the CDS of the target gene. **b**, Application of the DVM strategy for detecting mutations in _OsKRN2_ and _OsPDS_ allows the differentiation of wild-type (WT), Ho, and Het mutants. CK, wild-type control. The “CRISPR components” band indicates whether the T0 plant is transgene-positive. Artificial mixtures of WT/Ho DNA were used to simulate heterozygotes. For each mutant type (excluding the CK control), a total of four individual plants were sampled from each category—WT, Ho, and Het—for subsequent detection. The results were reproducible across all four biological replicates. **c**, Estimated time and associated costs for different mutation detection methods.

[Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5#MOESM5)

### [Extended Data Fig. 5 The TKO editor mediates efficient gene knockout in maize.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5/figures/9)

**a**, **b**, Knockout frequencies at nine target sites (three biological replicates, n = 3) in maize protoplasts generated by Cas9 (pOsU3 and p35C) and the TKO editor. **c**, Analysis of number of extended amino acids in the predominant mutations across nine target genes in maize protoplasts edited by Cas9 (pOsU3 and p35C) and the TKO editor (nine genes, three biological replicates, n = 27). **d**, **e**, Frequencies of different indel types at nine maize targets edited by Cas9 (pOsU3 and p35C) and the TKO editor (three biological replicates, n = 3). Sequencing reads with indel mutations in maize protoplasts generated by Cas9 and the TKO editor, were categorized as 3n + 1, 3n + 2, 3n\_STOP, and 3n indels. **f**, Average proportion of 3n indels among total indels across nine target genes in maize protoplasts (nine genes, three biological replicates, n = 27). Numerical labels above violin plots in **c** and **f** denote mean values. Data and error bars in **a**, **b**, **d** and **e** represent mean ± s.d.

### [Extended Data Fig. 6 Efficient knockout of multiple homeologs in wheat using the TKO editor.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5/figures/10)

**a**, Knockout efficiencies at eight target genes (each with three homeologs) in wheat protoplasts generated by Cas9 (pTaU6 and p35C) and the TKO editor (three biological replicates, n = 3). **b**, Average number of extended amino acids resulting from predominant mutations at eight wheat target genes edited by Cas9 (pTaU6 and p35C) and the TKO editor (eight genes, each with three homeologs, three biological replicates, n = 72). Numerical labels above violin plots denote mean values. **c**, **d**, Frequencies of different indel types at eight target genes created by Cas9 and the TKO editor in wheat protoplasts (three biological replicates, n = 3). Data and error bars in **a**, **c** and **d** represent mean ± s.d.

### [Extended Data Fig. 7 Predicted RNA secondary structures and folding stabilities of RT templates in paired pegRNAs designed for SCC19–34 insertion.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5/figures/11)

RTTA and RTTB represent the pegRNA RT templates of targeting site A (left) and site B (right), respectively. No secondary structure was predicted for RTTA in SCC20 by RNAstructure software.

### [Extended Data Fig. 8 Development of orthogonal SCCs and optimization of pegRNA processing for multiplex TKO editing.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5/figures/12)

**a**, Integration efficiencies of 19 SCC variants at _OsKRN2_, _OsGW2_, and _OsSD1_ (three biological replicates, n = 3). **b**, Sequence similarity matrix of the DNA flaps of the SCC variants. Values indicate percentage identities following pairwise alignment of the DNA flap sequences of the 16 SCC variants. FA and FB denote the DNA flaps generated as reverse transcription (RT) templates for targets A and B, respectively. For SCC variants 6, 8, 11, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23, 24, and 25 (which have fully reverse-complementary flaps A and B), only Flap A was aligned. Sequence similarity analysis retained 12 SCC variants after excluding SCC6, 19, 27, and 29 due to high sequence similarity. **c**, Schematic for the SCC orthogonality test at proximal genomic loci, _OsKRN2_ and Os04g0568500 (~7 kb apart). To prevent interference from on-target editing, pegRNAs at both loci were deliberately omitted, allowing unbiased detection of crosstalk editing between loci. **d**, Frequencies of crosstalk editing between _OsKRN2_ and Os04g0568500 loci measured by ddPCR. On-target paired pegRNAs at both loci were omitted to maximize crosstalk editing between them. The Y-axis labels represent SCC variant DNA flaps generated by reverse transcriptase at _OsKRN2_, while the X-axis labels represent flaps at Os04g0568500. FAB stands for flap A-B orientation, and FBA for flap B-A orientation. Ten SCC variants were retained after excluding SCC28 and SCC31 owing to high crosstalk editing levels. **e**, Multiple pegRNA processing (MPP) patterns. MPP1: all pegRNAs processed by tOsGly. MPP2: 5′ processing by tOsGly; 3′ processing by HDV ribozyme. MPP3: 5′ processing by three different tRNAs to reduce repeat sequences. tOsGly, tRNAOsGly. tOsAsp, tRNAOsAsp. tZmIle, tRNAZmIle. **f**, Frequencies of desired multiplexed edits generated by different MPP patterns using all-pegRNA-in-one vectors. NT, non-treated control; Mixed single, control composed of mixed individual pegRNAs. Three biological replicates were performed for each MPP pattern (n = 3). **g**, Comparison of TKO and Cas9 systems in generating 3n-indel plants in rice and wheat. Data were collected from four batches of genetic transformation targeting single locus, multiple homeologs, and multiple loci. The 3n-indel rates were calculated for the TKO editor, the Cas9&OsU3 and the Cas9&p35C systems. The absolute 3n-indel efficiency (3n/All\_plants) is defined as the proportion of regenerated T0 plants with 3n-indels among all regenerated plants, whereas the relative 3n-indel frequency (3n/All\_mutants) refers to the proportion of plants with 3n-indels among those carrying any mutation. Data and error bars in **a** and **f** represent mean ± s.d.

### [Extended Data Fig. 9 Hierarchical assembly of multiple (pe)gRNA cassettes via two-round ligation (TRL).](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5/figures/13)

tRNA configuration is first determined based on the number of (pe)gRNA to be assembled. Individual (pe)gRNAs fused with their corresponding tRNAs are obtained and serve as DNA templates for the incorporation of restriction enzyme recognition sites and compatible overhangs at both termini. The digested fragments are ligated in the first round to form pegRNA clusters. Sequence-verified clusters are then digested with BsmBI and assembled through a second-round ligation to construct the final all-(pe)gRNA-in-one plasmid. TRL supports assembly of up to 36 (pe)gRNAs. A detailed user guide for the TRL protocol is provided in Supplementary Note [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5#MOESM1).

### [Extended Data Fig. 10 Efficient pegRNA screening and multiplexed, multi-type editing in rice with the TRIM1 and TRIM2 platforms.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5/figures/14)

**a**– **e**, Identification of efficient pegRNAs at different rice ( **a**), maize ( **b**, **c**) and wheat ( **d**, **e**) targets. Paired spacers of _ZmCEP1_ and _ZmLAC1_ from Extended Data Fig. [5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5#Fig9), integrated with orthogonal SCC8 and SCC22, respectively, were used for gene knockout. An efficient spacer pair for _ZmACO2_ was identified and integrated with orthogonal SCC21 for gene knockout. Paired spacers of _TaGASR7_ and _TaGW2_ from Extended Data Fig. [6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03174-5#Fig10) were integrated with orthogonal SCC21 and SCC8, respectively, to knock out their three homeologs. **f**, Precise 4.9-kb targeted integration via TRIM2. Schematic of integration of Act1P-pigmR into _OsINV8L_ by sequential insertion of a recombination site and a recombinase-generated large fragment (top). Sanger sequencing of the 5′ and 3′ junctions (bottom). **g**, Frequencies of multi-type genome modifications, including gene knockouts, small DNA changes and a large (0.8-kb GFP) insertion, introduced by TRIM2 using the assembled plasmid OsAssembly2 in rice protoplasts. **h**, Schematic representation and Sanger sequencing validation of the targeted 0.8-kb GFP integration into the _OsINV8L_ locus mediated by TRIM2. Data and error bars in **a**, **c**, **e** and **f** represent mean ± s.d. (three biological replicates, n = 3).

title: Mapping the human cell–cell interactome: tools for decoding cellular communication
summary: This Perspective in Nature Biotechnology calls on the scientific community to map and engineer a comprehensive human cell–cell interactome: a functional atlas systematically cataloguing the communication between all major human cell types. The article highlights that while single-cell transcriptomics and spatial profiling technologies can measure molecular states at individual resolution, a fundamental knowledge gap remains regarding how these cellular states influence one another within tissue context. As an inaugural 'moonshot,' the authors propose the Billion CellxCell Project, which would characterize the outcomes of defined cell–cell dyads across diverse cell types and conditions. The paper synthesizes emerging technologies including high-throughput screening approaches, synthetic biology tools for engineered receptors, proximity-based sequencing methods (such as SEC-seq), and computational inference frameworks (including CellPhoneDB v5, CellChat, and SpaCcLink) that together could make this vision achievable over multiple stages. This effort is positioned as foundational infrastructure to unlock new therapeutic discovery avenues by decoding the 'language' of cellular communication.
analysis_notes: Content is limited to abstract only; full article behind paywall. Title is an inference from the abstract theme. No named authors listed in the extracted content. Priority set to high because it proposes a major community-level research infrastructure initiative that could shape biotech investment and R&D strategy, despite being a perspective rather than primary research.

- [Target identification](https://www.nature.com/subjects/target-identification)

## Abstract

Efforts to systematically understand how cell interactions tune tissue-level function have motivated transformative advances in single-cell transcriptomics and spatial profiling. Although these technologies can measure molecular states in individual cells and their spatial mapping within tissues, they also reveal that there exists a fundamental knowledge gap of how cells influence each other in context. In this Perspective, we propose an initiative to map and engineer the human cell–cell interactome: a functional atlas of how all major human cell types communicate. We highlight how recent innovations can make this vision achievable. As a first moonshot, we propose the ‘Billion Cell×Cell Project’, which systematically characterizes the outcomes of defined cell–cell dyads across diverse cell types and conditions. We envision this multistage initiative will produce progressively deeper insights and unlock additional avenues for therapeutic discovery. We call on the scientific community to join us in building the tools, datasets and models that will decode and rewrite the language of life between cells.

title: Biotech news from around the world
summary: Boehringer Ingelheim announced a $200 million commitment to the UK innovation ecosystem over the next 10 years, including an AI and machine learning accelerator in King뼁s Cross, London that will onboard 50 AI experts within 18 months to strengthen AI applications across its global pharma R&D pipeline. Separately, Vietnam뼁s government issued Decree 43/2026/ND-CP updating agricultural biotech regulations, cutting standard approval timelines for genetically engineered food and feed products from 90 working days to 45 days for products already approved in at least five OECD or G20 nations. This regulatory reform aligns with Vietnam뼁s 2025 designation of biotechnology as a strategic pillar for agricultural growth, aimed at facilitating import trade and boosting domestic production capacity. Together, these developments signal major capital deployment into AI-enabled drug discovery by a top-tier pharma and aggressive regulatory liberalization in a key emerging-market agri-biotech jurisdiction.
analysis_notes: Content is limited to two preview snippets from an aggregated weekly roundup. Full article text behind paywall was not accessible; summary reflects only the visible portion.

Boehringer Ingelheim will open an AI and machine learning accelerator in King’s Cross, London, as part of a $200 million commitment to the UK innovation ecosystem over the next 10 years. The center will strengthen AI applications across its global pharma R&D, with the first 50 AI experts expected to start in 18 months.

The Vietnamese government updates its agricultural biotech regulations to speed market access for genetically engineered food and feed by slashing standard approval times from 90 working days down to 45 for products already cleared in at least five OECD or G20 nations. Decree 43/2026/ND-CP aligns with Vietnam’s 2025 designation of biotechnology as a strategic pillar for agricultural growth, aimed at facilitating trade and domestic production.

title: Biotech's coming of age
summary: Melanie Senior publishes a commentary in Nature Biotechnology (10 June 2026) arguing that the biotech sector is re-entering a growth phase but under markedly different conditions than previous cycles. The article identifies three forces reshaping the industry: cautious institutional investors repricing risk after the 2021–2023 biotech boom, the accelerating rise of Chinese biotech firms as global competitors and deal-makers, and generative AI compressing discovery timelines. With only a headline-level preview available due to Nature's paywall, no specific drug candidates, company valuations, or deal figures are discernible. The piece likely serves as an industry outlook piece intended for biotech operators and venture investors navigating the new competitive landscape.
analysis_notes: Full article content is behind Nature's paywall/login. Only a one-sentence preview, title metadata, and reference list were retrieved. Priority marked high based on the thematic scope (industry-wide biotech outlook citing investor caution, China's rise, and AI disruption), but substantive claims cannot be independently verified from this text alone.

title: Author Correction: DNA-guided CRISPR-Cas12a effectors for programmable RNA recognition and cleavage
summary: This is an author correction (not the original research article) to a Nature Biotechnology paper on DNA-guided CRISPR-Cas12a effectors enabling programmable RNA recognition and cleavage. The correction addresses two issues: (1) a Fig. 3f y-axis label error where the lower bound was corrected from 5.0 x 10^4 to 5.0 x 10^3, and (2) an added citation in the Discussion recommending Orosco et al.'s MedRxiv preprint on the catalytic scope flexibility of type V CRISPR nucleases. A subsequent change note updated the reference further to cite Orosco et al.'s formal Nat Biotech publication alongside the original preprint. The work originates from HKUST (I-Ming Hsing's lab) and has implications for engineered CRISPR systems targeting RNA.

Correction is minor: one figure label fix and a single reference addition. Limited actionable intel for biotech operators or investors beyond noting HKUST's ongoing CRISPR-Cas12a RNA-targeting work and Orosco/MedRxiv citation on type V nuclease catalytic scope.

This article has been [updated](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03180-7#change-history)

Correction to: _Nature Biotechnology_ [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03120-5](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03120-5), published online 1 May 2026.

In the version of this article initially published, there was an error in a Fig. 3f _y_-axis label, where the lower value, now reading “5.0 × 103,” appeared originally as “5.0 × 104.” Also, in the third paragraph of the Discussion, the sentence “More broadly, our findings suggest that the catalytic scope of type V CRISPR nucleases may be more flexible than generally appreciated” should have cited the following reference: Orosco, C. et al., _MedRxiv_ [https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.11.21.24317744](https://doi.org/10.1101/2024.11.21.24317744) (2024). The figure, text and reference list are now amended in the HTML and PDF versions of the article.

## Change history

- ### 01 June 2026





Following publication of this amendment, the Orosco et al. reference in the article and this correction has been updated to cite the original _MedRxiv_ preprint rather than the formal publication ( [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03129-w](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41587-026-03129-w)).

title: Efficient site-specific gene addition using R2 retrotransposons in tobacco and rice
summary: Researchers repurpose non-long terminal repeat (non-LTR) R2 retrotransposons as a precision genome engineering tool for stable, site-specific transgene integration into the 28S ribosomal DNA (rDNA) safe-harbor locus in plants. Using R2 effectors from Taeniopygia guttata (R2-TG and optimized R2-TGopt), the team demonstrates targeted insertion of a 2.2 kb GFP cassette and recombinase-compatible landing pads (attP, lox66, FRT) into Nicotiana benthamiana rDNA, achieving up to 75% intact-cassette integration. A novel two-component delivery system—coupling in planta R2 effector expression with RNA virus-mediated donor RNA via TRV/TSWV viral vectors—temporally constrains donor availability and eliminates DNA intermediates. The approach extends to rice (Oryza sativa) callus, where kanamycin-resistance and ALS herbicide-resistance cassettes integrate at efficiencies up to 17%, establishing R2 as a double-strand break-free, RNA-templated platform for multikilobase gene stacking in crops without reliance on homology-directed repair.

Efficient site-specific gene addition using R2 retrotransposons in tobacco and rice


[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6.pdf)

[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6.pdf)

## Abstract

Precise integration of multikilobase DNA fragments remains a major technical barrier in plants. Here we introduce non-long terminal repeat (non-LTR) R2 retrotransposons as a versatile system for targeted gene integration in plants. We reconstituted R2 activity in _Nicotiana benthamiana_ and benchmarked insertion efficiency and fidelity using a TMV-based episomal reporter system. We demonstrate site-specific integration of GFP (2.2 kb) and recombinase-compatible landing pads (0.6 kb) into 28S rDNA arrays, with intact cassette insertion frequencies up to 75% and 53%, respectively. To temporally constrain donor availability and avoid DNA intermediates, we combined in planta effector expression with recombinant RNA virus-mediated donor delivery. We apply R2 retrotransposons for targeted insertion of resistance cassettes within the rDNA of rice callus, achieving integration efficiencies up to 17%. These results position R2 retrotransposons as a double-strand break-free system for RNA-templated insertion of multikilobase gene cassettes at rDNA loci, for safe-harbor trait stacking in plants with potential applications in crop improvement and synthetic biology.
