### [Transposase-assisted target-site integration for efficient plant genome engineering](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-024-07613-8?fromPaywallRec=false)

ArticleOpen access26 June 2024

## Main

Precision gene addition is a central objective in plant biotechnology, underpinning efforts to engineer crops with enhanced agronomic traits, nutritional value and biosynthetic capabilities[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR1 "Zhang, Y., Massel, K., Godwin, I. D. & Gao, C. Applications and potential of genome editing in crop improvement. Genome Biol. 19, 210 (2018)."), [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR2 "Ahmar, S., Hensel, G. & Gruszka, D. CRISPR/Cas9-mediated genome editing techniques and new breeding strategies in cereals: current status, improvements, and perspectives. Biotechnol. Adv. 69, 108248 (2023)."), [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR3 "Gilbertson, L., Puchta, H. & Slotkin, R. K. The future of genome editing in plants. Nat. Plants 11, 680–685 (2025)."). However, most transgenic approaches rely on random integration of T-DNA, often resulting in gene silencing[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR4 "Gao, Y. & Zhao, Y. Epigenetic suppression of T-DNA insertion mutants in Arabidopsis. Mol. Plant 6, 539–545 (2013)."), variable expression[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR5 "Schubert, D. et al. Silencing in Arabidopsis T-DNA transformants: The predominant role of a gene-specific RNA sensing mechanism versus position effects. Plant Cell 16, 2561–2572 (2004)."), [6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR6 "Daxinger, L. et al. Unexpected silencing effects from T-DNA tags in Arabidopsis. Trends Plant Sci. 13, 4–6 (2008)."), [7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR7 "Kirchhoff, J. et al. Gene expression variability between randomly and targeted transgene integration events in tobacco suspension cell lines. Plant Biotechnol. Rep. 14, 451–458 (2020)."), multicopy insertions[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR8 "Olhoft, P. M., Flagel, L. E. & Somers, D. A. T-DNA locus structure in a large population of soybean plants transformed using the Agrobacterium-mediated cotyledonary-node method. Plant Biotechnol. J. 2, 289–300 (2004)."), [9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR9 "Thomson, G., Dickinson, L. & Jacob, Y. Genomic consequences associated with Agrobacterium-mediated transformation of plants. Plant J. 117, 342–363 (2024)."), gene disruption and the need for selectable markers[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR9 "Thomson, G., Dickinson, L. & Jacob, Y. Genomic consequences associated with Agrobacterium-mediated transformation of plants. Plant J. 117, 342–363 (2024)."). These limitations have driven the development of targeted integration strategies that enable site-specific, stable transgene insertion at defined genomic loci[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR10 "Petolino, J. F. & Kumar, S. Transgenic trait deployment using designed nucleases. Plant Biotechnol. J. 14, 503–509 (2016)."), [11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR11 "Dong, O. X. & Ronald, P. C. Targeted DNA insertion in plants. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 118, e2004834117 (2021)."), [12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR12 "Tuncel, A. et al. Genome-edited foods. Nat. Rev. Bioeng. 1, 799–816 (2023).").

CRISPR-Cas systems have transformed genome editing by enabling programmable site-specific double-strand breaks (DSBs), facilitating gene knockouts via non-homologous end joining (NHEJ), and supporting precise gene insertion via homology-directed repair (HDR)[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR13 "Přibylová, A. & Fischer, L. How to use CRISPR/Cas9 in plants: from target site selection to DNA repair. J. Exp. Bot. 75, 5325–5343 (2024)."), [14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR14 "Schreiber, T. et al. Efficient scar-free knock-ins of several kilobases in plants by engineered CRISPR-Cas endonucleases. Mol. Plant 17, 824–837 (2024)."). However, HDR remains inefficient in plants, particularly for multikilobase sequence insertions[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR15 "Chen, J., Li, S., He, Y., Li, J. & Xia, L. An update on precision genome editing by homology-directed repair in plants. Plant Physiol. 188, 1780–1794 (2022)."). Prime editing and RNA-guided integrases offer alternatives for short- to medium-length DNA insertions but are constrained by limited cargo capacity and, in some implementations, reliance on additional genome-modifying effectors[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR16 "Zong, Y. et al. An engineered prime editor with enhanced editing efficiency in plants. Nat. Biotechnol. 40, 1394–1402 (2022)."), [17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR17 "Pandey, S. et al. Efficient site-specific integration of large genes in mammalian cells via continuously evolved recombinases and prime editing. Nat. Biomed. Eng. 9, 22–39 (2025)."). Precise gene integration has also been achieved through gene targeting via homologous recombination (HR). Despite efficiency gains from nuclease-induced DSBs, gene targeting remains limited by reliance on endogenous repair pathways, variable repair outcomes, reduced efficiency for large inserts and the risk of unintended genomic rearrangements, particularly in complex or repetitive plant genomes[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR18 "Puchta, H., Dujon, B. & Hohn, B. Homologous recombination in plant cells is enhanced by in vivo induction of double strand breaks into DNA by a site-specific endonuclease. Nucleic Acids Res. 21, 5034–5040 (1993)."), [19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR19 "Puchta, H. & Fauser, F. Gene targeting in plants: 25 years later. Int. J. Dev. Biol. 57, 629–637 (2013)."), [20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR20 "Van Vu, T. et al. Challenges and perspectives in homology-directed gene targeting in monocot plants. Rice 12, 95 (2019)."). More recently, DNA transposase-based systems, including Pong, have been adapted for targeted insertion at user-defined genomic loci in plants. In practice, however, efficient targeting has frequently required pre-engineered sites in the genome, limiting the general applicability of these approaches[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR21 "Liu, P. et al. Transposase-assisted target-site integration for efficient plant genome engineering. Nature 631, 593–600 (2024)."). Recombinase-mediated systems, including Cre/lox and Bxb1, enable efficient gene stacking but depend on preinstalled recombination sites, introduce recombination scars and are not broadly applicable across diverse species or genomic contexts[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR22 "Li, R. et al. Method for biolistic site-specific integration in plants catalyzed by Bxb1 integrase. Methods Mol. Biol. 1469, 15–30 (2016)."), [23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR23 "McCormick, S. Recombinases and rhizogenes for easy gene stacking. Plant J. 95, 571–572 (2018)."), [24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR24 "Sun, C. et al. Iterative recombinase technologies for efficient and precise genome engineering across kilobase to megabase scales. Cell 188, 4693–4710.e4615 (2025).").

A persistent bottleneck to scalable gene addition in plants is the limited availability of universally accessible ‘safe-harbor’ loci, genomic regions that support predictable transgene expression without disrupting endogenous gene function[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR25 "Dong, O. X. et al. Marker-free carotenoid-enriched rice generated through targeted gene insertion using CRISPR-Cas9. Nat. Commun. 11, 1178 (2020)."), [26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR26 "Sun, C. et al. Precise integration of large DNA sequences in plant genomes using PrimeRoot editors. Nat. Biotechnol. 42, 316–327 (2024)."). Ribosomal DNA (rDNA) arrays represent a compelling candidate in this context. In plants, the 18S, 5.8S and 25S rRNA genes (homologous to 28S rRNA in animals) are encoded within a single 45S rDNA transcriptional unit arranged as head-to-tail tandem repeats at specific chromosomal loci. These arrays occur at high copy number, varying across species, for example, ~1,022 in _Oryza sativa_ [27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR27 "Shang, L. et al. A complete assembly of the rice Nipponbare reference genome. Mol. Plant 16, 1232–1236 (2023)."), ~6,650 in _Triticum aestivum_ [28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR28 "Tulpová, Z. et al. Fine structure and transcription dynamics of bread wheat ribosomal DNA loci deciphered by a multi-omics approach. Plant Genome 15, e20191 (2022)."), ~2,974 in _Zea mays_ [29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR29 "Chen, J. et al. A complete telomere-to-telomere assembly of the maize genome. Nat. Genet. 55, 1221–1231 (2023)."), ~800 in _Arabidopsis thaliana_ [30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR30 "Sims, J., Sestini, G., Elgert, C., von Haeseler, A. & Schlogelhofer, P. Sequencing of the Arabidopsis NOR2 reveals its distinct organization and tissue-specific rRNA ribosomal variants. Nat. Commun. 12, 387 (2021).") and ~1,247 in _Nicotiana benthamiana_ [31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR31 "Chen, W. et al. The complete genome assembly of Nicotiana benthamiana reveals the genetic and epigenetic landscape of centromeres. Nat. Plants 10, 1928–1943 (2024)."). rDNA loci are attractive safe-harbor targets due to their repetitive structure and strong transcriptional activity, yet no broadly adopted method enables efficient, site-specific integration in plants.

Transposable elements are mobile genomic sequences that play central roles in shaping genome structure, evolution and gene regulatory architecture across diverse organisms[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR32 "Zupunski, V., Gubensek, F. & Kordis, D. Evolutionary dynamics and evolutionary history in the RTE clade of non-LTR retrotransposons. Mol. Biol. Evol. 18, 1849–1863 (2001)."), [33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR33 "Fedoroff, N. V. Presidential address. Transposable elements, epigenetics, and genome evolution. Science 338, 758–767 (2012)."), [34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR34 "Wells, J. N. & Feschotte, C. A field guide to eukaryotic transposable elements. Annu. Rev. Genet. 54, 539–561 (2020)."), [35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR35 "Malik, H. S., Burke, W. D. & Eickbush, T. H. The age and evolution of non-LTR retrotransposable elements. Mol. Biol. Evol. 16, 793–805 (1999)."). Among these, retrotransposons constitute a major subclass that mobilize through RNA intermediates and integrate into new genomic locations via reverse transcription[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR34 "Wells, J. N. & Feschotte, C. A field guide to eukaryotic transposable elements. Annu. Rev. Genet. 54, 539–561 (2020)."), [36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR36 "Zhang, X. et al. Harnessing eukaryotic retroelement proteins for transgene insertion into human safe-harbor loci. Nat. Biotechnol. 43, 42–51 (2025)."), [37](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR37 "Thawani, A., Florez Ariza, A. J., Nogales, E. & Collins, K. Template and target-site recognition by human LINE-1 in retrotransposition. Nature 626, 186–193 (2024)."). Non-long terminal repeat (non-LTR) retrotransposons are distinguished by the absence of terminal repeats and their unique mechanism of target-primed reverse transcription (TPRT)[38](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR38 "Han, J. S. Non-long terminal repeat (non-LTR) retrotransposons: mechanisms, recent developments, and unanswered questions. Mob DNA 1, 15 (2010)."), [39](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR39 "Wilkinson, M. E., Frangieh, C. J., Macrae, R. K. & Zhang, F. Structure of the R2 non-LTR retrotransposon initiating target-primed reverse transcription. Science 380, 301–308 (2023)."), [40](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR40 "Luan, D. D., Korman, M. H., Jakubczak, J. L. & Eickbush, T. H. Reverse transcription of R2Bm RNA is primed by a nick at the chromosomal target site: a mechanism for non-LTR retrotransposition. Cell 72, 595–605 (1993)."). Over the past two decades, mechanistic studies have elucidated the molecular determinants governing non-LTRs target recognition and TPRT across species[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR36 "Zhang, X. et al. Harnessing eukaryotic retroelement proteins for transgene insertion into human safe-harbor loci. Nat. Biotechnol. 43, 42–51 (2025)."), [39](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR39 "Wilkinson, M. E., Frangieh, C. J., Macrae, R. K. & Zhang, F. Structure of the R2 non-LTR retrotransposon initiating target-primed reverse transcription. Science 380, 301–308 (2023)."), [41](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR41 "Eickbush, D. G., Luan, D. D. & Eickbush, T. H. Integration of Bombyx mori R2 sequences into the 28S ribosomal RNA genes of Drosophila melanogaster. Mol. Cell. Biol. 20, 213–223 (2000)."), [42](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR42 "Christensen, S. M., Ye, J. & Eickbush, T. H. RNA from the 5′ end of the R2 retrotransposon controls R2 protein binding to and cleavage of its DNA target site. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. 103, 17602–17607 (2006)."), [43](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR43 "Eickbush, T. H. & Eickbush, D. G. Integration, regulation, and long-term stability of R2 retrotransposons. Microbiol. Spectr. 3, Mdna3-0011-2014 (2015)."), [44](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR44 "Palm, S. M., Horton, C. A., Zhang, X. & Collins, K. Structure and sequence at an RNA template 5′ end influence insertion of transgenes by an R2 retrotransposon protein. RNA 30, 1227–1245 (2024)."). Notably, several members of non-LTR retrotransposons exhibit strict insertion preferences within rDNA: R1 (ref. [45](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR45 "Xiong, Y. & Eickbush, T. H. The site-specific ribosomal DNA insertion element R1Bm belongs to a class of non-long-terminal-repeat retrotransposons. Mol. Cell. Biol. 8, 114–123 (1988).")), R2 (ref. [46](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR46 "Kojima, K. K. & Fujiwara, H. Long-term inheritance of the 28S rDNA-specific retrotransposon R2. Mol. Biol. Evol.22, 2157–2165 (2005).")), R4 (ref. [47](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR47 "Burke, W. D., Muller, F. & Eickbush, T. H. R4, a non-LTR retrotransposon specific to the large subunit rRNA genes of nematodes. Nucleic Acids Res. 23, 4628–4634 (1995).")), R5 (ref. [48](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR48 "Burke, W. D., Singh, D. & Eickbush, T. H. R5 retrotransposons insert into a family of infrequently transcribed 28S rRNA genes of planaria. Mol. Biol. Evol. 20, 1260–1270 (2003).")), R6 (ref. [49](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR49 "Kojima, K. K. & Fujiwara, H. Evolution of target specificity in R1 clade non-LTR retrotransposons. Mol. Biol. Evol. 20, 351–361 (2003).")), R9 (ref. [50](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR50 "Gladyshev, E. A. & Arkhipova, I. R. Rotifer rDNA-specific R9 retrotransposable elements generate an exceptionally long target site duplication upon insertion. Gene 448, 145–150 (2009).")) and reverse transcriptase (RT)[51](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR51 "Besansky, N. J., Paskewitz, S. M., Hamm, D. M. & Collins, F. H. Distinct families of site-specific retrotransposons occupy identical positions in the rRNA genes of Anopheles gambiae. Mol. Cell. Biol. 12, 5102–5110 (1992).") insert specifically into 28S rDNA, whereas R7 (ref. [49](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR49 "Kojima, K. K. & Fujiwara, H. Evolution of target specificity in R1 clade non-LTR retrotransposons. Mol. Biol. Evol. 20, 351–361 (2003).")) and R8 (ref. [52](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR52 "Kojima, K. K., Kuma, K., Toh, H. & Fujiwara, H. Identification of rDNA-specific non-LTR retrotransposons in Cnidaria. Mol. Biol. Evol. 23, 1984–1993 (2006).")) target 18S rDNA.

Among these, R2 elements are well characterized and display exceptional site specificity, inserting into conserved sites within 28S rDNA across diverse taxa, including insects, mollusks, and vertebrates[41](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR41 "Eickbush, D. G., Luan, D. D. & Eickbush, T. H. Integration of Bombyx mori R2 sequences into the 28S ribosomal RNA genes of Drosophila melanogaster. Mol. Cell. Biol. 20, 213–223 (2000)."), [53](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR53 "Burke, W. D., Malik, H. S., Jones, J. P. & Eickbush, T. H. The domain structure and retrotransposition mechanism of R2 elements are conserved throughout arthropods. Mol. Biol. Evol. 16, 502–511 (1999)."), [54](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR54 "Kuroki-Kami, A. et al. Targeted gene knockin in zebrafish using the 28S rDNA-specific non-LTR-retrotransposon R2Ol. Mob. DNA 10, 23 (2019)."). R2 elements encode a single open reading frame (ORF) with DNA binding, endonuclease and RT activity[40](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR40 "Luan, D. D., Korman, M. H., Jakubczak, J. L. & Eickbush, T. H. Reverse transcription of R2Bm RNA is primed by a nick at the chromosomal target site: a mechanism for non-LTR retrotransposition. Cell 72, 595–605 (1993)."), [55](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR55 "Yang, J., Malik, H. S. & Eickbush, T. H. Identification of the endonuclease domain encoded by R2 and other site-specific, non-long terminal repeat retrotransposable elements. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 96, 7847–7852 (1999)."). During TPRT, the R2-encoded endonuclease recognizes the conserved retrotransposon upstream motif and introduces a site-specific nick at the retrotransposon-associated insertion site within the 28S rDNA[39](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR39 "Wilkinson, M. E., Frangieh, C. J., Macrae, R. K. & Zhang, F. Structure of the R2 non-LTR retrotransposon initiating target-primed reverse transcription. Science 380, 301–308 (2023)."), [56](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR56 "Deng, P. et al. Structural RNA components supervise the sequential DNA cleavage in R2 retrotransposon. Cell 186, 2865–2879 e2820 (2023)."), [57](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR57 "Thawani, A. et al. Structures of vertebrate R2 retrotransposon complexes during target-primed reverse transcription and after second-strand nicking. Sci. Adv. 11, eadu5533 (2025)."). The exposed 3′ hydroxyl end then primes reverse transcription by the R2-encoded RT, leading to cDNA synthesis directly from the donor RNA and its integration at the target locus[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR36 "Zhang, X. et al. Harnessing eukaryotic retroelement proteins for transgene insertion into human safe-harbor loci. Nat. Biotechnol. 43, 42–51 (2025)."), [39](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR39 "Wilkinson, M. E., Frangieh, C. J., Macrae, R. K. & Zhang, F. Structure of the R2 non-LTR retrotransposon initiating target-primed reverse transcription. Science 380, 301–308 (2023)."), [40](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR40 "Luan, D. D., Korman, M. H., Jakubczak, J. L. & Eickbush, T. H. Reverse transcription of R2Bm RNA is primed by a nick at the chromosomal target site: a mechanism for non-LTR retrotransposition. Cell 72, 595–605 (1993)."). In contrast to nuclease-based genome editing systems, this R2-mediated RNA-templated integration proceeds without simultaneous DNA DSBs or direct reliance on host DNA repair pathways, potentially reducing the risk of genomic instability or off-target effects, thereby sparing the unique coding regions[39](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR39 "Wilkinson, M. E., Frangieh, C. J., Macrae, R. K. & Zhang, F. Structure of the R2 non-LTR retrotransposon initiating target-primed reverse transcription. Science 380, 301–308 (2023)."), [58](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR58 "Tang, S. & Sternberg, S. H. Genome editing with retroelements. Science 382, 370–371 (2023)."). These properties position R2 elements as an attractive system for site-specific genome engineering[59](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR59 "Luo, S., Chen, Q., Chen, Y. & Li, W. Biology and utilization of R2 retrotransposons. RNA Biol. 22, 1–8 (2025).").

Recent work has repurposed R2 for precise gene insertion in mammalian systems[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR36 "Zhang, X. et al. Harnessing eukaryotic retroelement proteins for transgene insertion into human safe-harbor loci. Nat. Biotechnol. 43, 42–51 (2025)."), [60](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR60 "Chen, Y. et al. All-RNA-mediated targeted gene integration in mammalian cells with rationally engineered R2 retrotransposons. Cell 187, 4674–4689.e4618 (2024)."), [61](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR61 "Edmonds, K. K. et al. Structure and biochemistry-guided engineering of an all-RNA system for DNA insertion with R2 retrotransposons. Nat. Commun. 16, 6079 (2025)."). The PRINT (precise RNA-mediated insertion of transgenes) system uses R2 protein in combination with an engineered RNA template to achieve targeted insertion into human rDNA[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR36 "Zhang, X. et al. Harnessing eukaryotic retroelement proteins for transgene insertion into human safe-harbor loci. Nat. Biotechnol. 43, 42–51 (2025)."). Subsequent optimization of the R2 effectors through targeted amino acid substitutions and enhanced nucleic acid-binding capacity, along with rational engineering of the donor RNA templates, substantially improved donor RNA stability and insertion efficiency[60](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR60 "Chen, Y. et al. All-RNA-mediated targeted gene integration in mammalian cells with rationally engineered R2 retrotransposons. Cell 187, 4674–4689.e4618 (2024)."), [61](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR61 "Edmonds, K. K. et al. Structure and biochemistry-guided engineering of an all-RNA system for DNA insertion with R2 retrotransposons. Nat. Commun. 16, 6079 (2025)."). A recent report extends retrotransposon applicability by coupling R2TG to CRISPR-Cas9 nickases for targeting arbitrary loci beyond rDNA[62](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR62 "Fell, C. W. et al. Reprogramming site-specific retrotransposon activity to new DNA sites. Nature 642, 1080–1089 (2025).").

Despite these advances, the R2-mediated genome engineering remains largely unexplored in plants, with no reported demonstration in crop species. This gap is particularly relevant in plant biotechnology, given that many agronomically important traits require precise integration of full-length genes together with native regulatory elements, typically spanning 0.5 to 3 kb[63](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR63 "Kawahara, Y. et al. Improvement of the Oryza sativa Nipponbare reference genome using next generation sequence and optical map data. Rice (N Y) 6, 4 (2013)."), [64](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR64 "Hamilton, J. P., Li, C. & Buell, C. R. The rice genome annotation project: an updated database for mining the rice genome. Nucleic Acids Res. 53, D1614–d1622 (2025)."). However, existing plant gene-integration methods are inefficient for multikilobase cargo and often induce unintended genomic alterations[65](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR65 "Butt, H. et al. Efficient CRISPR/Cas9-mediated genome editing using a chimeric single-guide RNA molecule. Front. Plant Sci. 8, 1441 (2017)."), [66](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR66 "Ali, Z. et al. Fusion of the Cas9 endonuclease and the VirD2 relaxase facilitates homology-directed repair for precise genome engineering in rice. Commun. Biol. 3, 44 (2020)."), [67](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR67 "Molla, K. A., Sretenovic, S., Bansal, K. C. & Qi, Y. Precise plant genome editing using base editors and prime editors. Nat. Plants 7, 1166–1187 (2021)."), whereas retrotransposon-based strategies offer a complementary approach for safe, stable and sequence-specific integration of exogenous DNA.

Here we investigate the feasibility of repurposing R2 retrotransposons for precision genome engineering in plants. Using R2 elements derived from _Taeniopygia guttata_ and _Zonotrichia albicollis_, we demonstrate targeted transgene insertion into the 28S rDNA loci in _N. benthamiana_. Our results demonstrate R2-mediated successful integration of one to four copies of GFP expression cassette and installation of orthogonal recombinase landing pads (attP, lox66, and FRT) at rDNA target sites in _N. benthamiana_. To advance toward an all-RNA R2 system implementation in plants, we developed an RNA virus-mediated RNA donor delivery strategy that decouples donor template availability from sustained effector expression, enabling temporally constrained rDNA targeting. Finally, we extend this approach to crop systems by assessing targeted insertion of kanamycin-resistance and ALS (Acetolactate synthase) herbicide-resistance cassettes into rice rDNA. Together, our findings establish R2 retrotransposons as a site-specific gene addition systems in plants, providing a foundation for safe-harbor integration of multikilobase DNA and enabling stable, heritable genome engineering across plant species to support multigene stacking, synthetic pathway assembly and deployment of complex agronomic traits.

## Results

### Design and construction of R2 retrotransposon machinery for in planta activity

Non-LTR retrotransposons represent a promising system for targeted gene integration in plants. Successful deployment requires efficient delivery, stable expression and proper localization of both the integration machinery and gene cargo within the plant cell nucleus. Here we repurposed the R2 retrotransposon system (Fig. [1a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig1)), previously shown to mediate site-specific integration in non-native hosts[44](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR44 "Palm, S. M., Horton, C. A., Zhang, X. & Collins, K. Structure and sequence at an RNA template 5′ end influence insertion of transgenes by an R2 retrotransposon protein. RNA 30, 1227–1245 (2024)."), [54](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR54 "Kuroki-Kami, A. et al. Targeted gene knockin in zebrafish using the 28S rDNA-specific non-LTR-retrotransposon R2Ol. Mob. DNA 10, 23 (2019)."), [60](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR60 "Chen, Y. et al. All-RNA-mediated targeted gene integration in mammalian cells with rationally engineered R2 retrotransposons. Cell 187, 4674–4689.e4618 (2024)."), for use in plant systems. Our strategy builds on core principles necessary for R2 function in heterologous contexts, namely, nuclear targeting of the effector protein, transcript stabilization and optimization of the RNA donor template[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR36 "Zhang, X. et al. Harnessing eukaryotic retroelement proteins for transgene insertion into human safe-harbor loci. Nat. Biotechnol. 43, 42–51 (2025)."), [60](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR60 "Chen, Y. et al. All-RNA-mediated targeted gene integration in mammalian cells with rationally engineered R2 retrotransposons. Cell 187, 4674–4689.e4618 (2024).").

**Fig. 1: Mechanism and in planta validation of R2-mediated targeted integration into rDNA.**

![Fig. 1: Mechanism and in planta validation of R2-mediated targeted integration into rDNA.](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41587-026-03181-6/MediaObjects/41587_2026_3181_Fig1_HTML.png)

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6/figures/1)

**a**, Mechanism of non-LTR R2-mediated targeted insertion into plant rDNA. Target-site DNA nicking by the R2 endonuclease generates a 3′ flap structure that initiates TPRT)-mediated integration of the RNA donor template into rDNA. A second single-strand DNA nick introduced by R2 at the upper strand of the target initiates second-strand DNA synthesis, completing targeted integration of the transgene into the rDNA locus. **b**, Schematic of in planta expression of codon-optimized R2 effectors, R2-TG ( _Taeniopygia guttata_, zebra finch), R2-BM ( _Bombyx mori_, silk moth _)_, ZA ( _Zonotrichia albicollis_, white-throated sparrow) and their variants (TGopt, optimized for enhanced activity; TG-intron, TGopt-intron; Intron, potato invertase intron IV) and RNA donor templates for R2-mediated TPRT-based integration into rDNA. Arrows indicate RNA synthesis, and donor insertion boundaries are shown in red. R2 effectors are drawn to the scale. Zf (zinc-finger DNA binding domain), RT (reverse-transcriptase domain) and RLE (restriction enzyme-like endonuclease domain) are shown. LB, T-DNA left border, RB, T-DNA right border, pUBI, ubiquitin promoter, NLS, nuclear localization signal, Flag tag, rbcS-E9 terminator. The RNA donor template comprises R2-specific 5′ untranslated region (UTR) and 3′ UTR that flank the GOI cassette and are expressed from the CaMV 35S promoter. Ends of the donor insert site at the target site are shown in red boxes. LHA, left-hand homology arm; RHA, right-hand homology arm; p35S, 35Ster, CaMV 35S promoter and terminator. **c**, Expression analysis of R2 effector proteins. Immunoprecipitation confirming nuclear enrichment of the R2 effectors in plant cells. Cas9-NLS-Flag was used as a control. Red arrows indicate the expected sizes of protein. The multiple lanes shown under each annotation represent independent replicates of the experiment. **d**, R2-mediated resurrection of the dTRBO-GFP reporter into the replicative TRBO-GFP reporter in plant cells. _N. benthamiana_ plants co-infiltrated with _Agrobacterium_ containing dTRBO-GFP and R2 effectors and Ω sequence expressing RNA templates of 324 bp. GFP fluorescence was visualized and photographed under handheld UV illumination. CNT, control leaves infiltrated with _Agrobacterium_ containing dTRBO-GFP only. **e**, Alignment of Sanger sequencing reads from RT-PCR amplicons confirming insertion of the Ω sequence into episomal rDNA.

[source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#MOESM4)

To test the broad utility of R2 systems, we selected R2 proteins from three species: _Zonotrichia albicollis_ (R2-ZA), _Bombyx mori_ (R2-BM) and two constructs from _Taeniopygia guttata_, the wild-type R2-TG and an optimized variant, R2-TGopt (Fig. [1b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig1))[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR36 "Zhang, X. et al. Harnessing eukaryotic retroelement proteins for transgene insertion into human safe-harbor loci. Nat. Biotechnol. 43, 42–51 (2025)."), [60](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR60 "Chen, Y. et al. All-RNA-mediated targeted gene integration in mammalian cells with rationally engineered R2 retrotransposons. Cell 187, 4674–4689.e4618 (2024)."). R2 ORFs and donor templates were codon-optimized for plant expression and custom-synthesized. Constructs were introduced into _Agrobacterium tumefaciens_, but whereas R2-ZA and R2-BM constructs transformed efficiently, R2-TG and R2-TGopt constructs led to growth inhibition or failed to produce colonies (Supplementary Fig. [1a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#MOESM1)). As TG variants previously exhibited the highest TPRT efficiency[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR36 "Zhang, X. et al. Harnessing eukaryotic retroelement proteins for transgene insertion into human safe-harbor loci. Nat. Biotechnol. 43, 42–51 (2025)."), we hypothesized that leaky expression in _Agrobacterium_ compromised cell viability. To mitigate this, we replaced the strong CaMV 35S promoter with a moderate pUBI promoter and introduced _Solanum tuberosum_ invertase intron IV to suppress leaky effector expression in _Agrobacterium_. Intron insertion into the TG (after K126) and TGopt (at R436) ORFs resulted in successful transformation of _Agrobacterium_ (Supplementary Fig. [1b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#MOESM1)).

To assess nuclear localization, constructs were co-delivered with the P19 silencing suppressor into _N. benthamiana_ leaves via _Agrobacterium_. Subcellular fractionation and FLAG immunoprecipitation confirmed nuclear enrichment of R2 proteins (Fig. [1c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig1) and Supplementary Fig. [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#MOESM1)). These findings enabled us to develop a high-throughput visual reporter system to assess R2-mediated integration activity in planta.

Because structural requirements for R2 donor RNA and TPRT function in plants remain poorly characterized, we adapted a TMV-based TRBO-GFP visual marker system as an episomal insertion reporter. The native 5′ leader (Ω sequence) of TRBO-GFP, essential for replication and translation, was replaced with an R2 target rDNA sequence to create a nonfunctional dead (dTRBO-GFP) reporter (Extended Data Fig. [1a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig5)). This nonfunctional dead (dTRBO-GFP) version fails to express GFP. Restoration of GFP fluorescence thus serves as a direct readout of successful R2-mediated DNA insertion (Extended Data Fig. [1a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig5)). Donor templates encoding the Ω sequence, flanked by R2-specific 5′ and 3′ untranslated regions (UTRs), were co-infiltrated with dTRBO-GFP and R2 effector constructs into _N. benthamiana_ leaves. Bright GFP fluorescence was observed in infiltrated regions, indicating targeted repair and reconstitution of the TRBO-GFP reporter system (Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig1) and Extended Data Fig. [1b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig5)).

Next, we compared the insertion efficiencies of different R2 variants. R2-TGopt and R2-TG produced robust GFP signal restoration, whereas R2-BM and R2-ZA showed comparatively lower activity, producing few fluorescent foci per leaf (R2-TGopt ~16, R2-TG ~24, R2-BM ~14, R2-ZA ~4) (Extended Data Fig. [1c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig5)). dTG (endonuclease-dead TG) and RT-only donor repair template did not produce GFP fluorescence foci, confirming R2-dependent resurrection of the TRBO-GFP reporter system (Extended Data Fig. [1b, c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig5)). To validate insertion at the molecular level, we extracted RNA from GFP-signal-positive tissue and performed reverse-transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) using specific primers flanking the target site. Amplified products digested with restriction enzymes (StyI or MlyI) confirmed the expected size of DNA fragments (Extended Data Fig. [1d, e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig5)). Sanger sequencing of PCR amplicons further verified precise and directional integration of the Ω element into the engineered rDNA target (Fig. [1e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig1)).

These results establish that the R2 retrotransposon can be repurposed as a DNA integration tool in plants. The system supports targeted insertion of large DNA payloads into episomal rDNA and lays the groundwork for stable integration into genomic rDNA loci, thereby expanding the plant genome engineering toolkit.

### R2-mediated integration of full-length gene cassettes into the plant rDNA

Although R2-mediated gene integration into rDNA loci has been demonstrated in mammalian systems[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR36 "Zhang, X. et al. Harnessing eukaryotic retroelement proteins for transgene insertion into human safe-harbor loci. Nat. Biotechnol. 43, 42–51 (2025)."), [60](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR60 "Chen, Y. et al. All-RNA-mediated targeted gene integration in mammalian cells with rationally engineered R2 retrotransposons. Cell 187, 4674–4689.e4618 (2024)."), [61](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR61 "Edmonds, K. K. et al. Structure and biochemistry-guided engineering of an all-RNA system for DNA insertion with R2 retrotransposons. Nat. Commun. 16, 6079 (2025)."), detailed insight into active R2 effectors and RNA template requirements TPRT in plants remains lacking. Having established functional R2 expression and TPRT activity using a visual reporter system coupled to episomal rDNA (Fig. [1d, e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig1)), we next evaluated whether R2 machinery could catalyze the stable integration of a full-length gene cassette into the nuclear rDNA of _N. benthamiana_. We infiltrated the R2 effectors along with specifically designed GFP (GFinP, GFP-intron)[68](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR68 "Moran, J. V. et al. High frequency retrotransposition in cultured mammalian cells. Cell 87, 917–927 (1996)."), [69](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR69 "Ostertag, E. M., Prak, E. T., DeBerardinis, R. J., Moran, J. V. & Kazazian, H. H. Jr. Determination of L1 retrotransposition kinetics in cultured cells. Nucleic Acids Res. 28, 1418–1423 (2000).") constructs into _N. benthamiana_ leaves (Fig. [2a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig2)). RT-PCR analysis using intron-flanking primers confirmed both the synthesis and correct splicing of the GFP donor RNA in planta, verifying the production of a mature transcript compatible with R2-mediated, TPRT-based insertion into rDNA (Extended Data Fig. [2a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig6)). Subsequently, to evaluate retrotransposition and transcriptional activity of the integrated cassette, cDNA was synthesized using a GFP-specific reverse primer, followed by RT-PCR with an intron-spanning primer pair. Amplification of the expected product confirmed correct expression of the integrated gene cassette (Extended Data Fig. [2b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig6)). Next, GFP protein expression from the integrated genetic circuit was examined by confocal microscopy of infiltrated leaf tissue. GFP fluorescence was detected throughout the cell, confirming expression from the rDNA-integrated pMinSyn-GFP-NosT cassette (Fig. [2b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig2)). No GFP signal was observed when the GFP donor RNA construct was delivered alone or together with endonuclease-dead TGopt (dTGopt) effectors (Extended Data Fig. [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig7)), indicating that GFP expression was dependent on catalytically active R2-mediated integration. The absence of fluorescence in the dTGopt condition further confirmed the lack of off-target donor insertion in the _N. benthamiana_ genome. To independently validate protein translation from the integrated transcript, GFP was immunoprecipitated from leaves infiltrated with TG and TGopt effectors and donor RNA templates-expressing constructs using an anti-GFP antibody, confirming translation of the integrated transcript (Fig. [2c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig2)). Genomic integration of the donor cassette was next assessed by PCR analysis of total genomic DNA isolated four days post infiltration. Junction-specific primers (2F/1R for the 3′ junction and 1F/2R for the 5′ junction) revealed precise integration into nuclear rDNA loci (Fig. [2d, e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig2)). Amplicons of the expected size were detected for R2-TG and R2-TGopt, whereas R2-BM and R2-ZA showed reduced integration activity. Gel purification and Sanger sequencing of expected size junction fragments confirmed accurate integration boundaries (Fig. [2f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig2)).

[source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#MOESM4)

Because R2-mediated insertions in mammalian cells can exhibit 5′ truncation[61](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR61 "Edmonds, K. K. et al. Structure and biochemistry-guided engineering of an all-RNA system for DNA insertion with R2 retrotransposons. Nat. Commun. 16, 6079 (2025)."), [70](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR70 "McIntyre, J. J. R., Horton, C. A. & Collins, K. Different DNA repair pathways support intact or truncated insertions by R2 retrotransposon protein. Science 391, eadz3121 (2026)."), we examined the integrity of 5′ integration junctions in plants infiltrated with R2-TG and R2-TGopt. To minimize amplification bias, the complete pool of 5′ junction amplicons, including both full-length and truncated species, was recovered without size selection from R2-TG samples, cloned into a library, and subjected to Oxford Nanopore single-molecule sequencing. This analysis revealed that 75% of reads correspond to full-length insertions, whereas the remaining 25% exhibit 5′ truncations in R2-mediated insertions, highlighting heterogeneity at the 5′ integration boundary during rDNA-targeted insertion in plants (Fig. [2g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig2) and Extended Data Fig. [4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig8)).

To further validate junction architecture and assess integration specificity, we applied a genomic DNA transposition mapping strategy (Extended Data Fig. [5a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig9)). This approach independently captured both intact and truncated insertion products and detected integration events exclusively at the expected rDNA locus, with no evidence of off-target insertions. Notably, the same workflow readily detected randomly integrated T-DNA fragments present within the same samples, supporting the sensitivity of the method. Of the total reads, 15.4% in R2-TGopt and 30.9% in R2-TG aligned to the rDNA locus (Fig. [2h](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig2) and Extended Data Fig. [5b, c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig9)). Together, these results confirm that R2-mediated insertions in plants are structurally heterogeneous at the 5′ boundary while maintaining high target specificity.

To confirm the precise integration of donor RNA template at the genomic DNA level, we performed a Southern blot analysis. Southern blot analysis using a GFP-specific probe further validated stable integration of the donor sequence into rDNA loci in the _N. benthamiana_ genome (Fig. [2i](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig2) and Supplementary Fig. [3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#MOESM1)), supporting the use of non-LTR R2 retrotransposons for direct DNA writing at plant safe-harbor loci. Quantification of full-length transgene copy number by digital droplet PCR targeting the 5′ rDNA-donor junction indicated an average of 0.7 to 1.2 copies per _N. benthamiana_ genome (Fig. [2j](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig2)).

Together, these results demonstrate that non-LTR R2 retrotransposons can mediate site-specific integration and expression of full-length gene circuits within nuclear rDNA loci in planta. This approach enables targeted insertion of functional genetic units without the need for auxiliary recombinases or nuclease-based genome editing, highlighting the potential of R2 retrotransposons as a versatile system for plant genome engineering.

### RNA template-mediated efficient integration of recombinases landing pads in the rDNA

Advanced agronomic and synthetic biology applications require precise multigene integration; however, current technologies are limited by cargo capacity and unpredictable insertion. To overcome these limitations, we leveraged R2 retrotransposons to install recombinase-compatible landing pads directly into nuclear rDNA loci, which represent transcriptionally active and relatively stable genomic regions. These landing pads enable the use of orthogonal site-specific recombinases, such as BxbI, Cre, and Flp, for targeted and scalable insertion of multikilobase transgenes into preinstalled recombination ‘landing pads’ (for example, attP, lox66 and FRT) at defined loci. Despite the broad utility of recombinase-based strategies, installing landing pads in plants remains technically challenging. CRISPR-Cas-mediated HR and prime editing have shown limited success, particularly when multiple distinct landing pads must be introduced in the plant genome in parallel[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR22 "Li, R. et al. Method for biolistic site-specific integration in plants catalyzed by Bxb1 integrase. Methods Mol. Biol. 1469, 15–30 (2016)."), [26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR26 "Sun, C. et al. Precise integration of large DNA sequences in plant genomes using PrimeRoot editors. Nat. Biotechnol. 42, 316–327 (2024)."), [71](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR71 "De Paepe, A. et al. High frequency of single-copy T-DNA transformants produced by floral dip in CRE-expressing Arabidopsis plants. Plant J. 59, 517–527 (2009).").

We designed a synthetic donor RNA cassette encoding three recombinase landing pads and infiltrated it along with R2 effectors into _N. benthamiana_ leaves, and assessed integration by junction PCR (Fig. [3a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig3) and Extended Data Fig. [6a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig10)). Amplification of products of the expected size confirmed precise integration of the full-length landing-pad cassette by R2-TGopt and TG effectors, whereas R2-BM and R2-ZA exhibited lower integration efficiencies (Extended Data Fig. [6b, c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig10)). To assess sequence integrity, PCR amplicons were Sanger sequenced, confirming full-length alignment with the designed landing pad and accurate insertion at both DNA fragment boundaries (Extended Data Fig. [6d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig10)). Consistent with the observations described above for other templates (Extended Data Figs. [4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig8) and [5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig9)), integration of the landing-pad cassette exhibited 5′ junction heterogeneity while remaining highly target-specific, as supported by Nanopore sequencing and transposition mapping analyses (Extended Data Figs. [7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig11) and [8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig12)).

[source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#MOESM4)

These results demonstrate the feasibility of using non-LTR R2 retrotransposons to install multiple recombinase-specific landing pads at defined genomic safe-harbor loci in plants, providing a foundation for targeted genome engineering and multigene stacking to enable complex trait improvement in crops.

Although all-RNA R2 delivery strategies have been demonstrated in mammalian systems, their implementation in plants is challenged by physical barriers such as the cell wall, as well as limitations in RNA uptake and stability. In addition, DNA-based donor delivery can prolong the availability of the R2 machinery, leading to cumulative perturbation of the rDNA array and potential impacts on cellular viability, and can generate PCR-detectable episomal molecules or randomly integrated fragments, which complicate the interpretation of targeted insertion events. To overcome these constraints, we implemented a two-component strategy in which the R2 effector is expressed in planta, whereas the donor RNA template is supplied using a recombinant RNA virus enrichment system (Fig. [3b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig3)). RNA virus-mediated donor RNA production has been reported to increase donor RNA abundance in planta, enabling transient donor availability without sustained nuclear transcription or persistent DNA intermediates[72](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR72 "Ali, Z. et al. Efficient virus-mediated genome editing in plants using the CRISPR/Cas9 system. Mol. Plant 8, 1288–1291 (2015)."), [73](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#ref-CR73 "Liu, Q., Zhao, C., Sun, K., Deng, Y. & Li, Z. Engineered biocontainable RNA virus vectors for non-transgenic genome editing across crop species and genotypes. Mol. Plant 16, 616–631 (2023).").

To decouple donor template availability from prolonged effector expression and thereby constrain cumulative perturbation of the rDNA array, enriched sap containing recombinant RNA viruses (TRV and TSWV) encoding the R2 donor sequence was agroinfiltrated into _N. benthamiana_ leaves expressing the R2 effector. Junction PCR analysis detected amplification products of the expected size exclusively in samples receiving donor-encoding viral sap, whereas no amplification was observed in plants infected with a virus lacking the donor sequence (Supplementary Fig. [4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#MOESM1)). These results demonstrate that RNA donor templates produced via recombinant RNA viruses are sufficient to support R2-mediated rDNA integration in planta.

To assess the robustness and physiological impact of this approach, _N. benthamiana_ plants constitutively expressing the optimized R2 effectors were regenerated, and protein expression was confirmed in F0 and F1 generations by immunoblotting (Supplementary Fig. [5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#MOESM1)). Only ZA-expressing plants remained viable, exhibited mild and transient leaf phenotypes, and progressed through normal development. Infiltration of donor-encoding recombinant RNA virus into ZA-expressing F1 plants resulted in donor integration into rDNA, detectable at both the 5′ and 3′ junctions by PCR (Supplementary Fig. [6a, b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#MOESM1)). Sanger sequencing verified precise junction formation consistent with canonical R2-mediated TPRT (Fig. [3c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig3)).

Quantification by digital droplet PCR targeting the 5′ rDNA-donor junction revealed low but reproducible insertion frequencies within the rDNA array, with an estimated 1.97–4.06 donor copies per _N. benthamiana_ genome (Fig. [3d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig3)), consistent with limited rounds of R2 activity during transient donor exposure. Southern blot analysis of restriction-digested genomic DNA using a probe specific to the synthetic landing-pad cassette detected fragments of the predicted size, confirming stable genomic integration into rDNA loci (Fig. [3e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig3) and Supplementary Fig. [7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#MOESM1)).

Together, these results establish that RNA virus-mediated donor RNA delivery is sufficient to support controlled R2-mediated integration of recombinase landing pads into rDNA. This strategy provides experimental support for a temporally self-limiting, all-RNA R2 genome-engineering framework in plants and enables the scalable installation of recombinase landing pads for large-fragment transgene stacking.

### R2-mediated integration of full-length genetic cassettes into the rice rDNA

To extend R2 retrotransposon-based genome engineering to crops, we established an R2-mediated system for targeted gene insertion into the _O. sativa_ (rice) genome. Building on our results in _N. benthamiana_, we engineered rice-compatible binary vectors expressing R2 effectors and donor RNA templates carrying a full-length kanamycin-resistance gene. Expression of R2 proteins from rice compatible plasmids was confirmed via western blot analysis (Supplementary Fig. [8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#MOESM1)). For post-integration expression, the kanamycin gene was placed under control of the CaMV 35S promoter (pCaMV) (Fig. [4a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03181-6#Fig4)).

The modified payload with homology arms (100 bp of homologous sequence on each side of the donor template) and payload with 5´ UTR and 3´ UTR. Binary plasmids expressing both the donor template and R2 effectors were transformed into rice calli.
