ArticleOpen access08 June 2023

## Main

A broad variety of neurological disorders are characterized by atrophy and involution of the cerebral white matter, with oligodendrocytic loss and demyelination unaccompanied by adaptive or compensatory remyelination. In a number of these conditions, including disorders as varied as progressive multiple sclerosis (PMS), Huntington disease (HD), juvenile onset schizophrenia and the periventricular leukomalacia of cerebral palsy, dysmyelination evolves despite the persistence of a pool of parenchymal glial progenitor cells (GPCs)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR1 "Franklin, R. J. M., Bodini, B. & Goldman, S. A. Remyelination in the central nervous system. Cold Spring Harb. Perspect. Biol. 16, a041371 (2024)."), [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR2 "Groh, J. & Simons, M. White matter aging and its impact on brain function. Neuron 113, 127–139 (2025)."). These GPCs, which are also typically referred to as oligodendrocyte progenitor cells but may give rise to astrocytes or oligodendrocytes in humans, appear to lose mitotic and differentiation competence in the setting of these disorders. This may occur by virtue of mitotic exhaustion, as in PMS and late aging, or because of cell-intrinsic defects in maturational pathways, as in HD and schizophrenia[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR3 "Huynh, N. P. T. et al. Shared patterns of glial transcriptional dysregulation link Huntington’s disease and schizophrenia. Brain 147, 3099–3112 (2024)."), [4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR4 "Osipovitch, M. et al. Human ESC-derived chimeric mouse models of Huntington’s disease reveal cell-intrinsic defects in glial progenitor cell differentiation. Cell Stem Cell 24, 107–122 (2019)."), [5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR5 "Windrem, M. S. et al. Human iPSC glial mouse chimeras reveal glial contributions to schizophrenia. Cell Stem Cell 21, 195–208 (2017)."). To target these cells for therapeutic manipulation, vectors able to deliver genetic payloads to these cells, whether specifically so or to neurons as well, and to target the central nervous system (CNS) only, with little or no systemic transduction, are needed.

Adeno-associated virus (AAV) vectors, the most widely used viral vector in clinical trials and contemporary gene therapy, enable stable expression of therapeutic transgenes in cell types of interest. Natural AAV serotypes can infect many different cell types, which can lead to undesired off-target effects and toxicities. To address this issue, a number of strategies have been developed for evolving serotype-specific binding domains to target prospectively defined cell types of interest. In particular, previous studies have developed approaches toward capsid evolution based on Cre recombinase-dependent identification of viruses evolved to specifically target desired cells[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR6 "Deverman, B. E. et al. Cre-dependent selection yields AAV variants for widespread gene transfer to the adult brain. Nat. Biotechnol. 34, 204–209 (2016)."). In particular, viral variants with randomly mutagenized VP1 capsid sequences have been screened for their relative infectivity of given cell types[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR7 "Kumar, S. R. et al. Multiplexed Cre-dependent selection yields systemic AAVs for targeting distinct brain cell types. Nat. Methods 17, 541–550 (2020)."). However, most of these studies have evolved viral variants either in mouse transgenic reporters with phenotype-selective Cre expression in vivo[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR6 "Deverman, B. E. et al. Cre-dependent selection yields AAV variants for widespread gene transfer to the adult brain. Nat. Biotechnol. 34, 204–209 (2016)."), [7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR7 "Kumar, S. R. et al. Multiplexed Cre-dependent selection yields systemic AAVs for targeting distinct brain cell types. Nat. Methods 17, 541–550 (2020).") or in human cells in vitro[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR8 "Giacomoni, J. et al. Identification and validation of novel engineered AAV capsid variants targeting human glia. Front. Neurosci. 18, 1435212 (2024)."); none have pursued viral evolution against human cells in vivo. Yet, the surface epitopes presented by human cells are quite distinct from those presented by mice, and epitopes expressed in vivo may differ substantially from those targeted in vitro. Moreover, most studies focused on the brain have sought to target neurons; only a few have targeted glial cells[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR8 "Giacomoni, J. et al. Identification and validation of novel engineered AAV capsid variants targeting human glia. Front. Neurosci. 18, 1435212 (2024)."), [9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR9 "Powell, S. K. et al. Characterization of a novel adeno-associated viral vector with preferential oligodendrocyte tropism. Gene Ther. 23, 807–814 (2016)."). An AAV vector that preferentially targets striatal oligodendrocytes was developed in rats that also infects these cells in macaques[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR9 "Powell, S. K. et al. Characterization of a novel adeno-associated viral vector with preferential oligodendrocyte tropism. Gene Ther. 23, 807–814 (2016)."), [10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR10 "Mandel, R. J. et al. Novel oligodendroglial alpha synuclein viral vector models of multiple system atrophy: studies in rodents and nonhuman primates. Acta Neuropathol. Commun. 5, 47 (2017)."); however, this vector requires intracerebral administration and its extrastriatal and human phenotypic selectivities remain unclear. To our knowledge, no prior studies have focused on targeting human glia in vivo and no vectors have been reported that efficiently target GPCs, the precursor to both oligodendrocytes and astrocytes, which comprise 3–4% of all brain cells in adult humans and mice alike[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR11 "Roy, N. S. et al. Identification, isolation, and promoter-defined separation of mitotic oligodendrocyte progenitor cells from the adult human subcortical white matter. J. Neurosci. 19, 9986–9995 (1999)."), [12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR12 "Scolding, N. et al. Oligodendrocyte progenitors are present in the normal adult human CNS and in the lesions of multiple sclerosis. Brain 121, 2221–2228 (1998)."), [13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR13 "Scolding, N. J., Rayner, P. J. & Compston, D. A. Identification of A2B5-positive putative oligodendrocyte progenitor cells and A2B5-positive astrocytes in adult human white matter. Neuroscience 89, 1–4 (1999)."), [14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR14 "Windrem, M. S. et al. Fetal and adult human oligodendrocyte progenitor cell isolates myelinate the congenitally dysmyelinated brain. Nat. Med. 10, 93–97 (2004)."), [15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR15 "Nunes, M. C. et al. Identification and isolation of multipotential neural progenitor cells from the subcortical white matter of the adult human brain. Nat. Med. 9, 439–447 (2003).").

To develop vectors targeting human GPCs (hGPCs) and their progeny in vivo, we adapted the previously described M-CREATE selection strategy[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR6 "Deverman, B. E. et al. Cre-dependent selection yields AAV variants for widespread gene transfer to the adult brain. Nat. Biotechnol. 34, 204–209 (2016)."), [7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR7 "Kumar, S. R. et al. Multiplexed Cre-dependent selection yields systemic AAVs for targeting distinct brain cell types. Nat. Methods 17, 541–550 (2020)."), through which Cre recombinase-expressing cells are used to screen recombination-reported viral variants for cell tropism, but we did so through capsid evolution on Cre-expressing hGPCs in vivo. In particular, we screened viral candidates in human glial chimeric mice, which were neonatally engrafted with human embryonic stem-cell-derived GPCs expressing Cre under the control of PDGFRA, which is selectively expressed by hGPCs in the brain. Our viral candidates were engineered to include Lox66/Lox71-flanked sequences, whose Cre-dependent recombination in targeted Cre-expressing host cells allowed the PCR-based identification of those viral variants that most efficiently infected hGPCs (Fig. [1a–c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig1)).

**Fig. 1: Production of capsid-evolved AAV5s designed to target hGPCs in vivo.**

![Fig. 1: Production of capsid-evolved AAV5s designed to target hGPCs in vivo.](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41587-026-03185-2/MediaObjects/41587_2026_3185_Fig1_HTML.png)

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2/figures/1)

**a**, hES cells were engineered to express PDGFRA-driven Cre by knocking in Cre 3′ to the endogenous _PDGFRA_ locus. **b**, **c**, After glial induction, the hGPCs were injected into newborn _Rag1_ _−_/ _−_ mice ( **b**) to produce PDGFRA-Cre glial chimeras ( **c**). **d**, Map of approach toward generating a Cre-dependent AAV5 viral capsid library. A 21-nt (7-mer) random sequence is placed between Q574 and S575 of the VP1 capsid gene, in one of the VP1 loop-binding domains. A Cre-sensitive Lox71/Lox66-flanked sequence harbors PCR primer sequences (P1–P3) that are amplified, or not, depending upon whether Cre-triggered recombination has occurred in the infected human target cell. **e**, At 16 weeks of age, the viral library was injected intracisternally into the brains of these mice (10 µl; 1012 vg per ml) under hypertonic conditions to potentiate glymphatic transport of virus. **f**, The mice were killed 1 week later and the brain and liver were analyzed by PCR with primers for either Cre recombined (hGPCs; P1 > 3) or nonrecombined sequences (mouse; P1 > 2). Capsid sequences recovered from the liver were excluded from further analysis. **g**, Viral capsids in infected GPCs as a function of species; sequencing of PCR products showed that a number of viral variants were relatively specific to hGPCs, while others infected both mouse GPCs and hGPCs.

[Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#MOESM4)

The engineering of capsid-evolved vectors addresses only one aspect of the larger challenge of vector delivery to the brain; viral vectors, however efficient and cell type selective, are only as good as their tissue access. AAV delivery to the brain has typically been limited by poor blood–brain barrier (BBB) permeability and broad systemic leakage, requiring high and, hence, toxic doses. The BBB, in particular, poses a formidable obstacle to the efficient transduction of neurons and glia by systemically administered viral vectors, as it hinders viral entry from the systemic circulation. While serotypes such as AAV9-PHP.B and capsid-modified variants have been engineered to traverse the barrier[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR16 "Huang, Q. et al. Targeting AAV vectors to the central nervous system by engineering capsid-receptor interactions that enable crossing of the blood–brain barrier. PLoS Biol. 21, e3002112 (2023)."), [17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR17 "Huang, Q. et al. An AAV capsid reprogrammed to bind human transferrin receptor mediates brain-wide gene delivery. Science 384, 1220–1227 (2024)."), [18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR18 "Huang, Q. et al. Delivering genes across the blood-brain barrier: LY6A, a novel cellular receptor for AAV-PHP.B capsids. PLoS ONE 14, e0225206 (2019)."), their clinical use has nonetheless been limited by off-target transduction and the attendant toxicity of systemic infection[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR19 "Hinderer, C. et al. Severe toxicity in nonhuman primates and piglets following high-dose intravenous administration of an adeno-associated virus vector expressing human SMN. Hum. Gene Ther. 29, 285–298 (2018)."), as well as by the immunotoxicity associated with high-dose intravenous administration[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR20 "Arjomandnejad, M., Dasgupta, I., Flotte, T. R. & Keeler, A. M. Immunogenicity of recombinant adeno-associated virus (AAV) vectors for gene transfer. BioDrugs 37, 311–329 (2023)."), [21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR21 "Duan, D. Lethal immunotoxicity in high-dose systemic AAV therapy. Mol. Ther. 31, 3123–3126 (2023)."), [22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR22 "Gardin, A. & Ronzitti, G. Current limitations of gene therapy for rare pediatric diseases: lessons learned from clinical experience with AAV vectors. Arch. Pediatr. 30, 8S46–48S52 (2023)."), [23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR23 "Guo, Y. et al. High-titer AAV disrupts cerebrovascular integrity and induces lymphocyte infiltration in adult mouse brain. Mol. Ther. Methods Clin. Dev. 31, 101102 (2023)."), [24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR24 "Ling, Q., Herstine, J. A., Bradbury, A. & Gray, S. J. AAV-based in vivo gene therapy for neurological disorders. Nat. Rev. Drug Discov. 22, 789–806 (2023)."). Thus, despite the development of creative strategies for circumventing the BBB, the targeting efficiencies for desired phenotypes, particularly so for glia, have remained relatively low.

To establish a practicable strategy for circumventing the BBB using cell-type-selective vectors, we developed a method to co-opt the brain’s glymphatic system[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR25 "Iliff, J. J. et al. A paravascular pathway facilitates CSF flow through the brain parenchyma and the clearance of interstitial solutes, including amyloid β. Sci. Transl. Med. 4, 147ra111 (2012).") to achieve widespread viral delivery. The glymphatic pathways comprise a perivascular network of channels through which cerebrospinal fluid enters and perfuses the brain to remove metabolic waste[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR25 "Iliff, J. J. et al. A paravascular pathway facilitates CSF flow through the brain parenchyma and the clearance of interstitial solutes, including amyloid β. Sci. Transl. Med. 4, 147ra111 (2012)."). Past studies have shown that tracers and nanoparticles can be distributed throughout the brain through glymphatic flow[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR26 "Lilius, T. O. et al. Glymphatic-assisted perivascular brain delivery of intrathecal small gold nanoparticles. J. Control. Release 355, 135–148 (2023)."), suggesting its potential as an alternative route for drug, antibody and vector delivery to the brain parenchyma[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR26 "Lilius, T. O. et al. Glymphatic-assisted perivascular brain delivery of intrathecal small gold nanoparticles. J. Control. Release 355, 135–148 (2023)."), [27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR27 "Plog, B. A. et al. Transcranial optical imaging reveals a pathway for optimizing the delivery of immunotherapeutics to the brain. JCI Insight 3, e120922 (2018)."), [28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR28 "Wu, G. et al. The glymphatic system delivery enhances the transduction efficiency of AAV1 to brain endothelial cells in adult mice. J. Neurosci. Methods 328, 108441 (2019)."). In addition, parallel studies have indicated that fluid influx to the glymphatic pathways can be potentiated through systemic hyperosmolarity[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR26 "Lilius, T. O. et al. Glymphatic-assisted perivascular brain delivery of intrathecal small gold nanoparticles. J. Control. Release 355, 135–148 (2023)."), [27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR27 "Plog, B. A. et al. Transcranial optical imaging reveals a pathway for optimizing the delivery of immunotherapeutics to the brain. JCI Insight 3, e120922 (2018).") and access to these influx pathways can be gained through infusion into the cisterna magna[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR29 "Bailey, R. M., Rozenberg, A. & Gray, S. J. Comparison of high-dose intracisterna magna and lumbar puncture intrathecal delivery of AAV9 in mice to treat neuropathies. Brain Res. 1739, 146832 (2020)."), [30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR30 "Xavier, A. L. R. et al. Cannula implantation into the cisterna magna of rodents. J. Vis. Exp. 135, e57378 (2018)."), [31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR31 "Taghian, T. et al. A safe and reliable technique for CNS delivery of AAV vectors in the cisterna magna. Mol. Ther. 28, 411–421 (2020)."), [32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR32 "Castle, M. J., Cheng, Y., Asokan, A. & Tuszynski, M. H. Physical positioning markedly enhances brain transduction after intrathecal AAV9 infusion. Sci. Adv. 4, eaau9859 (2018)."), bypassing the BBB. On the basis of these studies, we combined intracisternal injection of our AAVs with systemic hyperosmolarity and, by doing so, achieved broad AAV distribution throughout the brain while avoiding BBB impedance. This delivery platform accordingly enabled the in vivo screening of a capsid library targeting hGPCs in human glial chimeric mice. We then filtered candidate vectors to exclude those with detectable off-target hepatic infection to identify variants that selectively and efficiently transduced hGPCs and their astrocytic and oligodendrocytic progeny. This approach yielded a panel of gliotropic viral vectors, each optimized to preferentially target a specific state along the human macroglial lineage. Combined with glymphatic delivery, these vectors demonstrate high on-target efficiency in vivo with minimal systemic infection.

## Results

### Human glial chimeric mice with Cre-expressing hGPCs

To establish human glial chimeric mice in which hGPCs could be identified as such following cell-specific Cre-dependent recombination, we first engineered human embryonic stem cells (hES cells) to express Cre recombinase under the control of _PDGFRA_, which is selectively expressed by GPCs in the adult brain[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR33 "Sim, F. J. et al. CD140a identifies a population of highly myelinogenic, migration-competent and efficiently engrafting human oligodendrocyte progenitor cells. Nat. Biotechnol. 29, 934–941 (2011)."). To this end, the Cre gene was knocked into the endogenous _PDGFRA_ locus of hES cells (Genea19 line) by homology-directed repair (HDR)-mediated insertion of donor DNA[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR34 "Ran, F. A. et al. Genome engineering using the CRISPR–Cas9 system. Nat. Protoc. 8, 2281–2308 (2013)."). After sequence confirmation of the insert and confirmation by array chromosomal genome hybridization (aCGH) that no major structural variants were introduced into the Cre-expressing hES cell line during editing, the cells were then differentiated into hGPCs using our described protocols[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR35 "Wang, S. et al. Human iPSC-derived oligodendrocyte progenitor cells can myelinate and rescue a mouse model of congenital hypomyelination. Cell Stem Cell 12, 252–264 (2013)."). At 140–160 days in vitro, the hGPCs were transplanted (2 × 105 cells per brain, in four divided injections of 5 × 104 cells each) into the forebrains of neonatal _Rag1_ _−/−_ immunodeficient mice; this technique allows the large-scale replacement of mouse by hGPCs, yielding chimeras in which most GPCs and large numbers of their derived astrocytes are human[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR35 "Wang, S. et al. Human iPSC-derived oligodendrocyte progenitor cells can myelinate and rescue a mouse model of congenital hypomyelination. Cell Stem Cell 12, 252–264 (2013)."), [36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR36 "Windrem, M. S. et al. Neonatal chimerization with human glial progenitor cells can both remyelinate and rescue the otherwise lethally hypomyelinated shiverer mouse. Cell Stem Cell 2, 553–565 (2008)."), [37](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR37 "Windrem, M. S. et al. A competitive advantage by neonatally engrafted human glial progenitors yields mice whose brains are chimeric for human glia. J. Neurosci. 34, 16153–16161 (2014)."), whereas few if any differentiate as neurons (Extended Data Fig. [1a–d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig7)). We generated six human glial chimeric mice for this set of experiments that were colonized with hGPCs expressing Cre recombinase under _PDGFRA_ control. Histological assessment of two of the hGPC-engrafted mice at 14 weeks of age confirmed widespread engraftment by human donor cells, and fluorescence in situ hybridization using RNAScope confirmed the expression of Cre recombinase mRNA by these cells (Extended Data Figs. [1e–h](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig7)).

### Hypertonic intracisternal delivery of AAV5 achieves widespread parenchymal access

We next asked whether we might use glymphatic pathways to access perivascular routes on the brain side of the BBB so as to efficiently deliver AAV5 directly to the brain parenchyma. The glymphatic system comprises the network of perivascular channels by which CSF enters and pervades the brain and then exchanges with brain interstitial fluid to clear metabolic and protein wastes from the brain parenchyma[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR25 "Iliff, J. J. et al. A paravascular pathway facilitates CSF flow through the brain parenchyma and the clearance of interstitial solutes, including amyloid β. Sci. Transl. Med. 4, 147ra111 (2012)."), [38](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR38 "Iliff, J. J., Goldman, S. A. & Nedergaard, M. Implications of the discovery of brain lymphatic pathways. Lancet Neurol. 14, 977–979 (2015)."). Previous work showed that glymphatic influx pathways may be used for compound delivery, just as its efflux pathways mediate metabolite clearance[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR27 "Plog, B. A. et al. Transcranial optical imaging reveals a pathway for optimizing the delivery of immunotherapeutics to the brain. JCI Insight 3, e120922 (2018)."). Therefore, we asked whether the glymphatic network might also serve as a route for viral delivery, circumventing the BBB to allow direct parenchymal entry. We first assessed the utility and perivascular flow patterns of viral delivery through injection into the cisterna magna, which permits direct access to the glymphatic system through anterograde CSF flow[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR30 "Xavier, A. L. R. et al. Cannula implantation into the cisterna magna of rodents. J. Vis. Exp. 135, e57378 (2018)."). Critically, we paired intracisternal viral injection with both the systemic delivery of hypertonic saline and head-down prone (Trendelenburg) positioning, which together greatly increase glymphatic influx[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR27 "Plog, B. A. et al. Transcranial optical imaging reveals a pathway for optimizing the delivery of immunotherapeutics to the brain. JCI Insight 3, e120922 (2018)."). We first used FITC-coupled dextran as a tracer by which to validate the efficacy of this delivery approach and observed widespread tracer entry throughout the forebrain (Extended Data Fig. [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig8)).

We next evaluated the utility of this pathway for viral administration. We assessed forebrain infection following the intracisternal hypertonic delivery of both an unmodified AAV5-serotyped AAV2 inverted terminal repeat (ITR)-based vector (AAV2/5 (ref. [39](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR39 "Louboutin, J. P., Wang, L. & Wilson, J. M. Gene transfer into skeletal muscle using novel AAV serotypes. J. Gene Med. 7, 442–451 (2005).")), hereafter referred to as AAV5) and a modified AAV5 into whose VP1 capsid protein we inserted the neurotropic capsid sequence of AAV-retrograde (AAVrg)[40](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR40 "Tervo, D. G. et al. A designer AAV variant permits efficient retrograde access to projection neurons. Neuron 92, 372–382 (2016)."), yielding AAV5-retro. Using our injection protocol, we observed widespread parenchymal infection by each of these control viruses. AAV5-retro particularly infected high proportions of hippocampal pyramidal and corticofugal neurons following a single intracisternal injection (Extended Data Fig. [3a–c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig9)), yet unmodified AAV5 similarly infected primarily neurons; both control vectors exhibited relatively sparse glial labeling (Extended Data Fig. [3d–f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig9)). Together, these experiments indicated that intracisternal administration of AAV5 under hypertonic conditions enables its rapid, brain-wide perivascular spread, circumventing the BBB to allow direct viral entry into the brain with widespread, efficient cellular transduction. They also confirm prior observations that both unmodified AAV5 and modified AAVs bearing the retro capsid insert are primarily neurotropic[41](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR41 "Castle, M. J., Turunen, H. T., Vandenberghe, L. H. & Wolfe, J. H. Controlling AAV tropism in the nervous system with natural and engineered capsids. Methods Mol. Biol. 1382, 133–149 (2016).").

### AAV5 capsids may be evolved to target hGPCs in vivo

Once we established chimeric mice whose incorporated hGPCs expressed Cre recombinase (Fig. [1a–c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig1)) and optimized a strategy for delivering AAVs bearing recombination-sensitive sequences, we next injected the human glial chimeric mice intracisternally with a viral library of candidate AAV5 capsids (10 µl at 1 × 1012 viral genome copies (vg) per ml, 1 × 1010 vg per injection). The library was generated by the ligation of a random sequence of 21 nt inserted after Q574 of VP1, a recognition loop on the sialic-acid-receptor-binding pocket, so as to randomly modify the binding specificity of the capsid, as previously described[42](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR42 "Wang, Y. et al. Directed evolution of adeno-associated virus 5 capsid enables specific liver tropism. Mol. Ther. Nucleic Acids 28, 293–306 (2022)."). We inserted a GCT codon (alanine) between Q574 and S575 of the AAV5 VP1 capsid to introduce an NheI cut site for insertion of the heptamer-encoding sequences of the library; thus, the engineered AAVs carried random Cap sequences mediating their binding specificity. As such, each of these viral candidates expressed a random heptapeptide insert in the VP1-binding domain, thereby permuting its differential adhesion to target cells. We estimated the resultant library to encode 4 × 105 of these capsid variants. Importantly, the resultant AAV Cap sequences were placed upstream to two poly(A) tails, BGH and SV40 poly(A)sequences inserted in opposite directions, both flanked by two different Lox sequences (Lox66 and Lox71); this allowed the viral DNA to be detected by PCR as either unperturbed or as the product of Cre-dependent reorientation using PCR primers within each poly(A) sequence (Fig. [1d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig1)). In virally infected PDGFRA-Cre-expressing hGPCs, the recombination of these sites allowed for the specific amplification of those viruses that successfully transduced the human cells, while viruses that infected mouse cells remained unchanged.

As noted, 10 µl of this viral capsid library (1 × 1012 vg per ml) were then injected intracisternally into each of four 16-week-old mice, after inducing systemic hypertonicity with an intraperitoneal infusion of NaCl (1 M or 5.8%; 20 µl g−1 body weight), with each mouse in a prone Trendelenburg position as described above (Fig. [1e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig1)). The mice were killed 1 week after viral delivery and their forebrain viral sequences were assessed by high-throughput sequencing following PCR using two sets of primers: one corresponding to the rearranged virion DNA resulting from recombination in hGPCs, where PDGFRA-driven Cre was active at the time of infection, and a second set corresponding to the native viral DNA sequence, which remained unchanged in the infected mouse cells. Importantly, to exclude viral capsids with notable off-target systemic affinities, the liver was also isolated from the infected mice, gDNA was extracted and those capsids that infected the liver were separately assessed by PCR for each mouse (Fig. [1f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig1)). This step allowed us to define and exclude those capsid variant sequences that targeted both the liver and the brain, regardless of their relative affinity for hGPCs. We thereby identified those capsids most selective for human brain cells and for hGPCs and their derivatives in particular while manifesting the least off-target systemic hepatic infection (Fig. [1g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig1) and Supplementary Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#MOESM3)). Choosing the top five most human glia-enriched capsids recovered from each mouse allowed us to identify a set of 20 different capsid variants, which we designated CM1–CM20, that efficiently infected GPCs and exhibited differential infectivity of hGPCs relative to mouse (Extended Data Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Tab1)).

### A family of capsid-evolved AAV5s selectively infect GPCs and astrocytes

A number of glial ectodomains are coexpressed by astrocytes and their progenitors; accordingly, they may also be recognized by viruses that infect PDGFRA-expressing GPCs. As a result, we found that our hGPC-selective clones manifested some degree of astrocytic targeting as well: some minimal, such as CM1 (Fig. [2a–e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig2)), and some profoundly so, such as CM6, which selectively targeted both GPCs and astrocytes efficiently (Fig. [2f,g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig2)). Focusing our efforts on these two vectors, after initial screening that identified them as transducing mouse and human glia, we found that, while some neuronal transduction was exhibited by each, both demonstrated marked gliotropism relative to the unmodified AAV5s from which they were derived (Fig. [2i–k](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig2)). In contrast, in controls injected with unmodified WT AAV5, almost 50% of infected cells were neurons (Extended Data Fig. [4a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig10)); thus, both AAV-CM1 and AAV-CM6 exhibited significantly more avidity for glia than did WT AAV5 ( _n_ = 3–4 mice per group; _P_ < 0.0001, one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with post hoc Tukey’s _t_-tests). Neuronal infection by these AAV5-derived vectors was quantitatively weak; in the same brains and regions in which glial infection was scored, <1% of all neurons were infected (Extended Data Fig. [4b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig10)). Together, these data serve to highlight both the relative gliotropism and the greater net glial infectivity of our engineered vectors relative to unmodified AAV5.

### Capsid-evolved AAVs were filtered to exclude those manifesting systemic infection

The off-target infection of systemic organs has been a notable source of AAV-associated toxicity[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR19 "Hinderer, C. et al. Severe toxicity in nonhuman primates and piglets following high-dose intravenous administration of an adeno-associated virus vector expressing human SMN. Hum. Gene Ther. 29, 285–298 (2018)."), [20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR20 "Arjomandnejad, M., Dasgupta, I., Flotte, T. R. & Keeler, A. M. Immunogenicity of recombinant adeno-associated virus (AAV) vectors for gene transfer. BioDrugs 37, 311–329 (2023)."), [21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR21 "Duan, D. Lethal immunotoxicity in high-dose systemic AAV therapy. Mol. Ther. 31, 3123–3126 (2023)."), [22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR22 "Gardin, A. & Ronzitti, G. Current limitations of gene therapy for rare pediatric diseases: lessons learned from clinical experience with AAV vectors. Arch. Pediatr. 30, 8S46–48S52 (2023)."), [23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR23 "Guo, Y. et al. High-titer AAV disrupts cerebrovascular integrity and induces lymphocyte infiltration in adult mouse brain. Mol. Ther. Methods Clin. Dev. 31, 101102 (2023)."), [24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR24 "Ling, Q., Herstine, J. A., Bradbury, A. & Gray, S. J. AAV-based in vivo gene therapy for neurological disorders. Nat. Rev. Drug Discov. 22, 789–806 (2023)."). Therefore, to minimize the possibility of non-CNS infection, we screened our vectors to identify those with the least infection of systemic tissues. To this end, we included PCR screening for viral sequence of the livers of each animal in which viral candidates were assessed, in light of that organ’s frequent off-target infection by AAVs and associated toxicity. The hepatic viral genome concentrations were compared to those of the targeted brains of the same animals to assess the degree of off-target infection by each of our candidate capsid variants. All candidates manifesting appreciable hepatic infection were excluded from further analysis; this step served as a gatekeeper to identify those capsid variants appropriate for further development and in vivo assessment. Two of these vectors in particular, CM1 and CM6, appeared to allow phenotype-restricted delivery of reporter transgenes to brain glia in vivo, with minimal visceral infection. When delivered intracisternally, these capsid variants exhibited little or no detectable infection of the systemic tissues assessed, which included the liver, spleen and kidneys (Extended Data Fig. [5a–d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig11)), as well as the dorsal root ganglia (Extended Data Fig. [5e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig11)). In addition, the levels of systemic viremia were far lower in mice injected intracisternally than intravenously, suggesting the much greater efficiency of CNS viral distribution using _trans_-glymphatic delivery, which may have contributed further to the minimization of systemic off-target infection. Interestingly, the plasma level of AAV-CM1 was found to be significantly greater than that of WT AA5 (indicating less systemic uptake) when assessed at the same point after direct intravenous administration of equivalent viral doses (Extended Data Fig. [5f](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig11)). Similarly, when hepatocytes were exposed to equivalent doses of AAV-CM1 and WT AAV5 in vitro (2.5 × 105 viral particles per ml, corresponding to the viral genome level detected in mouse plasma following intracisternal delivery), significantly less hepatocellular infection was noted by AAV-CM1 than by WT AAV5 (Extended Data Fig. [5g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig11)). Together, these data indicate that hGPC-targeted capsid-modified AAV5s, in particular our prototypic vectors CM1 and CM6, exhibit substantially greater glial infectivity than wild-type AAV5, with lesser albeit persistent infectivity of neuronal populations and little if any off-target visceral infection.

### Capsids evolved on hGPCs exhibited efficient human glial infectivity in vivo

The data described thus far describe the relative infectivities of our hGPC capsid-evolved vectors on mouse glia in nonchimeric mice; these experiments demonstrate the marked gliotropism of these vectors relative to the wild-type, unmodified AAV5 from which our variants were derived. We next asked whether these vectors could efficiently target hGPCs and their derivatives in vivo and, if so, whether human cells were selectively infected relative to their mouse counterparts. To that end, we compared the glial targeting efficacy of our capsid-modified AAV5-CM1 to that of wild-type (WT) unmodified AAV5 (AAV2/5) and did so in human glial cells in vivo. In particular, we engineered both WT AAV5 and AAV5-CM1 vectors to express distinct fluorescent reporters in a Cre-dependent manner and then directly compared the relative infectivities of these viruses in hGPCs in vivo, in PDGFRA-Cre human glial chimeric mice. For this purpose, the flex-EGFP reporter[43](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR43 "Schnutgen, F. et al. A directional strategy for monitoring Cre-mediated recombination at the cellular level in the mouse. Nat. Biotechnol. 21, 562–565 (2003).") was packaged into AAV-CM1, while the flex-dtTomato reporter was packaged into WT AAV5 (Fig. [3a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig3)); the two reporter viruses were prepared in equal concentrations and equal volumes were then mixed together. A total of 10 µl (1 × 1012 vg per ml) of this 1:1 mixture was then injected into the cisterna magna of 12-month-old mice ( _n_ = 3), each of which were neonatally chimerized with PDGFRA-Cre-expressing hGPCs. Then, 3 weeks later, the mice were killed and the relative expression of each vector was assessed in the engrafted human cells (Fig. [3b,c](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig3)). This analysis revealed that AAV-CM1-EGFP transduced a significantly and substantially higher proportion of hGPCs than did AAV5-dtTomato (Fig. [3d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig3)), confirming the superior glial avidity of the AAV-CM1 vector for human glia in vivo.

Having established the enhanced tropism of AAV-CM1 for human glia in vivo relative to wild-type AAV, we next assessed the respective phenotypic biases of our most efficient vectors, CM1 and CM6. We again used adult human glial chimeric mice that were perinatally engrafted with hGPCs produced from unmodified hES cells (line WA09) to assess the avidity of these vectors; we did so by injecting the viruses intracisternally at 16 weeks of age with either AAV-CM1 or AAV-CM6 and killing them 3 weeks later to assess both their transduction efficiencies and preferred phenotypic targets. We found that CM1 exhibited significant bias toward hGPC and oligodendroglial lineage infection, while CM6 was especially tropic for astrocytes (Fig. [4a–c,e–g](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig4)). In the corpus callosum, AAV-CM1 transduced 5.3-fold more human Olig2+-defined glia than did the parental WT AAV5; 34.9% ± 5.7% of all human Olig2+ glia in the scored field were infected by a single injection of AAV-CM1, whereas only 6.5% ± 1.1% of these cells were infected in WT AAV5-injected mice ( _n_ = 4 per group, means ± s.e.m.; _P_ = 0.0014, two-way ANOVA with Šidák’s multiple-comparison test; Fig. [4d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig4)). In contrast, AAV-CM6 transduced 3.8-fold more Sox9+-defined astroglia than did WT AAV5 (AAV-CM6: 22.2% ± 3.9%, AAV5: 5.9% ± 2.6%; _n_ = 4 per group, means ± s.e.m.; _P_ = 0.002, two-way ANOVA; Fig. [4h](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig4)). Thus, while neither CM1 nor CM6 was exclusive for its preferred target, their respective very strong biases towards hGPCs and astrocytes were each highly significant.

The phenotypic selectivity of these vectors was notable in that each exhibited greater tropism to human than mouse cells of each phenotype (Fig. [5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig5)). To assess the human selectivity of these vectors, over and above their glial tropism, we assessed the relative infection of mouse GPCs and hGPCs in the corpus callosa of chimeric _shiverer_ mice ( _MBP_ _shi/shi_ × _rag2_ _−/−_) that were neonatally engrafted, injected with AAV-CM1 at 16–17 weeks and killed 3 weeks later. In these immunodeficient and myelin-deficient mice, hGPCs competitively dominate the host mouse GPCs[37](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR37 "Windrem, M. S. et al. A competitive advantage by neonatally engrafted human glial progenitors yields mice whose brains are chimeric for human glia. J. Neurosci. 34, 16153–16161 (2014)."), such that just over half (52.2% ± 6.0%, _n_ = 3) of all Olig2-defined hGPCs and oligodendroglia were of human origin at the time of killing, consistent with our past studies of hGPC colonization of the neonatally engrafted _shiverer_ brain[44](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR44 "Windrem, M. S. et al. Human glial progenitor cells effectively remyelinate the demyelinated adult brain. Cell Rep. 31, 107658 (2020).") (Fig. [5a–d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig5)). We found that, of all Olig2-expressing cells in the scored corpus callosa, 87% ± 3.3% of the infected cells were human and 13% ± 3% were mouse ( _n_ = 3 mice per group; _P_ < 0.0001, _t_-test; Fig. [5d](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig5)). After normalization for the incidence of GPCs from each species in the chimeric white matter, we found that of all human Olig2-expressing cells in the scored corpus callosa, 30.2% ± 1.1% were infected by CM1. In contrast, just 5.0% ± 0.2% of Olig2+ mouse cells were infected ( _n_ = 3 mice per group; _P_ < 0.001, _t_-test; Fig. [5e](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig5)). As such, when coresident in the same regions of corpus callosum in roughly the same incidence, the hGPCs were roughly sixfold more likely to be infected by AAV-CM1 than were mouse cells. Thus, this hGPC-evolved vector manifested strong selective tropism for human GPCs relative to mouse GPCs.

### CM1 exhibited marked infection of mature oligodendrocytes and their progenitors

In light of the efficient infection by AAV5-CM1 of hGPCs and Olig+ oligodendroglial lineage cells broadly defined, we next asked whether this vector could efficiently infect mature oligodendrocytes, an especially important clinical target for which few competent delivery vectors have been described and even then only in rodent models. To this end, we established mice with humanized white matter through neonatal hGPC chimerization of immunodeficient _shiverer_ mice, as noted above _;_ this model permits the robust oligodendrocytic maturation of their engrafted hGPCs[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR35 "Wang, S. et al. Human iPSC-derived oligodendrocyte progenitor cells can myelinate and rescue a mouse model of congenital hypomyelination. Cell Stem Cell 12, 252–264 (2013)."), [36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR36 "Windrem, M. S. et al. Neonatal chimerization with human glial progenitor cells can both remyelinate and rescue the otherwise lethally hypomyelinated shiverer mouse. Cell Stem Cell 2, 553–565 (2008)."). When the mice reached 16–17 weeks of age, by which time they begin to exhibit initial myelination by the hGPC-derived oligodendrocytes, AAV5-CM1 was delivered intracisternally after induction of systemic hypertonicity. When killed 3 weeks later, robust infection of myelinating human oligodendrocytes, as well as parenchymal astrocytes and persistent hGPCs, was noted in the corpus callosum and major forebrain white-matter tracts (Fig. [6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig6)). These data indicate the potential for therapeutic transgene delivery by AAV5-CM1 to mature myelinated oligodendroglia and their progenitors.
