## Discussion

GPCs are an important target for gene therapy; in addition to being functionally important cells in their own right[45](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR45 "Lin, S. C. & Bergles, D. E. Synaptic signaling between GABAergic interneurons and oligodendrocyte precursor cells in the hippocampus. Nat. Neurosci. 7, 24–32 (2004)."), [46](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR46 "de Biase, L. M., Nishiyama, A. & Bergles, D. E. Excitability and synaptic communication within the oligodendrocyte lineage. J. Neurosci. 30, 3600–3611 (2010)."), they can give rise to both oligodendrocytes and astrocytes and, under select circumstances, neurons as well[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR15 "Nunes, M. C. et al. Identification and isolation of multipotential neural progenitor cells from the subcortical white matter of the adult human brain. Nat. Med. 9, 439–447 (2003)."), [47](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR47 "Belachew, S. et al. Postnatal NG2 proteoglycan-expressing progenitor cells are intrinsically multipotent and generate functional neurons. J. Cell Biol. 161, 169–186 (2003)."). While AAVs have been evolved to target a number of cell types of the adult CNS, most such efforts have focused on mouse cells; among those studies focused on human cells, most were necessarily conducted in vitro, as few systems appropriate for targeting human brain cells in vivo have been developed. In this work, we report the development of a set of AAVs, evolved from AAV serotype AAV5, that selectively and efficiently infect hGPCs and their derivatives in vivo, with little off-target CNS or systemic infection. To do so, we selected candidate viruses in vivo, introducing libraries of randomly mutagenized, Cre recombinase-reported AAV5s intracisternally into mice that were neonatally chimerized with Cre recombinase-expressing hGPCs. This approach combined the CREATE strategy[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR6 "Deverman, B. E. et al. Cre-dependent selection yields AAV variants for widespread gene transfer to the adult brain. Nat. Biotechnol. 34, 204–209 (2016).") with human glial chimeric brains[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR36 "Windrem, M. S. et al. Neonatal chimerization with human glial progenitor cells can both remyelinate and rescue the otherwise lethally hypomyelinated shiverer mouse. Cell Stem Cell 2, 553–565 (2008)."), [37](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR37 "Windrem, M. S. et al. A competitive advantage by neonatally engrafted human glial progenitors yields mice whose brains are chimeric for human glia. J. Neurosci. 34, 16153–16161 (2014).") as our platform for in vivo viral selection. We achieved robust glial infection in these mice using delivery through the perivascular glymphatic system[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR25 "Iliff, J. J. et al. A paravascular pathway facilitates CSF flow through the brain parenchyma and the clearance of interstitial solutes, including amyloid β. Sci. Transl. Med. 4, 147ra111 (2012)."), [48](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR48 "Nedergaard, M. & Goldman, S. A. Glymphatic failure as a final common pathway to dementia. Science 370, 50–56 (2020).") to avoid the BBB and, hence, enable efficient parenchymal entry of our engineered viruses using a clinically feasible strategy.

The hGPCs in these chimerized mice were derived from hES cells that were edited to express Cre recombinase under the regulatory control of the PDGFRA promoter, such that only hGPCs, which specifically express PDGFRA[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR33 "Sim, F. J. et al. CD140a identifies a population of highly myelinogenic, migration-competent and efficiently engrafting human oligodendrocyte progenitor cells. Nat. Biotechnol. 29, 934–941 (2011)."), expressed Cre. We injected the resultant human glial chimeras intracisternally with a library of AAVs, each of which expressed a random heptapeptide insertion into an AAV-binding domain that regulates target cell adhesion. Each harbored a BGH poly(A) sequence followed by an inverted SV40 poly(A) sequence, flanked by distinct Lox sequences (Lox66 and Lox71), which, upon recombination in PDGFRA-Cre-expressing cells, allowed the amplification of those viruses that successfully transduced hGPCs. This approach identified those variants that selectively infected hGPCs and their derivatives in vivo; the exclusion of some of these on the basis of their infection of host liver then allowed the selection of gliotropic viruses that exhibited minimal infection of nonneural or other systemic phenotypes.

The relative lack of systemic infectivity by these vectors, especially in the setting of _trans_-glymphatic delivery, is especially important. While there is scant literature concerning gliotropic AAVs, a number of neurotropic AAVs that have advanced to trials, including those modified from AAV9 that cross the BBB, have been associated with notable toxicity, largely because of adventitious infection of the liver and dorsal root ganglia, yielding hepatic dysfunction and pain syndromes respectively[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR19 "Hinderer, C. et al. Severe toxicity in nonhuman primates and piglets following high-dose intravenous administration of an adeno-associated virus vector expressing human SMN. Hum. Gene Ther. 29, 285–298 (2018)."). This toxicity may be the product of the high systemic doses need to achieve BBB passage and the large fraction of those doses that adventitiously infect off-target tissue, such as the liver and spleen, with consequent immune activation. Our use of glymphatic delivery to circumvent the BBB allows much lower viral doses, thus mitigating toxicity. While residual viral escape from the CNS might still permit viral access to the systemic circulation and, hence, peripheral tissues, we found that for our selected vectors CM1 and CM6, visceral infection was so minimal as to be scarcely detectable (Extended Data Fig. [5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig11)).

Within the brain, some persistent neuronal infection by our glial-evolved capsid variants was not unexpected. While our capsid modifications included the addition of random sequences with selection for those that bound bipotential GPCs, we did not delete or mutagenize the remaining VP1 sequence or modify its other exposed loops, which are together responsible for its neuronal avidity. This added strong glial tropism to these vectors, without necessarily removing neuronal infectivity; our filtration against liver excluded variants cross-reactive with non-CNS tissues but still allowed variants that retained infectivity for CNS neurons. As a large proportion of potential neurological disease targets involve both neuronal and glial pathology, this may prove a desirable feature in many clinical scenarios. However, in some disorders, glial specificity might be preferred or required. In these cases, further work to selectively remove the neuronal sequences on VP1 may be needed to create vectors whose range of infectivity is entirely restricted to glia. Alternatively, cell-type-selective regulatory elements may be added to further specify gene expression to glial phenotypes of interest. In this regard, a number of enhancers have been identified that can direct gene expression to astrocytes and/or oligodendrocytes[49](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR49 "Mich, J.K., et al. Enhancer-AAVs allow genetic access to oligodendrocytes and diverse populations of astrocytes across species. eLife                  https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.108640.1                                 (2025)."), whose pairing with capsids permitting cell-type-selective infection may provide especially robust vectors for safe clinical use.

To provide these vectors to the brain in as widespread and efficient a manner possible, while minimizing dose and systemic spread, we developed a multifaceted approach to enhance their glymphatic delivery. First, we combined AAV administration with systemic hypertonic saline, a strategy previously shown to improve CNS delivery of antibodies by transiently increasing CSF influx along perivascular spaces[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR27 "Plog, B. A. et al. Transcranial optical imaging reveals a pathway for optimizing the delivery of immunotherapeutics to the brain. JCI Insight 3, e120922 (2018)."), [50](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR50 "Salegio, E. A., Hancock, K. & Korszen, S. Pre-clinical delivery of gene therapy products to the cerebrospinal fluid: challenges and considerations for clinical translation. Front. Mol. Neurosci. 16, 1248271 (2023)."), [51](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR51 "Chen, X. et al. Biodistribution of adeno-associated virus gene therapy following cerebrospinal fluid-directed administration. Hum. Gene Ther. 34, 94–111 (2023)."). In addition, we positioned the mice in the Trendelenburg position after injection, leveraging gravity to facilitate rostral distribution of the vector. This method aligns with previous findings[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR32 "Castle, M. J., Cheng, Y., Asokan, A. & Tuszynski, M. H. Physical positioning markedly enhances brain transduction after intrathecal AAV9 infusion. Sci. Adv. 4, eaau9859 (2018).") demonstrating that maintaining rodents in Trendelenburg for 2 h after intrathecal AAV9 infusion greatly increased gene transfer to cortical and basal forebrain regions, with a >15-fold increase in neuronal transduction relative to upright controls. Our combination of intracisternal viral delivery, systemic hyperosmotic modulation and strategic physical positioning substantially enhanced infection throughout the brain parenchyma. Of note, our approach complements earlier studies using AQP4-knockout mice, which exhibit impaired glymphatic clearance, leading to the increased retention of AAV vectors within the brain following intracerebroventricular administration[52](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR52 "Murlidharan, G., Crowther, A., Reardon, R. A., Song, J. & Asokan, A. Glymphatic fluid transport controls paravascular clearance of AAV vectors from the brain. JCI Insight 1, e88034 (2016)."). Together, these findings underscore the importance of integrating physiological manipulations with delivery strategies so as to optimize CNS-targeted gene therapy.

An increasing number of neurological disorders are being recognized as either being glial in etiology or having contributory glial pathology. These include not only the pediatric enzymatic and storage disorders, but also the adult myelin disorders, such as PMS and age-related white-matter loss, for which hGPCs and their oligodendrocytic progeny are clear potential targets of glial-directed AAVs. In addition, hGPC-targeted vectors may selectively infect neoplastic stem and progenitor cells within malignant glial tumors, as human glioma cells may arise from resident hGPCs and express high levels of the PDGFA receptor[53](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR53 "Persson, A. I. et al. Non-stem cell origin for oligodendroglioma. Cancer Cell 18, 669–682 (2010)."). More broadly, selectively gliotropic vectors may find value in treating neurodegenerative disorders, given the importance of astrocytic and oligodendroglial pathology in diseases as diverse as HD, schizophrenia, Parkinson disease and Alzheimer disease[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR3 "Huynh, N. P. T. et al. Shared patterns of glial transcriptional dysregulation link Huntington’s disease and schizophrenia. Brain 147, 3099–3112 (2024)."), [54](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR54 "Depp, C. et al. Myelin dysfunction drives amyloid-β deposition in models of Alzheimer’s disease. Nature 618, 349–357 (2023)."), [55](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR55 "Kenigsbuch, M. et al. A shared disease-associated oligodendrocyte signature among multiple CNS pathologies. Nat. Neurosci. 25, 876–886 (2022)."), [56](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR56 "Nasrabady, S. E., Rizvi, B., Goldman, J. E. & Brickman, A. M. White matter changes in Alzheimer’s disease: a focus on myelin and oligodendrocytes. Acta Neuropathol. Commun. 6, 22 (2018)."), as reviewed previously[57](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR57 "Goldman, S. A. Glial evolution as a determinant of human behavior and its disorders. Ann. N. Y. Acad. Sci. 1471, 72–85 (2020)."). For each of these disorders, potential transcriptional targets have been identified, but the means of selectively and safely transducing a high proportion of the relevant glia in any given brain region in vivo, much less throughout the entire brain and CNS, has not previously been available. The vector selection system described here enables the discovery of AAVs targeting human glial phenotypes of interest in vivo. Furthermore, it advances a glymphatic delivery pathway that entails injection into the cisterna magna with concurrent systemic hypertonicity, a minimally invasive procedure that minimizes extracerebral viral spread while efficiently circumventing the BBB. This combined approach may enable the efficient and selective delivery of therapeutic transgenes to brain cells of interest in a clinically safe and effective manner.

## Methods

### Generation of PDGFRA-Cre hES cells

The ES cell line GENEA019 (GENEA Biocells; a female line) was used to generate hGPCs in this study. First, at the hES cell stage, Cre recombinase was inserted immediately downstream of the PDGFRA coding sequence and an intraribosomal entry site (IRES) to allow bicistronic expression. Donor DNA was generated by creating a plasmid containing 842 bp downstream and 875 bp upstream of the cut site for the homology arms. Between the homology arms and immediately after the stop codon, an IRES-Cre followed by both a puromycin selection gene under the PGK promoter and a polyadenylation sequence was added. The knock-in was performed by HDR-mediated insertion of the donor DNA, with the sgRNA targeting the PDGFRA gene using the following sequence, 5′-ctgtaactggcggattcgag-3′. This was targeted immediately after the stop of the coding sequence (Fig. [1a](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Fig1)) and the sgRNA was inserted downstream of a U6 promoter in the pSpCas9(BB)-2A-Puro (PX459) plasmid (gift from F. Zhang; Addgene, 62988)[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR34 "Ran, F. A. et al. Genome engineering using the CRISPR–Cas9 system. Nat. Protoc. 8, 2281–2308 (2013)."). This plasmid contains spCas9 expressed under a CBH promoter. Both the donor and the guide/Cas9 plasmid were electroporated into undifferentiated GENEA019 hES cells, as we previously described[58](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR58 "Vieira, R. et al. Young glial progenitor cells competitively replace aged and diseased human glia in the adult chimeric mouse brain. Nat. Biotechnol. 42, 719–730 (2024)."). Briefly, hES cells were collected as a single-cell suspension following dissociation with Accutase (StemCell Technologies, 07920), washed in culture medium and counted with the automated cell counter NucleoCounter NC-200 (ChemoMetec). Per electroporation, a total of 1.5 × 106 cells were mixed with 5 µg of the AAVS1 targeting CRISPR–Cas9 plasmid (pXAT2) and 5 µg of reporter donor plasmid (pAAVS1-P-CAG-mCherry or pAAVS1-P-CAG-EGFP). pXAT2 (Addgene, plasmid 80494), pAAVS1-P-CAG-mCherry (Addgene, plasmid 80491) and pAAVS1-P-CAG-EGFP (Addgene, plasmid 80492) were gifts from K. Woltjen. Electroporation was performed using an Amaxa 4D-Nucleofector (Lonza) with the P3 primary cell kit (Lonza, V4XP-3024) according to the manufacturer’s guidelines. After nucleofection, the electroporated hES cell suspensions were transferred to 10-cm cell culture dishes and cultured with mTeSR1 supplemented with 10 µM Y-27632 (Tocris, 1254) for the first 24 h. Electroporated hES cells were grown for 48–72 h and then selected with puromycin (0.5 µg ml−1; Thermo Fisher, A1113803) until colonies were large enough to be picked up for expansion and genotyping. The genomic integrity of selected homozygous clones was assessed by karyotype and aCGH and a lack of major structural variants was confirmed before hGPC production, as described previously[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR35 "Wang, S. et al. Human iPSC-derived oligodendrocyte progenitor cells can myelinate and rescue a mouse model of congenital hypomyelination. Cell Stem Cell 12, 252–264 (2013).").

### Mouse models

For screening viruses in wild-type, nonchimeric mice, we purchased C57BL/6J mice from Jax Laboratories (Jackson Labs, 000664). For screening viruses in human glial chimeras, with an emphasis on targeting hGPCs and astrocytes, we used homozygous B6.129S7- _Rag1_ _tm_ 1M _om_/J ( _Rag1_ _−_/ _−_) immunodeficient mice (Jackson Labs, 002216), chimerized neonatally with hES cell GPCs; the brains of these mice typically exhibited widespread colonization hGPCs and their derived astrocytes.

For screening potential oligodendrocyte-tropic viruses, we used Rag2-null immunodeficient and myelin-deficient mice chimerized neonatally with hES cell hGPCs. These mice reliably produced a largely humanized white matter. For this purpose, we bred homozygous C3HeB/FeJ _MBP_ _shi/shi_ _shiverer_ mice (Jackson Laboratory, 001428) with homozygous C3H.129S6(B6)-Rag2tm1FwaN12 ( _rag2_ _−_/_−_) immunodeficient mice on the C3h background (Taconic, 000602-M) to produce myelin-deficient and immunodeficient mice, as described previously[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR36 "Windrem, M. S. et al. Neonatal chimerization with human glial progenitor cells can both remyelinate and rescue the otherwise lethally hypomyelinated shiverer mouse. Cell Stem Cell 2, 553–565 (2008).").

All mice were housed in a temperature and humidity-controlled environment (64–73 °F, 30–70% humidity) within a pathogen-free colony room on a 12:12-h light cycle. They were given ad libitum access to Modified ProLab RMH 3000, 5P00 containing 0.025% trimethoprim and 0.124% sulfamethoxazole (Mod LabDiet 5P00) and autoclaved acid water (pH 2.5–3.0).

### AAV production

#### AAV capsid library construction and amplification

To generate a viral library, we modified the M-CREATE strategy[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR6 "Deverman, B. E. et al. Cre-dependent selection yields AAV variants for widespread gene transfer to the adult brain. Nat. Biotechnol. 34, 204–209 (2016)."), [7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR7 "Kumar, S. R. et al. Multiplexed Cre-dependent selection yields systemic AAVs for targeting distinct brain cell types. Nat. Methods 17, 541–550 (2020)."). The plasmid to receive the 7-mer insert library was created using the backbone of the pAAV-MCS Expression Vector (Agilent, VPK-410). This backbone, including AAV2 ITRs, was fused to an expression cassette, in which a portion of the AAV2-P40-Rep2 was included for maintaining the expression of the capsid proteins and their proper splicing. The capsid sequence was modified by inserting a GCT codon (alanine) between Q574 and S575 of the AAV5 VP1 capsid to introduce an NheI cut site for insertion of a random Heptamer, as well as a silent mutation at L543 to destroy one of the XhoI sites and make it unique. The library was then created by amplifying the region between NheI and XhoI with AAV5-Cap-XhoI-Forward primer (5′-ccacgaccaataggatggagc-3′) and AAV5-Cap-NNN-NheI-Reverse primer (5′-gggcagtggtggagctagcNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNactagcctggttgttggtggc-3′). The PCR amplicon was then inserted into the capsid opened NheI and XhoI. Downstream of the Capsid gene, the construct also included two polyadenylation sequences, BGH (sense) and SV40 (anti-sense), both flanked by Lox66 and Lox71, to allow for a Cre-dependent single flip. The resulting library preps contained 4 × 105 variants by limiting dilution of the bacterial clones.

#### AAV2-Rep construct

To abolish the expression in _cis_ of the native capsid and generate viral particles exclusively from the engineered capsid library, pAAV2/5 (Addgene, 104964; donated by M. Fan) was subjected to PCR-directed mutagenesis followed by recombination-mediated ligation (In-Fusion, Takara). In this construct (pAAV2/5-Rep2-ΔCap5-3STOP), the expression of Cap proteins was abrogated by introduction of nonsense mutations at the start of each of VP1 (Glu12Ter), VP2 (Lys6Ter) and VP3 (Gln14Ter). All cloning was performed using Takara Bio’s In-Fusion snap assembly kit (638952).

#### Generating capsids with evolved peptide inserts

After enriched capsid variants were recovered, their 21-bp sequences were ordered as primers and ligated into AAV5 Rep-Cap containing the NheI and XhoI cut sites to create pAAV2/5-574-Empty-Recipient plasmid. Individual 21-bp heptamer coding sequences for each viral variant (that is, CM1 or CM6) were then inserted by PCR at the NheI end and ligated to the vector open NheI/XhoI to create a separate rep-cap plasmid for each variant.

#### Viral amplification

For amplification of the final AAV library preps, HEK293FT cells were cultured in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C using 10% FBS formulated in high-glucose DMEM (Thermo Fisher). Previously published protocols[59](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR59 "Challis, R. C. et al. Systemic AAV vectors for widespread and targeted gene delivery in rodents. Nat. Protoc. 14, 379–414 (2019).") were followed to produce the AAVs. Briefly, per viral prep, ten 150-mm plates of approximately 80% confluency HEK293FT cells were transfected with 0.4 mg of DNA-mix containing the Capsid, Helper (Addgene, 112867; a gift from J. Wilson) and AAV shuttle plasmids (pAAV-CAG-EGFP, pAAV-CAG-FLEX-EGFP and pAAV-CAG-FLEX-tdTomato; all gifts from E. Boyden; Addgene, 37825, 28306 and 28304, respectively) at a ratio of 0.6:0.3:0.1, along with PEI transfection reagent at a ratio of 1:4. The medium was replaced at 24 and 72 h after transfection and the cells and medium were harvested at 120 h. At both the 72-h and the 120-h time points, the medium was treated with polyethylene glycol for precipitating the virus. Cell pellets were lysed using salt active nuclease and the viral particles were separated by iodixanol density gradient solution spun using Type 60Ti rotor at 59,000 rpm for 3 h. The resulting viral band was further washed on an Amicon filter (30-kDa molecular weight cutoff; Millipore, UFC910024). The virus was then aliquoted and stored at −80 °C. Viral titers were established by way of a qPCR AAV titer kit (ABM, G931), as per the manufacturer’s instructions.

### Production of hGPCs and establishment of human glial chimeric mice

GPCs were produced from PDGFRA-Cre transduced hES cells (GENEA019; Genea Biocells) or from unmodified hES cells (WA09 line, WiCell Research Institute) using our previously described protocol[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR35 "Wang, S. et al. Human iPSC-derived oligodendrocyte progenitor cells can myelinate and rescue a mouse model of congenital hypomyelination. Cell Stem Cell 12, 252–264 (2013)."). At 130–140 days in vitro, hGPCs were collected in Ca2+/Mg2+-free Hanks’ balanced salt solution (HBSS−/−; Thermo Fisher, 14170112), then mechanically dissociated to small clusters by gentle pipetting, spun and resuspended in cold HBSS−/− at 1 × 105 cells per µl. Newborn mice (postnatal day 1) received bilateral intrastriatal injections of 1 µl of cell suspension (that is, 1 × 105 hGPCs per hemisphere) under cryoanesthesia, as described previously[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR35 "Wang, S. et al. Human iPSC-derived oligodendrocyte progenitor cells can myelinate and rescue a mouse model of congenital hypomyelination. Cell Stem Cell 12, 252–264 (2013)."), [36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR36 "Windrem, M. S. et al. Neonatal chimerization with human glial progenitor cells can both remyelinate and rescue the otherwise lethally hypomyelinated shiverer mouse. Cell Stem Cell 2, 553–565 (2008)."), [37](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR37 "Windrem, M. S. et al. A competitive advantage by neonatally engrafted human glial progenitors yields mice whose brains are chimeric for human glia. J. Neurosci. 34, 16153–16161 (2014)."). The injection tracks permitted donor cell infiltration and chimerization of the overlying corpus callosum and cortex.

### Cisternal viral delivery with systemic hypertonicity

At 12 weeks of age, the neonatally chimerized mice were anesthetized with ketamine (100 mg kg−1) and xylazine (10 mg kg−1) and monitored for anesthetic depth through cessation of toe pinch reflex. Mice were affixed in a stereotaxic frame and had their cisterna magna exposed. Surgical procedures were conducted as previously described[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR30 "Xavier, A. L. R. et al. Cannula implantation into the cisterna magna of rodents. J. Vis. Exp. 135, e57378 (2018)."). A 30-gauge cannula, connected by PE10 tubing to a 100-µl Hamilton syringe (gas-tight 1700 series), was implanted into the cisterna magna and secured with cyanoacrylate glue and dental cement. Then, 10 µl of high-titer (>1012 genome copies per ml) AAV injectate was then infused into the cisterna magna at 2 µl min−1 using a syringe micropump (Harvard Apparatus). Concurrent with infusion start, the mice were administered 20 μl g−1 body weight IP injection of 1 M hypertonic saline as previously described[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR27 "Plog, B. A. et al. Transcranial optical imaging reveals a pathway for optimizing the delivery of immunotherapeutics to the brain. JCI Insight 3, e120922 (2018)."). Once the infusion was complete, mice were removed from the stereotaxic frame, placed either flat or in a prone Trendelenburg position on a −45° slope for 30 min and then returned to their home cage. Anesthetic depth was maintained for 1 h following the time at which the infusion began. Animals were given analgesic and allowed to recover for either 7 or 21 days, depending on the experiment, at which time the mice were anesthetized and killed by transcardiac perfusion with 20 ml of cold PBS followed by 20 ml 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA). Tissues were then isolated and immersion-fixed in 4% PFA for an additional 2 h.

### AAV capsid identification

First, 1 week after injection, mice were killed and their livers, corpora callosa and striata were dissected. Genomic DNA was then extracted from either liver or pooled callosal and striatal cells using the Wizard SV Genomic DNA purification system (A2361). Next=generation sequencing libraries were generated using a nested PCR and PrimeSTAR GXL DNA polymerase (Takara Bio, R050A) to recover either recombined or nonrecombined viral genomes from the samples. The PCR consisted of ten cycles of outer PCR using the primers Cap-outer-Universal-Forward (5′-ctcatcaccagcgagagcg-3′) and either bGHPA-Reverse (5′-ccatagagcccaccgcatc-3′) or hGHPA-Recombined-Reverse (5′-cacaaataaagcactctggtatgacaag-3′) for nonrecombined and recombined sequences, respectively. After that, forward (5′-TCGTCGGCAGCGTCAGATGTGTATAAGAGACAGNNNNNNNNcgtacaacgtcggcgggcagat-3′) and reverse (5′-GTCTCGTGGGCTCGGAGATGTGTATAAGAGACAGNNNNNNNNggcacaggcgtgttcttgatgag-3′) primers with Illumina adaptors and sample indices added were spiked into the reactions and run for 25 more cycles to give each sample a unique barcode. These products were run on a 1% agarose gel and the expected 352-bp band was purified. Final samples were indexed using NexteraXT Indexing and sequencing on an Illumina MiSeq V2 Nano (150-bp paired-end reads).

Following sequencing and demultiplexing, all analysis was carried out in R version 4.2.3; the 21-bp DNA sequences, corresponding to the random heptamer coding sequences, were extracted on the basis of their flanking sequences. Each sample was then normalized by sequencing depth and converted into 7-aa peptide sequences using Biostrings version 2.66.0. Within individual animal libraries, recombined brain libraries were then evaluated for enrichment versus nonrecombined brain or liver libraries. From each mouse, the top five most enriched human-selective heptamers were used to generated viral particles for downstream testing. For the generation of Venn diagram intersections, capsid sequences with a greater than 98% organ specificity (defined as the percentage of an organ’s reads among all reads) were considered selective for hGPCs, mouse brain or mouse liver.

All code for our analysis of capsid identification and assignment may be found on GitHub ( [https://github.com/CTNGoldmanLab/hGPC\_AAV\_Capsid\_Evolution](https://github.com/CTNGoldmanLab/hGPC_AAV_Capsid_Evolution)).

### Tissue processing and immunohistochemistry

Mice were transcardially perfused with HBSS (Thermo Fisher Scientific) followed by 4% PFA in 0.1 M PBS at 3 weeks following AAV delivery. The brains, livers, spleens and kidneys were dissected, postfixed in 4% PFA for 2 h and stored in 1× PBS. Tissues for histology were cryopreserved in 0.1 M PBS + 30% sucrose before embedding in optimal cutting temperature (OCT) compound. Cryopreserved organs were sagittally sectioned into 20-μm-thick serial sections using a cryostat.

To quantify plasma viral concentrations, mice were first anesthetized and then given either an intracisternal or intravenous injection of 10 µl of virus at 1 × 1012 vg per ml. Then, 10 min later, the mice were exsanguinated through inferior vena cava blood draw, their blood was spun into heparinized tubes and their plasma was taken for PCR quantification. To quantify peripheral viral concentrations, the liver, kidney, spleen and dorsal root ganglia were collected 24 h after intracisternal or intravenous viral injection, after perfusion with 4% PFA.

Brain sections were blocked with 5% normal goat serum in 0.1 M PBS + 0.3% Triton-X for 1 h at room temperature and incubated overnight at 4 °C with primary antibodies against EGFP (chicken, 1:1,000; Thermo Fisher Scientific), human nuclear antigen (hNA; mouse, 1:400; Abcam ab254080), PDGFRA (rabbit, 1:600; Cell Signaling Technology, 5231S), Olig2 (rabbit, 1:500; Millipore), NeuN (rabbit, 1:800; Millipore), Sox9 (rabbit, 1:500; Abcam), myelin basic protein (MBP; 1:400; Abcam, ab40390), anti-dtTomato (1:400; Invitrogen, M11217), cyclic nucleotide diphosphatase (CNPase; 1:800; Abcam, ab18527), NeuN (1:400; Millipore, ABN78MI) or HA tag (mouse, 1:400; Invitrogen). Immunostaining was visualized with species-specific secondary antibodies conjugated with AlexaFluor-488, AlexaFluor-568 (1:400; Invitrogen) or AlexaFluor-647 (1:200; Invitrogen). Lastly, sections were coverslipped with mounting medium containing DAPI (Vector Laboratories). Slides were sealed and imaged with Olympus Slideview vs200 or a Leica DM6000B confocal microscope, and then analyzed using Stereo Investigator version 2019.

### qPCR

For reverse transcription PCR, tissues from spleen, kidney, liver and thoracic dorsal root ganglia were homogenized and DNA extracted using the Wizard genomic DNA purification kit (Promega) following the manufacturer’s protocol. qPCR was run using TaqMan probes for the 18S ribosomal gene and EGFP (Thermo Fisher, Mr04329676 and Mm03928990, respectively). For digital PCR, used for assessment of viral genomes in plasma, spun mouse plasma was incubated for 3 min at 25 °C using viral lysis buffer from the qPCR AAV titer kit to release the viral genomes (Applied Biological Materials, G931). Viral genome quantification was then performed using the Thermo Fisher QuantStudio AbsoluteQ dPCR instrument with a TaqMan probe for EGFP (Thermo Fisher, Mm03928990). All PCR data were analyzed in R as one-way ANOVAs with Tukey post hoc tests and visualized using ggplot2.

### Primary hepatocyte culture and AAV transduction

Human and mouse primary hepatocytes were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (human hepatocytes, PCS-450-010; mouse hepatocytes, AML12). Cells were maintained in HepatoXcell primary hepatocyte maintenance medium according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Hepatocytes were seeded at a density of 6.0 × 105 cells per well in poly(ornithine)-coated (Sigma-Aldrich, P4957-50ML) six-well tissue-culture-treated plates (Fisher Scientific, 07-200-83). Cells were transduced with AAV-CM1 or AAV5 in complete medium at a final concentration of 2.5 × 105 viral particles per ml, corresponding to the viral genome copy number detected in mouse plasma following cisterna magna injection. Cells were maintained for 5 days after transduction and harvested using TrypLE Select enzyme (Invitrogen, 12563-011); genomic DNA was isolated using the Wizard genomic DNA purification kit (Promega, PR-A2361).

### Fluorescence in situ hybridization

Mice (14 weeks-old) engrafted neonatally at PND1 with G19-derived PDGFRA-Cre GPCs were anesthetized and rapidly decapitated. Brains were then isolated, frozen in chilled isopentane, embedded in OCT (TissueTek), sectioned sagittally at 10 μm on a cryostat and stored at −80 °C. Slides with tissue sections were immersed in 4% PFA for 15 min, rinsed twice with 1× PBS and dehydrated at room temperature in increasing ethanol solutions: 50%, 70%, 100% and 100% for 5 min each. Using the RNAscope multiplex fluorescent reagent kit (323100, ACDBio), tissue sections were incubated in hydrogen peroxide for 10 min in a humidified box, rinsed with distilled water and incubated with protease III for 30 min at room temperature. Hybridization probes against Cre recombinase (312281-C1, ACDBio), human PDGFRa (604481-C2, ACDBio) and human Olig2 (424191-C3, ACDBio) or human NeuN (415591-C3, ACDBio) were then incubated for 2 h at 40°C. Control probes were also incubated in separate tissue sections. Probe signals were then serially amplified for 30 min at 40 °C each and individually developed starting with Opal 520 (1:750; FP1487001KT, PerkinElmer), followed by Opal 570 (1:750; FP1488001KT, PerkinElmer) and then Opal 690 (1:1500; FP1497001KT, PerkinElmer). Slides were coverslipped with Vectashield with DAPI for imaging.

### Statistics and reproducibility

All experiments were repeated as indicated by their sample sizes. Immunostainings were repeated in 3–5 separate animals. All statistical analyses were performed in R, as noted.

### Reporting summary

Further information on research design is available in the [Nature Portfolio Reporting Summary](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#MOESM1) linked to this article.

## Data availability

All raw sequencing data generated in this study were deposited to the National Center for Biotechnology Information under BioProject [PRJNA1389387](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/bioproject/?term=PRJNA1389387). [Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#Sec31) are provided with this paper.

## Code availability

All code for our analysis of capsid identification and assignment may be found on GitHub ( [https://github.com/CTNGoldmanLab/hGPC\_AAV\_Capsid\_Evolution](https://github.com/CTNGoldmanLab/hGPC_AAV_Capsid_Evolution)).

### Contributions

A.C. and A.B. designed and constructed the viral vectors and N.W. and W.B. raised and purified them. E.N. and A.C. conducted the intracisternal injections and optimized the glymphatic delivery approach together with M.N. A.B. and D.K. engineered the PDGFRA-Cre hES cells and X.L. and D.C.M. produced hGPCs from them. S.S. established the chimeric mice. W.B. performed the qPCR. A.C., E.N., J.D. and A.I. performed the immunohistology and quantitative imaging and J.C. performed the in situ hybridizations for Cre. R.S. and J.N.M. analyzed the PCR and genomic data. A.B., M.N. and S.A.G. designed the study and analyzed all data. S.A.G. wrote the paper, with input from all coauthors.

### [Extended Data Fig. 1 Colonization of mouse forebrain with human PDGFRA:Cre glia allows in vivo capsid selection.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2/figures/7)

**A**. Mice neonatally engrafted with hESC-derived PDGFRA:Cre-expressing glial progenitor cells (hGPCs) displayed robust colonization of the striatum by 14 weeks post-transplant. Human donor-derived cells either remained as hGPCs ( **B**) or differentiated into Sox9-expressing astrocytes ( **C**), but did not differentiate into neurons ( **D**). **E**. RNAscope multiplex in situ hybridization on striata of engrafted mice showed that the CRE-specific probe ( **F**) colocalized with PDGFRA ( **G**) but not with that of NeuN ( **H**). Scale: 100 µm ( **A**); 25 µm ( **D**, **E-H**).

### [Extended Data Fig. 2 Hypertonic intracisternal delivery of tracer achieves widespread brain access.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2/figures/8)

**A**. Experimental schema of cisterna magna CSF tracer infusions (FITC-dextran, 2,000 kDa, 2.5% w/v in saline; 10 µl delivered at 2 µl/min), done under ketamine/xylazine anesthesia with postural modification, osmotic manipulation via hypertonic saline (1 M, 20 µl/g), or both. **B**. Representative images of coronal brain slices with CSF tracer influx (2000kDa FITC-dextran) after being placed prone on a level surface (left, top) or a declined plane (left, bottom) until t = 30 minutes post infusion start. **C**. Quantification of fluorescent intensity of coronal sections shows no change in influx with postural modification alone (right, p = .1775). **D**. Representative coronal brain slices with CSF tracer influx (2000kDa FITC-dextran) receiving either physiologic saline (PS, _top_) or hypertonic saline (HTS, _bottom_). **E**. Fluorescence intensity reveals increased CSF tracer influx with hypertonic saline treatment ( _right panel_, p = .0005; PS, n = 5 mice, HTS, n = 6). **F**. Representative images of coronal brain slices with 2000kDa FITC-dextran influx comparing injection in prone Trendelenburg position on a declined plane ( _left_), vs. hypertonic saline alone ( _middle_), or both ( _right_). **G**. Fluorescence intensity shows increased influx with HTS, and a further increase when HTS is combined with postural modification. **C, E**, unpaired two-sided Students t-tests. **G**, Linear model with specified contrasts and MVT multiple comparison adjustment. For **C, E** and **G**, individual mean and standard deviation were plotted; each data point is an individual mouse (n = 5 level, 5 ramp, 5 PS, 6 HTS, 6 Ramp + HTS, biological replicates). Ramp refers to postural modification; HTS, hypertonic saline; PS, physiologic saline. Scale: 1 mm.

[Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#MOESM9)

### [Extended Data Fig. 3 Intracisternally-delivered AAV pervades the forebrain parenchyma via glymphatic spread.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2/figures/9)

To establish the efficacy of our intracisternal hypertonic delivery protocol using a well-described control vector before testing our modified capsid variants, we injected a set of adult mice with either AAV5-retro ( **A-B**) or WT AAV5 ( **C-E**). For the AAV5-retro injections, of **A-B**, we inserted the neurotropic retro cassette (encoding the peptide LADQDYTKTA) in between Q574 and S575 of the AAV5 VP1 capsid protein; this locus presents an exposed sialic acid-binding loop domain by which target specificity may be modified. A single intracisternal injection under systemic hypertonicity yielded high-efficiency infection of forebrain hippocampal pyramidal and selected corticofugal neurons, as has been described for parenchymally-administered AAV2-based AAV retro[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#ref-CR34 "Ran, F. A. et al. Genome engineering using the CRISPR–Cas9 system. Nat. Protoc. 8, 2281–2308 (2013)."). **C-E**, Similarly, intracisternal WT AAV5 control virus yielded predominantly neuronal labeling thought the forebrain, most prominently so in the hippocampus ( **D**) and cortex ( **E**). These results confirm that glymphatic-mediated intracisternal delivery supports efficient, brain-wide parenchymal access and neuronal gene transfer. Scale: **A, C**: 1 mm; **B, D**: 250 µm; **E**: 100 µm.

### [Extended Data Fig. 4 Adventitious neuronal infection persists in gliotropic variants.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2/figures/10)

Despite their strong gliotropism, some neuronal transduction persisted to a variable extent in each of these engineered viral variants. **A**, In both the neostriatum and the overlying neocortex of human glial chimeric brains, AAV-CM1 and AAV-CM6 each exhibited significantly less neuronal infection than the unmodified AAV5 from which they were derived. In contrast, in human glial chimeras injected with unmodified AAV5, almost 50% of infected cells were neurons. **B**, In the same brains and regions, less than 1% of all neurons were infected, and as noted in **A**, these comprised <20% of all cells infected by either CM1 or CM6. 2-way ANOVA with Tukey’s post-hoc tests (n = 3-4 biological replicates). Mean ± SEM.

[Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#MOESM10)

### [Extended Data Fig. 5 Selected capsid-evolved AAVs manifest little systemic infection.](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2/figures/11)

Using qPCR to assess viral genome concentrations (vg/g) allowed estimation of the degree of off-target infection. **A-D**. When assessed three weeks after intracisternal delivery, AAV-CM1 and AAV-CM6, exhibited little or no infection as shown either by EGFP expression by immunohistology (A) or QPCR of EGFP coding sequence from the liver **(B**), or of the kidney ( **C**) or spleen ( **D**), suggesting the strong phenotypic selectivity afforded by capsid evolution on hGPCs in vivo. **E**. Similarly, when assessed 24 hrs after intracisternal delivery, AAV-CM1 and AAV-CM6 exhibited little infection of dorsal root ganglia. **F**. Viral loads in plasma were markedly lower following intracisternal delivery, compared to intravenous administration, when assessed at 10 min after injection. Note that AAV-CM1 showed higher plasma levels than WT AAV5 10 min after direct IV injection, suggesting lesser systemic binding and absorption relative to WT AAV5. **G**. Similarly, AAV-CM1 showed significantly less tropism for cultured human and mouse hepatocytes than WT AAV5, as determined by qPCR 5 days after infection, using viral doses matched to plasma viral genome levels observed after cisternal delivery ( **F**). **B–F**, one-way ANOVA with Tukey post hoc tests (n = ; panel **G**, two-way ANOVA with Tukey post hoc tests (n = 3-7 biological replicates). Means ± SEM. Scale: 100 µm.

[Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03185-2#MOESM11)

title: Retargeting serine integrase Bxb1 via modular integrase (MINT) system enables precise large-DNA insertion at desired human genomic loci
summary: Sangamo Therapeutics researchers developed a Modular Integrase for Targeting (MINT) system that retargets the bacteriophage Bxb1 serine integrase to user-specified human genomic loci. Using structural modeling combined with single-round directed evolution and screening directly in human cells, the team engineered Bxb1 specificity away from its native attachment site toward the AAVS1safe harbor locus and the TRAC locus for T-cell engineering. In K562 cells, MINT constructs paired with activity-increasing Bxb1 mutants and zinc-finger DNA-binding domains achieved 29% integration efficiency at the AAVS1 locus and 35% at the TRAC locus. The system was further validated in primary human T cells with 29% GFP integration at TRAC, demonstrating clinical viability for non-viral genome editing.

### Subjects

- [DNA recombination](https://www.nature.com/subjects/dna-recombination)
- [Genetic engineering](https://www.nature.com/subjects/genetic-engineering)
- [Targeted gene repair](https://www.nature.com/subjects/targeted-gene-repair)

## Abstract

Serine integrases can precisely integrate large DNA constructs into desired chromosomal sites but only if their natural target site is first installed into the recipient genome. Here, to retarget serine integrases to a desired genomic site, we develop a modular integrase (MINT) system for genome editing. Through a combination of structural modeling, single-round directed evolution and screening in human cells, we retargeted the specificity of the serine integrase Bxb1. We demonstrate the therapeutic potential of the MINT system by retargeting Bxb1 to the human _AAVS1_ and _TRAC_ loci, where wild-type Bxb1 has no detectable activity. By combining MINT constructs with both known activity-increasing Bxb1 mutants and zinc-finger DNA-binding domains, we achieve efficiencies of 29% at the _AAVS1_ locus and 35% at the _TRAC_ locus in K562 cells. To further demonstrate clinical potential, we achieved 29% GFP integration efficiencies at the _TRAC_ locus in human T cells.

## Data availability

Amino acid sequences and DNA sequences of constructs used in this study are provided as [Supplementary Information](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03186-1#Sec45). Illumina sequencing data underlying all experiments were deposited to the National Center for Biotechnology Information Sequence Read Archive under BioProject [PRJNA1450726](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/bioproject/?term=PRJNA1450726) and have been publicly released. [Source data](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03186-1#Sec46) are provided with this paper.

## Code availability

All custom scripts are available from GitHub ( [https://github.com/sangamo-therapeutics/mint-sgmo-scripts](https://github.com/sangamo-therapeutics/mint-sgmo-scripts)).

title: Germinal: efficient de novo design of epitope-specific antibodies with nanomolar affinity
summary: Researchers at Stanford University and the Arc Institute (led by Luis S. Mille-Fragoso and Brian L. Hie) have introduced Germinal, a generative AI pipeline that designs functional antibodies against specific protein epitopes with nanomolar binding affinities. The method co-optimizes antibody structure and sequence by integrating an AlphaFold-Miller (AF-M) structure predictor with an antibody-specific protein language model (IgLM), performing de novo design of complementarity-determining regions onto user-specified structural frameworks. When tested against four diverse protein targets, Germinal produced functional binders across all targets and multiple binder formats, requiring only 43 to 101 computational designs per antigen for validation. The designed antibodies exhibited robust expression in mammalian cells, high sequence novelty, and structurally novel CDR loops as confirmed by cryo-EM mapping (PDB 35TL, EMD-77181 for a PDL1 scFv complex). Code is freely available on GitHub (github.com/SantiagoMille/germinal) with full computational and experimental protocols in the Supplementary Information.

## Abstract

Obtaining antibodies to specific protein targets is a widely important yet experimentally laborious process. Meanwhile, computational methods for antibody design have been limited by low success rates that require resource-intensive screening. Here we introduce Germinal, a broadly enabling generative pipeline that designs antibodies against specific epitopes with nanomolar binding affinities while requiring only low- _n_ experimental testing. Our method co-optimizes antibody structure and sequence by integrating a structure predictor with an antibody-specific protein language model to perform de novo design of functional complementarity-determining regions onto a user-specified structural framework. When tested against four diverse protein targets, Germinal designed functional antibodies across all targets and binder formats, testing only 43–101 designs for each antigen. Validated designs also exhibited robust expression in mammalian cells and high sequence and structural novelty. We provide open-source code and full computational and experimental protocols to facilitate wide adoption.

## Data availability

The cryo-EM map and atomic model of the PDL1 scFv H5 complex were deposited to the EM Data Bank under accession code EMD- [77181](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/pdbe/entry/emdb/EMD-77181) and the PDB under accession code [35TL](https://doi.org/10.2210/pdb35TL/pdb), respectively. We also uploaded raw data to Zenodo ( [https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20221721](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20221721))[90](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03187-0#ref-CR90 "Driscoll, C. et al. Raw BLI and SPR data. Zenodo                  https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20221721                                 (2026)."). All data, including the sequences of all tested designs and template plasmid maps used in this study, are available in the main text or its [Supplementary Information](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03187-0#Sec51).

## Code availability

Code for running the pipeline is available from GitHub ( [https://github.com/SantiagoMille/germinal](https://github.com/SantiagoMille/germinal)).

## Change history

- ### 01 July 2026





In the version of the article initially published, the equal contribution statement in the author information was correct, but the relevant footnote was missing from Luis S. Mille-Fragoso’s name in the author list. This has now been corrected in the HTML and PDF versions of the article.

### Contributions

L.S.M.-F., J.N.W., T.W., B.L.H. and X.J.G. conceptualized the study. L.S.M.-F., C.L.D., J.N.W., H.D. and X.Z. designed the experiments. B.L.H. and X.J.G. supervised the project. L.S.M.-F. and J.N.W. developed the algorithms and pipeline. L.S.M.-F., J.N.W. and T.W. contributed to the codebase and performed the computational analysis of designs. L.S.M.-F. and J.N.W. ran the design campaigns and selected the designs for experiments. L.S.M.-F., C.L.D., J.N.W. and H.D. cloned the plasmids used in this work. L.S.M.-F., C.L.D., H.D. and X.Z. ran the luciferase assays. L.S.M.-F., C.L.D. and H.D. performed the protein purification and ran the BLI validation and alanine mutagenesis experiments. L.S.M.-F. and C.L.D. performed the polyspecificity particle assays. L.S.M.-F. and C.L.D. performed the alanine mutagenesis experiments. H.D. performed the differential analysis on computational and experimental data. J.L.Z. and B.R. determined the cryo-EM structure. L.F. oversaw the cryo-EM structure determination. L.S.M.-F., J.N.W., C.L.D., T.W., B.L.H. and X.J.G. wrote the paper. L.S.M.-F. and C.L.D. agree to list themselves first in bibliographic entries.

title: DeepMind Spinout Raises $2.1 Billion
summary: London-based Isomorphic Labs, the DeepMind spinout focused on AI-driven drug discovery, secured $2.1 billion in a Series B funding round led by Thrive Capital with participation from Alphabet, marking one of the largest private biotech rounds in history. The capital will be used to scale up the IsoDDE (Isomorphic Labs Drug Design Engine), unveiled in February 2026, which reportedly surpasses AlphaFold 3 in predictive accuracy across predicting novel structures, estimating binding affinities, and identifying novel ligand pockets. On the Runs N’ Poses protein-ligand structure prediction benchmark, IsoDDE more than doubled AlphaFold 3's accuracy while also outperforming physics-based methods like FEP+ on antibody binding affinity benchmarks. Notably, IsoDDE accurately identified cryptic allosteric binding pockets for cereblon -- a clinically relevant target underlying immunomodulatory drugs such as thalidomide -- starting from sequence alone without a specified ligand. This round follows a $600 million raise the previous year and signals deep investor conviction in large-language-model approaches to structural biology as a path toward drug discovery.
analysis_notes: Article content is partially behind a paywall but the preview text and citation metadata provide sufficient detail to produce a substantive summary. The funding figure ($2.1B Series B) and IsoDDE benchmark results are stated in article copy, not inferred.

London-based Isomorphic Labs has secured $2.1 billion from investors — one of the largest private rounds in biotech history. This series B funding from a syndicate led by Thrive Capital, with participation from Alphabet, follows on from $600 million raised by Isomorphic last year. The company aims to scale up the Isomorphic Labs Drug Design Engine (IsoDDE) to accelerate AI -powered drug discovery.

The company unveiled IsoDDE in February, saying it had surpassed the predictive accuracy of its [2024 AlphaFold 3](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-024-07487-w) predecessor in three areas: predicting novel structures, estimating binding affinities and identifying novel ligand pockets. Tested on the [Runs N’ Poses](https://doi.org/10.1101/2025.02.03.636309), a challenging protein–ligand structure prediction benchmark, IsoDDE [more than doubled](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19699685) the accuracy of AlphaFold 3. The model also addresses another challenge faced by AI models: estimating how well an antibody will bind. Testing on three benchmarks — FEP+ 4, OpenFE and the CASP16 blind binding affinity prediction task — IsoDDE was able to outperform physics-based in silico methods such as FEP+. When tested on the human protein cereblon, the substrate receptor for an E3 ubiquitin ligase complex and the target for immunomodulatory dugs like thalidomide, IsoDDE could accurately identify new and cryptic allosteric binding pockets starting from an amino acid sequence and in the absence of a specified ligand.

title: Meatly to scale cultivated meat for pets
summary: British startup Meatly announced plans to build Europe's largest cultivated meat facility in London, a 20,000-liter pilot production site that will manufacture bioreactor-grown chicken meat for the pet food market. The project is funded by a £10.4 million (US $14 million) Series A raise also announced in May 2026. The move is notable because total cultivated meat sector investment has collapsed from a $1.4 billion peak in 2021 to just $74 million in 2025, according to data from the Washington-based Good Food Institute. Meatly's pivot toward pet food as an early commercial channel signals an industry adaptation strategy — targeting a less regulated, higher-margin market than human consumption amid investor fatigue. The announcement may help stabilize sentiment in a sector that has seen rapid funding contraction.

British startup Meatly has announced it will build what it says will be Europe’s largest cultivated meat factory in London. The 20,000-liter pilot facility will produce bioreactor-grown chicken meat for the pet food market.

The facility will be funded with a £10.4 million (US $14 million) series A investment round, also announced in May. The news stands out in a sector that has experienced waning investment over the last few years. Cultivated meat companies in 2025 raised just $74 million — a fraction of the $1.4 billion raised in 2021, when investment reached its peak, according to the Good Food Institute, a Washington, DC-based think tank.
